
### 4.3 Phytochemical Antioxidant Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PHY[Phytochemical Antioxidants] --> P1[Polyphenols]
    PHY --> P2[Carotenoids]
    PHY --> P3[Organosulfur Compounds]
    PHY --> P4[Vitamins]

    P1 --> P1A[Flavonoids Quercetin Catechin]
    P1 --> P1B[Phenolic Acids Caffeic Ferulic]
    P1 --> P1C[Lignans Stilbenes Resveratrol]

    P2 --> P2A[Beta Carotene provitamin A]
    P2 --> P2B[Lycopene]
    P2 --> P2C[Lutein Zeaxanthin]

    P3 --> P3A[Allicin from Garlic]
    P3 --> P3B[Sulforaphane from Broccoli]

    P4 --> P4A[Vitamin C Ascorbate]
    P4 --> P4B[Vitamin E Tocopherols]

    P1A -.Scavenges OH.-> ACT1[Radical Scavenging]
    P2C -.Quenches 1O2.-> ACT2[Singlet Oxygen Quenching]
    P1B -.Chelates Fe.-> ACT3[Metal Chelation]
    P4A -.Reduces Vit E radical.-> ACT4[Antioxidant Regeneration]

    style PHY fill:#16a085,color:#fff
    style ACT1 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style ACT2 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style ACT3 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style ACT4 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
*Define Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS). List any four biologically important ROS with one example of their damaging action.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** ROS are reactive oxygen-derived molecules, including free radicals and non-radicals, formed as by-products of aerobic metabolism, capable of oxidising cellular biomolecules.

**Four ROS (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Superoxide anion (O₂•⁻)** — damages iron-sulphur clusters in enzymes.
2. **Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** — oxidises thiol (-SH) groups of proteins.
3. **Hydroxyl radical (•OH)** — most reactive; attacks DNA bases causing strand breaks.
4. **Singlet oxygen (¹O₂)** — reacts with histidine, tryptophan, methionine; promotes lipid peroxidation.

**Damaging action example (0.5 marks):** •OH abstracts H from PUFAs initiating chain lipid peroxidation in foods.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
*State the "Antioxidant Hypothesis" and give two examples of exogenous dietary antioxidants.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hypothesis (1.5 marks):** The antioxidant hypothesis proposes that **dietary antioxidants** (especially plant-derived phytochemicals) **scavenge free radicals** and **augment endogenous antioxidant defences**, thereby reducing oxidative stress and lowering the risk of chronic diseases such as atherosclerosis, cancer, and ageing.

**Two exogenous dietary antioxidants (1 mark each):**
1. **Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C)** — water-soluble; regenerates Vitamin E radical; present in citrus, amla, guava.
2. **α-Tocopherol (Vitamin E)** — lipid-soluble; chain-breaking antioxidant in membranes; present in nuts, vegetable oils.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU Model Question Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **generation of ROS** through mitochondrial electron transport chain leakage, NADPH oxidase activity, and metal-catalysed (Fenton) reactions. Highlight the cascade of one-electron reductions of O₂. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanisms of action** of enzymatic antioxidants (SOD, CAT, GPx) with their stoichiometric reactions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Generation of ROS (7 marks):**

(i) **Mitochondrial ETC leakage (2 marks):** During oxidative phosphorylation, electrons flowing through Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase) and Complex III (bc₁ complex) prematurely react with O₂ at ~1–2% efficiency, generating **superoxide anion (O₂•⁻)**. Mn-SOD in the matrix and Cu/Zn-SOD in the intermembrane space dismutate O₂•⁻ to H₂O₂.

(ii) **NADPH oxidase (2 marks):** A membrane-bound enzyme complex (NOX family) deliberately generates O₂•⁻ by transferring one electron from cytosolic NADPH to extracellular O₂: **NADPH + 2O₂ → NADP⁺ + H⁺ + 2O₂•⁻**. Important in phagocytic respiratory burst and in plant defence against pathogens.

(iii) **Fenton reaction (2 marks):** Trace Fe²⁺ in foods/biology reacts with H₂O₂: **Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + •OH + OH⁻**. The •OH generated is the most damaging ROS, with E°′ = +2.31 V, attacking biomolecules within Ångström distance.

(iv) **Cascade of one-electron reductions (1 mark):**

$$\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{e^-} \text{O}_2^{\bullet -} \xrightarrow{e^-, 2H^+} \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{e^-, H^+} {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \xrightarrow{e^-, H^+} \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

**(b) Enzymatic Antioxidant Mechanisms (7 marks):**

(i) **Superoxide Dismutase (SOD) (2.5 marks):** Catalyses **dismutation** of superoxide:

$$2\,\text{O}_2^{\bullet -} + 2\text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{SOD}} \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

Three isoforms: Cu/Zn-SOD (cytosol), Mn-SOD (mitochondria), Fe-SOD (bacteria/plants). Rate constant ≈ 2 × 10⁹ M⁻¹s⁻¹ (diffusion-limited).

(ii) **Catalase (CAT) (2 marks):** Decomposes H₂O₂ without consuming reducing equivalents:

$$2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Catalase}} 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2$$

Haem-containing enzyme located in peroxisomes; very high turnover (~40 million molecules H₂O₂/sec).

(iii) **Glutathione Peroxidase (GPx) (2.5 marks):** Selenium-dependent enzyme; uses glutathione (GSH) as electron donor:

$$2\,\text{GSH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GPx}} \text{GSSG} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

GSSG is recycled to GSH by glutathione reductase using NADPH. Critical in detoxifying lipid hydroperoxides (LOOH) as well.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Describe the **antioxidant hypothesis** in detail. Discuss the classification of dietary (exogenous) antioxidants with suitable examples of phytochemicals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With relevant equations, explain the **DPPH and FRAP assays** for evaluating antioxidant capacity. Mention any two **limitations** of these in-vitro methods. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Antioxidant Hypothesis & Classification (7 marks):**

**Statement of Hypothesis (2 marks):** First formalised by Harman (1956) as the *Free Radical Theory of Aging* and expanded by Halliwell & Gutteridge, the antioxidant hypothesis states that **endogenous + exogenous antioxidants** maintain the redox homeostasis of the cell. A deficit leads to **oxidative stress**, damaging lipids, proteins, and DNA, which manifests as chronic disease. Phytochemicals (polyphenols, carotenoids, organosulfur compounds) supply the dietary antioxidant load.

**Classification (5 marks):**

| Class | Sub-class | Example | Food Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polyphenols | Flavonoids | Quercetin | Onion, apple |
| Polyphenols | Phenolic acids | Caffeic acid | Coffee |
| Polyphenols | Stilbenes | Resveratrol | Grape skin, red wine |
| Carotenoids | Xanthophyll | Lutein | Spinach, kale |
| Carotenoids | Carotene | β-Carotene | Carrot |
| Vitamins | Water-soluble | Ascorbic acid (Vit C) | Amla, citrus |
| Vitamins | Lipid-soluble | α-Tocopherol (Vit E) | Wheat germ, almond |
| Organosulfur | Isothiocyanate | Sulforaphane | Broccoli sprouts |
| Organosulfur | Sulfoxide | Allicin | Garlic |

---

**(b) DPPH and FRAP Assays (7 marks):**

**DPPH Assay (3 marks):**

$$\text{DPPH}^{\bullet}\ (\text{purple}) + \text{AH} \longrightarrow \text{DPPH-H}\ (\text{yellow}) + \text{A}^{\bullet}$$

- **Wavelength:** 517 nm; decrease in absorbance measured.
- **Reference standard:** Trolox (water-soluble vitamin E analogue).
- **Parameter reported:** IC₅₀ (µg/mL) or % scavenging.

**FRAP Assay (2 marks):**

$$\text{Fe}^{3+}\text{-TPTZ} + \text{AH} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}\text{-TPTZ}\ (\text{blue})$$

- **Wavelength:** 593 nm; increase in absorbance measured.
- **Standard:** FeSO₄·7H₂O; result expressed as µM Fe²⁺ equivalents.

**Limitations (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. Both assays are **non-physiological** — they do not reflect in-vivo ROS such as •OH at diffusion-limited rates.
2. They measure **only chemical reactivity**, not *bioavailability, tissue distribution, or metabolism* of the antioxidant in the human body.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Conflating ROS with free radicals** — H₂O₂ is ROS but NOT a free radical; students often lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong stoichiometry of SOD** — must be *2* superoxide + *2 H⁺*, not 1:1. Read the equation carefully.
> 3. **Confusing FRAP with DPPH wavelength** — FRAP is 593 nm (blue product), DPPH is 517 nm (purple → yellow). Easy to swap.
> 4. **Writing "antioxidants kill ROS"** — incorrect phrasing. Correct: "scavenge / neutralise / quench" ROS.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ in Fenton** — half-marks deducted for incomplete redox accounting.
> 6. **Listing only Vitamins C and E** — examiner expects *phytochemical examples* (quercetin, resveratrol, lycopene, curcumin).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

**Definitions to Memorise:**
- ROS — reactive oxygen derivatives with/without unpaired electrons.
- Antioxidant hypothesis — dietary antioxidants reduce oxidative stress and disease risk.
- Oxidative stress — imbalance between ROS and antioxidant defences.

**Key ROS (with damage targets):**
- O₂•⁻ (superoxide) → iron-sulphur enzyme damage
- H₂O₂ → -SH oxidation, membrane diffusion
- •OH (hydroxyl) → DNA strand breaks, PUFA peroxidation
- ¹O₂ (singlet oxygen) → carotenoid/tryptophan destruction

**Critical Equations (must reproduce from memory):**
- Fenton: Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + •OH + OH⁻
- Haber-Weiss (net): O₂•⁻ + H₂O₂ → •OH + OH⁻ + O₂
- SOD: 2O₂•⁻ + 2H⁺ → H₂O₂ + O₂
- Catalase: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂
- GPx: 2GSH + H₂O₂ → GSSG + 2H₂O

**Redox Potentials (ascending):** O₂/O₂•⁻ = −0.33 V; O₂•⁻/H₂O₂ = +0.94 V; H₂O₂/H₂O = +1.78 V; •OH/H₂O = **+2.31 V** (most reactive).

**Enzymatic Antioxidants (location & cofactor):**
- SOD: cytosol (Cu/Zn) / matrix (Mn) — dismutates O₂•⁻
- Catalase: peroxisome — haem — decomposes H₂O₂
- GPx: cytosol & mitochondria — Se (selenocysteine) — reduces H₂O₂ & LOOH

**Non-Enzymatic (Phytochemical) Antioxidants:**
- Lipid-soluble: α-tocopherol, β-carotene, lycopene, lutein, resveratrol
- Water-soluble: ascorbic acid, glutathione, quercetin, catechin, caffeic acid

**Antioxidant Defence Categories:**
- Preventive (CAT, GPx, chelators)
- Chain-breaking (Vit E, Vit C, flavonoids)
- Repair (phospholipases, DNA repair enzymes)

**Assay Key Data:**
- DPPH → 517 nm, purple→yellow, IC₅₀ reporting
- ABTS → 734 nm, Trolox standard
- FRAP → 593 nm, Fe²⁺ standard, blue complex
- ORAC → fluorescence AUC, Trolox standard

**Common Phytochemical Source Foods:**
- Quercetin — onion, apple, broccoli
- Resveratrol — red grapes, red wine
- Lycopene — tomato, watermelon
- β-Carotene — carrot, sweet potato
- Curcumin — turmeric
- Catechins — green tea
- Sulforaphane — broccoli sprouts
- Allicin — garlic

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** For any 7-mark sub-question on ROS/antioxidants, always structure the answer as (i) Definition → (ii) Generation mechanism → (iii) Equation → (iv) Damage/Defence role. This four-part framework is the KTU high-scoring template.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Antioxidant Activity Assays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Total Antioxidant Activity Assays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Total Antioxidant Activity (TAA) Assays** are a battery of *in vitro* spectrophotometric and fluorometric analytical procedures used to quantitatively estimate the cumulative reducing capacity, radical scavenging potential, and chain-breaking antioxidant power of phytochemical-rich food extracts, purified bioactive compounds, and nutraceutical formulations against multiple reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS).

The assays collectively measure the **hydrogen atom transfer (HAT)** and **single electron transfer (SET)** mechanisms by which phytochemicals (polyphenols, flavonoids, carotenoids, ascorbic acid, tocopherols, glutathione) neutralize free radicals, peroxyl radicals, and transition metal ions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight**
> *Total Antioxidant Activity* ≠ *Individual Antioxidant Quantification*. TAA gives a **cumulative, integrated value** (e.g., µmol TE/g, µmol Fe²⁺/g, mg AAE/g) of ALL antioxidant species present, including synergistic effects. This is fundamentally different from quantifying Vitamin C alone via HPLC.

### 1.2 Classification of Antioxidant Activity Assays

The two mechanistic families are:

| Mechanism | Mode of Action | Example Assays |
|---|---|---|
| **HAT (Hydrogen Atom Transfer)** | Antioxidant donates H• to a radical | ORAC, TRAP, β-carotene bleaching |
| **SET (Single Electron Transfer)** | Antioxidant transfers one e⁻ to a radical/oxidant | DPPH, ABTS, FRAP, CUPRAC, Phosphomolybdenum |

Some assays (e.g., **CUPRAC**, **Crocin Bleaching**) operate via a **mixed HAT/SET** hybrid mechanism.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bodyguard Squad" Intuition

Imagine your food extract is a **basket of bodyguards (antioxidants)** protecting a celebrity (a free radical that wants to cause oxidative damage to your cells).

- **DPPH/ABTS assays** = A standardized "attacker test." We throw a purple/blue free radical (DPPH•/ABTS•⁺) at the bodyguards and measure how fast the colour disappears (i.e., how many bodyguards neutralized the attacker).
- **FRAP/CUPRAC assays** = A "strength test." We ask each bodyguard: "How many bullets (electrons) can you defuse?" We tally up the total electron-donating ammunition.
- **ORAC assay** = A "marathon test." We measure how long the bodyguards can hold a defensive line against a continuous stream of attackers (peroxyl radicals) until they fall.
- **Phosphomolybdenum assay** = A "credit-score test" measuring total reducing equivalence, like a financial credit rating.
- **Metal chelating assay** = A "neutralizer test" — measuring how well the bodyguards tie up the weapons (pro-oxidant Fe²⁺/Cu⁺) before they can hurt anyone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept**: No single assay can fully describe antioxidant capacity. Therefore, KTU 2024 emphasises **multiple-assay reporting** — at minimum a SET-based (e.g., DPPH) and an HAT-based (e.g., ORAC) assay together with metal chelation data for a robust phytochemical profile.

### 1.4 Standardized Reporting Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **TEAC** — Trolox Equivalent Antioxidant Capacity (µmol TE/g extract)
- **VCEAC** — Vitamin C Equivalent Antioxidant Capacity (mg AAE/g)
- **GAE** — Gallic Acid Equivalent (often used for polyphenols but extrapolated to TAA)
- **IC₅₀** — Concentration of extract that scavenges **50%** of the test radical (lower IC₅₀ → stronger antioxidant)
- **AEDC** — Antioxidant Equivalent to Ascorbic Acid Dose-response Curve

### 1.5 Standard Reactive Species Used in Assays

$$\text{DPPH}^{\bullet} \quad \text{ABTS}^{\bullet+} \quad \text{ROO}^{\bullet} \text{ (peroxyl)} \quad \text{OH}^{\bullet} \text{ (hydroxyl)} \quad \text{O}_2^{\bullet-} \text{ (superoxide)} \quad \text{NO}^{\bullet} \text{ (nitric oxide)}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle-by-Principle Breakdown

#### A. DPPH (2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) Radical Scavenging Assay

**Chemistry**: DPPH is a stable, commercially available, **deep violet-coloured** nitrogen-centred radical with an unpaired electron. In methanol/ethanol solution, it absorbs strongly at **λ_max = 517 nm**.

When an antioxidant (AH) donates a hydrogen atom or electron:

$$\text{DPPH}^{\bullet} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{DPPH-H} + \text{A}^{\bullet}$$

The violet colour bleaches to **pale yellow** (DPPH-H, the hydrazine form), and absorbance at 517 nm decreases.

**Operational Steps (Bulleted Logic)**:

1. Prepare a **0.1 mM DPPH• solution** in **anhydrous methanol** (absorbance ≈ 0.95–1.00 at 517 nm).
2. Add **1.0–3.0 mL** of DPPH• to **1 mL** of varying extract concentrations (typically 20–200 µg/mL).
3. Vortex, incubate in the dark at **room temperature for 30 min** (some protocols use 15 min or 60 min).
4. Measure absorbance at **517 nm** against methanol blank.
5. Run a **control** (DPPH + methanol) and **standard** (Trolox or ascorbic acid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why methanol, not water?** DPPH• is poorly soluble in water. Aqueous extracts must be diluted in methanol. Ethanol may be used as an alternative but methanol gives sharper kinetic profiles. Some labs use DMSO for highly lipophilic phytochemicals.

#### B. ABTS / TEAC (Trolox Equivalent Antioxidant Capacity) Assay

**Chemistry**: ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) is oxidized (typically by **potassium persulfate K₂S₂O₈** at 1:0.5 molar ratio, 12–16 h dark incubation) to form the **blue-green ABTS radical cation (ABTS•⁺)**.

$$\text{ABTS} + \text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8 \rightarrow \text{ABTS}^{\bullet+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{K}^+$$

ABTS•⁺ shows absorption maxima at **λ = 414, 645, 734, and 815 nm**. The most common measurement wavelength is **734 nm** to avoid interference from colored pigments in food extracts.

Scavenging reduces ABTS•⁺ back to colorless ABTS.

> [!TIP]
> **DPPH vs ABTS — When to use which?**
> - DPPH: lipophilic antioxidants, simple, fast, single wavelength
> - ABTS: works for **both lipophilic and hydrophilic** antioxidants; broader applicability; more sensitive

#### C. FRAP (Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power) Assay

**Chemistry**: At low pH (**pH 3.6**, acetate buffer), Fe³⁺-TPTZ (2,4,6-tripyridyl-s-triazine) complex is reduced by antioxidants to **Fe²⁺-TPTZ**, forming an intense **blue-violet product** with λ_max = **593 nm**.

$$\text{Fe}^{3+}\text{-TPTZ} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}\text{-TPTZ} + \text{A}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^+$$

FRAP is a **pure SET assay**; it does **not** involve radical scavenging in the classical sense — it measures *electron-donating reducing power*.

Standard reference: **FeSO₄·7H₂O** (calibration curve in 100–1000 µM).

#### D. ORAC (Oxygen Radical Absorbance Capacity) Assay

**Chemistry**: A **fluorescence-based HAT assay** using:
- **Fluorescein** (or β-phycoerythrin in older protocols) as the fluorescent probe
- **AAPH (2,2′-azobis(2-amidinopropane) dihydrochloride)** as the peroxyl radical generator
- **Trolox** as the standard

AAPH thermally decomposes (37 °C) to generate ROO•, which oxidizes fluorescein → loss of fluorescence (excitation 485 nm, emission 520 nm). Antioxidants delay this decay.

Result expressed as **µmol Trolox Equivalent (TE)/g** via the **Net AUC** integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is ORAC considered the "gold standard" by the USDA?** It integrates both the **extent** and the **time** of antioxidant protection (Area Under the Curve), simulating physiological, time-resolved radical flux.

#### E. CUPRAC (Cupric Reducing Antioxidant Capacity) Assay

$$\text{Cu}(\text{Nc})_2^{2+} + n\text{e}^{-} + n\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Cu}(\text{Nc})_2^{+} + n\text{H}^+$$

Uses **bis(neocuproine)copper(II) chloride (Cu(Nc)₂²⁺)** at **pH 7** (physiological pH). Forms yellow-orange Cu(Nc)₂⁺ complex measured at **450 nm**. Advantage: works at physiological pH (unlike FRAP at pH 3.6), and detects both hydrophilic and lipophilic antioxidants without pH adjustment.

#### F. Phosphomolybdenum Method (Prieto et al., 1999)

$$\text{Mo}(\text{VI}) + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{Mo}(\text{V}) + \text{A}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^+$$

The reduced Mo(V) forms a green **phosphate-Mo(V) complex** measured at **λ = 695 nm**. Performed in acidic medium (H₂SO₄), heated at **95 °C for 90 min**. Standard: **ascorbic acid (mg AAE/g)**.

#### G. Metal Chelating Activity (Ferrozine assay)

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{Ferrozine} \rightarrow \text{violet complex (}\lambda_{max}=562\text{ nm)}$$

Antioxidants (chelators) sequester Fe²⁺, decreasing violet colour. This is a **secondary antioxidant mechanism** — it prevents **Fenton reaction** (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + •OH + OH⁻).

#### H. Nitric Oxide (NO) Scavenging Assay

Griess reagent (**sulphanilamide + NEDD**) reacts with NO₂⁻ (formed from NO + O₂) to form a pink azo dye at **λ = 546 nm**. Antioxidants that scavenge NO reduce azo dye formation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they account for ≥ 60% of numerical KTU 2024 questions on this topic.**

| # | Assay | Core Equation | Standard Reference | Wavelength (nm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DPPH** | $\%\text{ Scavenging} = \dfrac{A_{\text{control}} - A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{control}}} \times 100$ | Trolox / Ascorbic acid | 517 |
| 2 | **ABTS** | $\%\text{ Scavenging} = \dfrac{A_{\text{control}} - A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{control}}} \times 100$ | Trolox (TEAC) | 734 |
| 3 | **FRAP** | $\text{FRAP value} = \dfrac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{std}}} \times C_{\text{std}} \times D$ | FeSO₄·7H₂O (µM Fe²⁺/g) | 593 |
| 4 | **ORAC** | $\text{ORAC} = \dfrac{\text{AUC}_{\text{sample}} - \text{AUC}_{\text{blank}}}{\text{AUC}_{\text{Trolox}} - \text{AUC}_{\text{blank}}} \times \left[\text{Trolox}\right]$ | Trolox (µmol TE/g) | Fluor (485/520) |
| 5 | **CUPRAC** | $A_{450} = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ (Beer's Law) | Trolox | 450 |
| 6 | **Phosphomolybdenum** | $\text{TAA} = \dfrac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{std}}} \times C_{\text{std}} \times V \times D / m$ | Ascorbic acid (mg AAE/g) | 695 |
| 7 | **Metal chelating** | $\%\text{ Chelation} = \dfrac{A_{\text{control}} - A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{control}}} \times 100$ | EDTA / Citric acid | 562 |
| 8 | **NO scavenging** | $\%\text{ NO scav.} = \dfrac{A_{\text{control}} - A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{control}}} \times 100$ | Curcumin / Ascorbate | 546 |
| 9 | **IC₅₀** | Linear regression: $\%\text{ Scav.} = m \cdot \log[C] + c$ → solve for 50 % | — | — |
| 10 | **Beer's Law (universal)** | $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ | — | — |

**Where:**
- $A$ = absorbance; $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹); $b$ = path length (cm); $c$ = concentration (mol/L)
- $D$ = dilution factor; $V$ = volume; $m$ = mass of extract
- AUC for ORAC: $\text{AUC} = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{f_i}{f_0} + \dfrac{f_n}{2 f_0}$ where $f_i$ = fluorescence at time $i$

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering and Nutrition

- **Nutraceutical Industry**: ORAC database (USDA, retired 2012 but still cited) used for "superfood" marketing; current EFSA/FDA disallow such claims, requiring **multiple assays**.
- **Shelf-life prediction**: TAA correlates with lipid oxidation retardation in edible oils, meat products, and emulsion systems.
- **Food Processing Optimization**: Comparing TAA before/after pasteurization, freeze-drying, fermentation, or high-pressure processing validates "minimal processing" claims.
- **Cosmetic and Functional Beverage Formulations**: TAA guides ingredient selection for anti-ageing cosmeceuticals and antioxidant-rich juices.
- **Quality Assurance**: Batch-to-batch TAA monitoring using **DPPH + FRAP** dual-assay platform is industry standard for botanical extracts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: IC₅₀ Determination from DPPH Dose-Response

**Given:**
- Extract concentrations: 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 µg/mL
- Corresponding % scavenging: 18, 34, 48, 62, 74 %
- Control absorbance $A_c$ = 0.952; Sample absorbance $A_s$ at 100 µg/mL = 0.247

**Step 1 — Confirm % scavenging at highest concentration:**

$$\%\text{ Scav} = \frac{0.952 - 0.247}{0.952} \times 100 = \frac{0.705}{0.952} \times 100 = 74.05\%$$

**Step 2 — Linear Regression of $\log[C]$ vs % Scavenging**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log[20] &= 1.3010 \\
\log[40] &= 1.6021 \\
\log[60] &= 1.7782 \\
\log[80] &= 1.9031 \\
\log[100] &= 2.0000
\end{aligned}$$

Using least squares method (n = 5):

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{1.3010 + 1.6021 + 1.7782 + 1.9031 + 2.0000}{5} = 1.7169 \\
\bar{y} &= \frac{18 + 34 + 48 + 62 + 74}{5} = 47.2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = (-0.4159)(-29.2) + (-0.1148)(-13.2) + (0.0613)(0.8) + (0.1862)(14.8) + (0.2831)(26.8)$$

$$= 12.144 + 1.515 + 0.049 + 2.756 + 7.587 = 24.051$$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 0.1730 + 0.0132 + 0.0038 + 0.0347 + 0.0802 = 0.3049$$

$$\text{Slope } (m) = \frac{24.051}{0.3049} = 78.88$$

$$\text{Intercept } (c) = \bar{y} - m\bar{x} = 47.2 - (78.88 \times 1.7169) = 47.2 - 135.43 = -88.23$$

**Step 3 — Equation of the Line**

$$y = 78.88 \cdot \log[C] - 88.23$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $y = 50$ (i.e., IC₅₀)**

$$50 = 78.88 \cdot \log[\text{IC}_{50}] - 88.23$$

$$\log[\text{IC}_{50}] = \frac{50 + 88.23}{78.88} = \frac{138.23}{78.88} = 1.7524$$

$$\boxed{\text{IC}_{50} = 10^{1.7524} = 56.56 \; \mu\text{g/mL}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: FRAP Value Calculation with Dilution Factor

**Given:**
- $A_{\text{sample}} = 0.482$ at 593 nm
- FeSO₄ standard calibration: slope = 0.0011 AU/µM, $r^2 = 0.999$
- $D$ (dilution factor) = 10; final extract mass $m = 0.5$ g in 25 mL
- Control $A_{\text{blank}} = 0.051$

**Step 1 — Blank-correct the absorbance**

$$A_{\text{corrected}} = 0.482 - 0.051 = 0.431$$

**Step 2 — Convert absorbance to Fe²⁺ equivalents**

$$[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_{\text{sol}} = \frac{0.431}{0.0011} = 391.82 \; \mu\text{M}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Dilution Factor to Obtain Extract Concentration**

$$[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_{\text{extract}} = 391.82 \times 10 = 3918.2 \; \mu\text{M}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to µmol Fe²⁺/g dry extract**

$$\text{FRAP value} = \frac{3918.2 \times 25 \text{ mL}}{1000 \times 0.5 \text{ g}} = \frac{97.955}{0.5} = 195.91 \; \mu\text{mol Fe}^{2+}/\text{g}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: ORAC Net AUC Calculation

**Given:**
- Fluorescence readings (every 2 min, 0–60 min) for sample (conc. 50 µg/mL) and Trolox (10 µM)
- Blank (no antioxidant): $f_0 = 100, f_{12}= 95, f_{24} = 78, f_{36} = 55, f_{48} = 32, f_{60} = 15$
- Sample: $f_0 = 100, f_{12} = 98, f_{24} = 94, f_{36} = 86, f_{48} = 75, f_{60} = 62$
- Trolox: $f_0 = 100, f_{12} = 96, f_{24} = 90, f_{36} = 80, f_{48} = 65, f_{60} = 50$

**Step 1 — Compute AUC (relative units, normalized to $f_0$)**

For each curve, normalized fluorescence $R_i = f_i / f_0$. With time interval $\Delta t = 2$ min and using trapezoidal rule:

$$\text{AUC} = \Delta t \left[ \frac{R_0 + R_n}{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i \right]$$

(blank) $\text{AUC} = 2 \left[ \frac{1 + 0.15}{2} + (0.95 + 0.78 + 0.55 + 0.32) \right] = 2 \left[ 0.575 + 2.60 \right] = 6.35$

(sample) $\text{AUC} = 2 \left[ \frac{1 + 0.62}{2} + (0.98 + 0.94 + 0.86 + 0.75) \right] = 2 \left[ 0.81 + 3.53 \right] = 8.68$

(Trolox) $\text{AUC} = 2 \left[ \frac{1 + 0.50}{2} + (0.96 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.65) \right] = 2 \left[ 0.75 + 3.31 \right] = 8.12$

**Step 2 — Compute Net AUC**

$$\text{Net AUC}_{\text{sample}} = 8.68 - 6.35 = 2.33$$

$$\text{Net AUC}_{\text{Trolox}} = 8.12 - 6.35 = 1.77$$

**Step 3 — ORAC value (per the formula)**

$$\text{ORAC} = \frac{2.33}{1.77} \times 10 \; \mu\text{M} \;(\text{Trolox}) = 13.16 \; \mu\text{mol TE/g}$$

(where 10 µM is the Trolox concentration and a divisor of extract concentration converts to per gram basis).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | DPPH | ABTS | FRAP | ORAC | CUPRAC | Phosphomolybdenum |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | SET+HAT | SET+HAT | SET | HAT | SET | SET |
| Rad./Probe | DPPH• (N-centred) | ABTS•⁺ (N-centred) | Fe³⁺-TPTZ | ROO• (AAPH) | Cu²⁺-Nc | Mo(VI) |
| pH | Neutral (MeOH) | Neutral | 3.6 (acidic) | 7.4 (physiol.) | 7.0 | Acidic (H₂SO₄) |
| λ (nm) | 517 | 734 | 593 | Fluo. 485/520 | 450 | 695 |
| Time | 30 min | 6 min | 4–30 min | 35–120 min | 30 min | 90 min @ 95°C |
| Std. ref. | Trolox / AA | Trolox | FeSO₄·7H₂O | Trolox | Trolox | Ascorbic acid |
| H₂O sol.? | Partial | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Lipophilic? | Yes | Yes | Limited | Yes | Yes | Limited |
| Sensitivity | High | Very High | High | Very High | High | Moderate |
| Drawback | Steric hindrance | Pre-formed radical | pH-dependent | Fluorescence quench | None major | Heating needed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Selecting an Antioxidant Assay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Food Extract with Phytochemicals"] --> B{"Target Analyte Class?"}
    B -- "Polyphenols / Flavonoids" --> C{"Need HAT or SET?"}
    B -- "Carotenoids / Tocopherols" --> D["DPPH Assay"]
    B -- "Ascorbic Acid dominant" --> E["FRAP or Phosphomolybdenum"]
    B -- "Multiple classes, mixed" --> F["ABTS plus CUPRAC"]
    C -- "HAT time-integrated" --> G["ORAC Assay"]
    C -- "SET fast screening" --> H["DPPH or FRAP"]
    D --> I["Read at 517 nm"]
    E --> J["Read at 593 or 695 nm"]
    F --> K["Read at 734 and 450 nm"]
    G --> L["Read fluorescence 485 / 520 nm"]
    H --> M["T-test vs Trolox curve"]
    I --> N["Report IC50 in microgram per mL"]
    J --> O["Report micromole Fe two plus per g"]
    K --> P["Report micromole TE per g"]
    L --> Q["Report Net AUC and micromole TE per g"]
    M --> R["Final TAA profile report"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — DPPH Assay Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Chem as Chemist
    participant Vial as Reaction Vial
    participant Spec as UV-Vis Spectrophotometer
    participant Data as Data Logger
    Chem->>Vial: Add 1.0 mL extract 20 to 200 microgram per mL
    Chem->>Vial: Add 3.0 mL of 0.1 mM DPPH in methanol
    Chem->>Vial: Vortex 10 seconds
    Chem->>Vial: Stand 30 min in dark room temp
    Chem->>Spec: Transfer to cuvette
    Spec->>Spec: Blank with methanol at 517 nm
    Spec->>Data: Read absorbance Asample and Acontrol
    Data->>Chem: Compute percent scavenging
    Data->>Chem: Plot log concentration versus percent
    Chem->>Chem: Linear regression for IC50
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Total Antioxidant Activity Assay Platform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT ["Sample Preparation Block"]
        S1["Dried Plant Material"]
        S2["Solvent Extraction 70 percent methanol"]
        S3["Filtration and Rotary Evaporation"]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end
    subgraph SET ["SET Mechanism Assays"]
        T1["DPPH at 517 nm"]
        T2["ABTS at 734 nm"]
        T3["FRAP at 593 nm"]
        T4["CUPRAC at 450 nm"]
        T5["Phosphomolybdenum at 695 nm"]
    end
    subgraph HAT ["HAT Mechanism Assays"]
        T6["ORAC Fluorometric"]
        T7["Beta Carotene Bleaching"]
        T8["Crocin Bleaching"]
    end
    subgraph AUX ["Auxiliary Antioxidant Tests"]
        T9["Metal Chelating Ferrozine"]
        T10["Nitric Oxide Scavenging"]
        T11["Hydroxyl Radical Scavenging"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT ["Data Integration Block"]
        O1["Trolox Calibration"]
        O2["IC50 determination"]
        O3["TEAC and FRAP values"]
        O4["Statistical ANOVA and Pearson correlation"]
        O5["Final TAA Report micro mol TE per g"]
    end
    S3 --> SET
    S3 --> HAT
    S3 --> AUX
    SET --> O1
    HAT --> O1
    AUX --> O1
    O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4 --> O5
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — IC₅₀ Determination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Prepare extract dilution series 20 to 200 microgram per mL"] --> P2["React each with DPPH or ABTS"]
    P2 --> P3["Measure absorbance at 517 or 734 nm"]
    P3 --> P4["Compute percent scavenging for each concentration"]
    P4 --> P5["Plot percent scavenging on Y axis"]
    P4 --> P6["Plot log concentration on X axis"]
    P5 --> P7["Perform linear regression"]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8["Obtain slope m and intercept c"]
    P8 --> P9["Solve y equals 50 for x"]
    P9 --> P10["Report IC50 in microgram per mL"]
    P10 --> P11{"Acceptable Range less than 100?"}
    P11 -- Yes --> P12["High antioxidant potential"]
    P11 -- No --> P13["Moderate to low activity"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Differentiate between HAT and SET mechanisms of antioxidant action. Give one example assay for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**HAT (Hydrogen Atom Transfer)** involves transfer of a hydrogen atom from the antioxidant (AH) to the radical (R•), neutralizing it:

$$\text{AH} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{A}^{\bullet} + \text{RH}$$

It is **kinetically fast** (ms–s), **pH-independent**, and reflects the bond dissociation energy (BDE) of the O–H bond. **Example: ORAC assay.**

**SET (Single Electron Transfer)** involves transfer of a single electron, reducing the oxidant:

$$\text{AH} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{AH}^{\bullet+} + \text{R}^{-}$$

It is **pH-dependent** (favoured at high pH), **slower** (s–min), and governed by ionization potential. **Example: FRAP assay.**

(3 marks: 1 for HAT definition + reaction, 1 for SET + reaction, 1 for examples)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Why is FRAP performed at pH 3.6 and not at physiological pH 7.4?**

**Model Answer:**

FRAP uses the Fe³⁺-TPTZ complex as the oxidant probe. At pH 3.6:
1. **Fe³⁺ remains soluble** (avoids precipitation as Fe(OH)₃ that occurs above pH 4).
2. The **driving force** for the redox reaction is maximized because proton-coupled electron transfer is favoured at low pH (Nernstian shift).
3. **Selectivity for strong reductants** — only compounds with low enough reduction potential can reduce Fe³⁺ at this pH, making FRAP specific for "fast" reductants like ascorbic acid.
4. Physiological pH 7.4 would cause Fe(OH)₃ precipitation, interfering with absorbance measurement at 593 nm. CUPRAC (which uses Cu²⁺-bis(neocuproine)) is the alternative when physiological pH is needed.

(3 marks: 1 for solubility, 1 for selectivity, 1 for comparison with CUPRAC)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Describe the principle, procedure, and mechanism of the **DPPH radical scavenging assay** for the determination of total antioxidant activity of a plant extract. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) is a stable, commercially available, nitrogen-centred free radical with a deep violet colour (λ_max = 517 nm in methanol). When an antioxidant (AH) donates a hydrogen atom or electron, DPPH• is reduced to its non-radical hydrazine form DPPH-H, which is pale yellow. The decrease in absorbance at 517 nm is directly proportional to the radical scavenging capacity of the antioxidant.

$$\text{DPPH}^{\bullet} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{DPPH-H} + \text{A}^{\bullet}$$

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Preparation of DPPH solution:** Dissolve 3.94 mg DPPH in 100 mL anhydrous methanol to obtain 0.1 mM stock; store in an amber bottle.
2. **Sample preparation:** Dissolve dried plant extract in methanol to prepare concentrations of 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 µg/mL.
3. **Reaction:** Mix 1.0 mL of each extract concentration with 3.0 mL DPPH solution. Vortex for 10 s.
4. **Incubation:** Keep in the dark at room temperature for 30 min.
5. **Measurement:** Read absorbance at 517 nm against a methanol blank.
6. **Control:** DPPH + methanol (no extract); Standard: Trolox (10–100 µM) or ascorbic acid.
7. **Calculation:** % scavenging = [(A_c − A_s)/A_c] × 100; plot % vs log concentration; determine IC₅₀ via linear regression.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
The reaction is a **mixed HAT/SET** process. The antioxidant's phenolic –OH donates H• (HAT) while simultaneously reducing the radical via single electron transfer (SET) from phenoxide anions. Steric factors (ortho-substitution) and electronic factors (electron-donating groups) affect the rate. Carotenoids react via SET primarily, while polyhydroxy flavonoids react via HAT.

---

**b)** An extract of *Curcuma longa* (turmeric) rhizome was tested for DPPH scavenging. Results:

| Concentration (µg/mL) | Absorbance at 517 nm |
|---|---|
| Control | 0.965 |
| 20 | 0.821 |
| 40 | 0.692 |
| 60 | 0.541 |
| 80 | 0.402 |
| 100 | 0.288 |

**Calculate IC₅₀ and express in µg/mL. Comment on its antioxidant potency compared with IC₅₀ of pure curcumin reported as 18 µg/mL.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute % scavenging (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{20} &: \frac{0.965 - 0.821}{0.965} \times 100 = 14.92\% \\
C_{40} &: \frac{0.965 - 0.692}{0.965} \times 100 = 28.29\% \\
C_{60} &: \frac{0.965 - 0.541}{0.965} \times 100 = 43.94\% \\
C_{80} &: \frac{0.965 - 0.402}{0.965} \times 100 = 58.34\% \\
C_{100} &: \frac{0.965 - 0.288}{0.965} \times 100 = 70.16\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Linear Regression of log[C] vs % scavenging (3 marks):**

| log[C] | % Scavenging |
|---|---|
| 1.3010 | 14.92 |
| 1.6021 | 28.29 |
| 1.7782 | 43.94 |
| 1.9031 | 58.34 |
| 2.0000 | 70.16 |

$$\bar{x} = 1.7169, \quad \bar{y} = 43.13$$

$$\sum(x-\bar{x})(y-\bar{y}) = 25.78, \quad \sum(x-\bar{x})^2 = 0.3049$$

$$m = \frac{25.78}{0.3049} = 84.55, \quad c = 43.13 - 84.55(1.7169) = -102.04$$

**Step 3 — Solve for IC₅₀ (1 mark):**

$$50 = 84.55 \log[\text{IC}_{50}] - 102.04$$

$$\log[\text{IC}_{50}] = \frac{152.04}{84.55} = 1.7982 \implies \text{IC}_{50} = 62.84 \; \mu\text{g/mL}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on potency (1 mark):**

Since the **whole rhizome extract (62.84 µg/mL)** has a higher IC₅₀ than pure curcumin (18 µg/mL), it implies that:
- Curcumin is the dominant but not sole antioxidant in turmeric
- Other curcuminoids (demethoxycurcumin, bisdemethoxycurcumin) and volatile terpenoids contribute synergistically
- A 62.84 / 18 = **3.49-fold purification factor** would be needed to obtain pure curcumin-equivalent activity
- The extract still qualifies as a **potent natural antioxidant** (IC₅₀ < 100 µg/mL is generally considered strong)

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Discuss the principle and procedure of the **FRAP (Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power) assay**. How does it differ mechanistically from DPPH? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
FRAP measures the ability of an antioxidant to reduce the ferric tripyridyltriazine complex [Fe³⁺-(TPTZ)₂]³⁺ to the ferrous form [Fe²⁺-(TPTZ)₂]²⁺ at low pH (3.6, acetate buffer). The Fe²⁺ complex is intensely blue-violet with maximum absorbance at **593 nm**. The increase in absorbance is directly proportional to the electron-donating (reducing) capacity of the sample.

$$\text{Fe}^{3+}\text{-TPTZ} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}\text{-TPTZ} + \text{A}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^+$$

Standard: FeSO₄·7H₂O (100–1000 µM).

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **FRAP reagent** = 300 mM acetate buffer (pH 3.6) + 10 mM TPTZ in 40 mM HCl + 20 mM FeCl₃·6H₂O in 10:1:1 ratio, prepared fresh.
2. Mix 3.0 mL FRAP reagent + 0.1 mL sample extract (or standard).
3. Incubate at 37 °C for 4 min (or room temp 30 min — varies by protocol).
4. Read absorbance at 593 nm.
5. Express result as **µmol Fe²⁺/g** extract or as **mg AAE/g** if ascorbate is used.

**Mechanistic Difference from DPPH (2 marks):**

| Feature | DPPH | FRAP |
|---|---|---|
| Radical-based? | **Yes** (N-centred) | **No** (uses Fe³⁺ as oxidant) |
| Mechanism | HAT + SET | **Pure SET** |
| Measures | Radical scavenging | Reducing power |
| Reaction time | 30 min | 4–30 min |
| Color change | Violet → Yellow | Colorless → Blue-violet |
| What it can't detect | Compounds that reduce but don't scavenge radicals | Compounds that scavenge radicals but can't reduce Fe³⁺ |

DPPH detects **radical scavengers** (e.g., carotenoids that quench radicals but are poor Fe³⁺ reducers). FRAP detects **reductants** (e.g., ascorbic acid, uric acid) which may be poor DPPH scavengers due to steric hindrance.

---

**b)** The following data were obtained for a grape seed extract using the FRAP assay. Compute the FRAP value in µmol Fe²⁺/g dry extract. **(7 marks)**

| Standard Fe²⁺ (µM) | Absorbance at 593 nm |
|---|---|
| 100 | 0.105 |
| 250 | 0.265 |
| 500 | 0.530 |
| 750 | 0.795 |
| 1000 | 1.060 |

Sample absorbance = **0.612** at 100 µg/mL extract concentration; dilution factor = 5; final extract mass = 0.2 g made up to 50 mL.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calibration equation (2 marks):**
Linear regression of [Fe²⁺] vs A:

Slope $m = \frac{\Delta A}{\Delta[\text{Fe}^{2+}]} = \frac{1.060 - 0.105}{1000 - 100} = \frac{0.955}{900} = 0.001061 \; \text{AU/µM}$

Intercept $\approx 0$ (R² ≈ 0.999, passes through origin): $A = 0.001061 \cdot [\text{Fe}^{2+}]$

**Step 2 — Convert sample absorbance to [Fe²⁺] in cuvette (2 marks):**

$$[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_{\text{reaction}} = \frac{0.612}{0.001061} = 576.81 \; \mu\text{M}$$

**Step 3 — Apply dilution factor to obtain [Fe²⁺] in stock extract (1 mark):**

$$[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_{\text{extract}} = 576.81 \times 5 = 2884.07 \; \mu\text{M}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to µmol Fe²⁺ per gram dry extract (2 marks):**

$$\text{FRAP value} = \frac{2884.07 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{L}}{0.2 \; \text{g}} = \frac{144.20}{0.2} = 721.02 \; \mu\text{mol Fe}^{2+}/\text{g}$$

**Comment:** Grape seed extract exhibits a **high FRAP value (>500 µmol Fe²⁺/g)**, consistent with its rich proanthocyanidin content (catechin, epicatechin, gallic acid esters). This confirms strong reducing antioxidant capacity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Units mismatch** — Always state µmol TE/g or µmol Fe²⁺/g explicitly. Lose 1 mark for missing units.
> 2. **Forgetting blank correction** — Subtract A_blank before calculations in DPPH/FRAP.
> 3. **Mixing wavelengths** — DPPH = 517 nm ≠ ABTS = 734 nm ≠ FRAP = 593 nm.
> 4. **Using linear concentration instead of log[C]** — IC₅₀ requires log-transformed X-axis for sigmoidal radical scavenging curves.
> 5. **Reporting TEAC without specifying Trolox range** — Calibration should be 10–100 µM Trolox minimum.
> 6. **Confusing TEAC with TAA** — TEAC is specifically for ABTS/ABTS•⁺ assay, not DPPH.
> 7. **Forgetting dilution factor** in FRAP — Most common single numerical error.
> 8. **Writing "antioxidant" without specifying HAT vs SET** — Each assay has a defined mechanism.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Check:** Total Antioxidant Activity = **cumulative** measure of all antioxidant species via standardized assays; not equal to quantification of individual compounds.
- **Two Mechanistic Families:** **HAT** (time-integrated, pH-independent, e.g., ORAC) vs **SET** (pH-dependent, e.g., DPPH, FRAP, ABTS, CUPRAC, Phosphomolybdenum).
- **DPPH:** Violet → Yellow at **517 nm**; 30 min dark incubation; mixed HAT/SET; standard = Trolox or ascorbate.
- **ABTS:** Blue-green → Colorless at **734 nm**; pre-formed radical with K₂S₂O₈; broad applicability (hydro + lipophilic); standard = Trolox.
- **FRAP:** Colorless → Blue at **593 nm**; pH **3.6**; pure SET; FeSO₄ standard; reports µmol Fe²⁺/g.
- **ORAC:** Fluorescence decay at **485/520 nm**; uses **AAPH + fluorescein**; **Net AUC** integrated; gold standard for biological relevance.
- **CUPRAC:** Yellow-orange at **450 nm**; physiological **pH 7**; Cu²⁺-bis(neocuproine); HAT + SET hybrid.
- **Phosphomolybdenum:** Green complex at **695 nm**; Mo(VI) → Mo(V); 95 °C/90 min; mg AAE/g.
- **Metal Chelating:** Ferrozine at **562 nm**; detects Fe²⁺ sequestration; secondary antioxidant mechanism.
- **Reporting Standards:** Always include **units**, **standard reference**, **wavelength**, **pH**, and **incubation time**.
- **Universal Equation:** $\%\text{Activity} = \dfrac{A_{\text{control}} - A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{control}}} \times 100$
- **Beer's Law:** $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ — backbone of all UV-Vis-based assays.
- **Best Practice:** Use **at least two assays** (one HAT + one SET) for credible antioxidant profiling.
- **Lower IC₅₀** = **stronger** antioxidant activity; report with confidence interval.
- **Synergy:** Whole extracts often show **higher TAA than isolated compounds** due to synergistic phytochemical interactions.
- **Interference:** Always check for **non-antioxidant UV absorbers** (e.g., chlorophyll, carotenoids) using appropriate blanks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods to Study Phytochemicals and Health Promotion: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods to Study Phytochemicals and Health Promotion: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phytochemicals** (from the Greek word *phyton* meaning *plant*) are biologically active, non-nutritive chemical compounds produced by plants that function as natural defense molecules against pathogens, herbivores, and environmental stress. They are distinct from the classical macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) and micronutrients (vitamins, minerals) and are primarily classified into major groups: **polyphenols, carotenoids, alkaloids, terpenoids, organosulfur compounds, and phytosterols**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** Phytochemicals are secondary metabolites of plant origin possessing diverse biological activities. They are studied using **extraction, isolation, purification, characterization, and bioactivity evaluation** techniques, and are linked to **health promotion** through antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and chemopreventive mechanisms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plant Pharmacy"

Imagine a plant as a **miniature pharmaceutical factory** that has been running for over 400 million years of evolution. Just as a human pharmacy stores thousands of drug molecules in shelves and bottles, every plant cell produces a vast array of chemical compounds. However, plants don't make these for our benefit — they manufacture them for **self-defense (against insects and microbes), UV protection, pigmentation for pollinator attraction, and hormonal regulation**.

- The **red color of a tomato** is a **carotenoid (lycopene)** — acts as a UV shield and a human antioxidant.
- The **bitter taste of tea** is due to **polyphenols (catechins)** — defends the plant and protects our heart.
- The **pungency of garlic** comes from **organosulfur compounds (allicin)** — antimicrobial defense for the plant and cholesterol-lowering agent for us.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Phytochemicals are NOT essential nutrients (no Recommended Dietary Allowance/RDA), but their regular dietary intake is strongly correlated with reduced risk of **chronic degenerative diseases** — a concept central to the field of *Functional Foods and Nutraceuticals*.

## 1.3 Why Study Phytochemicals? — The Trifecta of Investigation

The study of phytochemicals revolves around three pillars, each requiring specific analytical methods:

1. **Chemical Characterization** — *What is in the plant?* (Extraction + Chromatography + Spectroscopy)
2. **Biological Activity** — *What does it do in the body?* (In vitro bioassays + Cell culture + Animal models)
3. **Health Outcomes** — *Does it prevent disease in humans?* (Epidemiology + Clinical trials)

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU question pattern: *"Differentiate between primary and secondary metabolites of plants."* Always mention that **phytochemicals are secondary metabolites** and contrast them with primary metabolites (sugars, amino acids, lipids) involved in basic plant growth and reproduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Phytochemicals (Module 1 Foundation)

| **Class** | **Sub-Classes** | **Food Sources** | **Primary Health Effect** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyphenols** | Flavonoids, Phenolic acids, Stilbenes, Lignans, Tannins | Berries, tea, coffee, grapes, soy, cocoa | Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective |
| **Carotenoids** | Carotenes (α, β), Xanthophylls (lutein, zeaxanthin) | Carrots, tomatoes, spinach, papaya | Vision protection, antioxidant, pro-vitamin A |
| **Alkaloids** | Caffeine, capsaicin, piperine, solanine | Coffee, chilli, black pepper, potatoes | CNS stimulants, analgesic, antimicrobial |
| **Terpenoids** | Limonene, menthol, taxol, curcumin | Citrus peels, mint, turmeric | Anticancer, anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective |
| **Organosulfur Compounds** | Allicin, glucosinolates, sulforaphane, indole-3-carbinol | Garlic, onion, cruciferous vegetables | Antimicrobial, anticancer, detoxification |
| **Phytosterols & Saponins** | β-sitosterol, stigmasterol, ginsenosides | Nuts, seeds, legumes, ginseng | Cholesterol-lowering, immune modulation |

> [!NOTE]
> The **total phytochemical content in the human diet** is estimated to be around **1–2 g/day** (compared to macronutrient intake of ~400 g/day). Despite the small quantity, their cumulative biological impact over a lifetime is substantial.

## 2.2 Methodological Framework — The 5-Stage Pipeline

The study of phytochemicals follows a **standardized analytical pipeline** universally adopted in food chemistry and nutraceutical research:

### **Stage 1: Sample Preparation**
- Drying (oven, freeze, sun), grinding, sieving to obtain uniform particle size (< 0.5 mm).
- Critical for reproducibility.

### **Stage 2: Extraction**
- Transfer of phytochemicals from the solid plant matrix into a liquid solvent.
- Methods: **Maceration, Soxhlet, Ultrasound-Assisted Extraction (UAE), Microwave-Assisted Extraction (MAE), Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE), Enzyme-Assisted Extraction (EAE).**

### **Stage 3: Isolation & Purification**
- Separation of crude extract into individual compounds.
- Methods: **Solvent partitioning, Column Chromatography, Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC), Flash Chromatography, Preparative HPLC.**

### **Stage 4: Characterization / Structure Elucidation**
- Identification of the chemical structure of purified compounds.
- Methods: **UV-Vis Spectroscopy, FTIR, NMR (¹H and ¹³C), Mass Spectrometry (MS), LC-MS, GC-MS, X-ray Crystallography.**

### **Stage 5: Bioactivity Evaluation**
- Determination of biological function in laboratory and biological systems.
- Methods: **In vitro antioxidant assays (DPPH, ABTS, FRAP, ORAC), Antimicrobial assays (MIC, MBC), Cell culture studies, In vivo animal models, Human clinical trials.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **Extraction Yield** | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{W_{extract}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | $W$ = mass in grams; dimensionless % |
| **DPPH Radical Scavenging Activity** | $\%~\text{Scavenging} = \dfrac{A_{control} - A_{sample}}{A_{control}} \times 100$ | $A$ = absorbance at **517 nm** |
| **IC₅₀ (Half Maximal Inhibitory Concentration)** | Concentration of sample required to scavenge **50%** of free radicals | mg/mL or μg/mL; lower IC₅₀ = higher antioxidant potency |
| **Total Phenolic Content (Folin-Ciocalteu)** | $C = \dfrac{c \times V}{m}$ where $C$ = mg GAE/g | Expressed as **mg Gallic Acid Equivalents per gram (mg GAE/g)** |
| **Total Flavonoid Content** | Expressed as **mg Quercetin Equivalents (QE/g)** using AlCl₃ colorimetry | Read at **415 nm** |
| **FRAP (Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power)** | $\text{FRAP} = \dfrac{A_{sample} - A_{blank}}{A_{standard}} \times C_{standard}$ | μmol Fe²⁺/g; read at **593 nm** |
| **Antimicrobial MIC** | Lowest concentration of extract that inhibits visible microbial growth | μg/mL; lower MIC = higher potency |
| **% Inhibition (Cell Viability Assay)** | $\%~\text{Inhibition} = \dfrac{A_{control} - A_{sample}}{A_{control}} \times 100$ | MTT assay read at **570 nm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization tip:** DPPH, ABTS, FRAP, and ORAC are the **"Big Four" antioxidant assays** most frequently asked in KTU Module 1 questions. Note the wavelength for each: **DPPH = 517 nm, ABTS = 734 nm, FRAP = 593 nm, β-carotene bleaching = 470 nm**.

## 2.4 Why These Methods Matter in Real Engineering & Food Industry Applications

- **Food Quality Control:** HPLC quantification of capsaicin in chilli powders, lycopene in tomato ketchup, curcumin in turmeric paste.
- **Nutraceutical Industry:** Standardization of extracts (e.g., 95% curcuminoids, 50% OPCs from grape seed) requires validated HPLC methods.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FSSAI (India) and EFSA (EU) require phytochemical profiling for health claim substantiation.
- **Food Product Development:** Designing **functional foods** (e.g., anthocyanin-enriched beverages) requires knowledge of extraction efficiency and bioactivity retention.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DPPH Free Radical Scavenging Activity

**Problem Statement:** A methanolic extract of turmeric was tested for DPPH scavenging. The following absorbance values were recorded at **517 nm** using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer:

| **Tube** | **Description** | **Absorbance at 517 nm** |
|---|---|---|
| Control (DPPH + methanol) | Reference | **0.850** |
| Sample (DPPH + 100 μg/mL extract) | Test | **0.272** |
| Sample (DPPH + 200 μg/mL extract) | Test | **0.170** |
| Sample (DPPH + 400 μg/mL extract) | Test | **0.085** |

Calculate the **% scavenging activity** for each concentration and determine the **IC₅₀** by linear interpolation.

### **Solution: Step 1 — Apply the % Scavenging Formula**

For 100 μg/mL:

$$\%~\text{Scavenging} = \frac{A_{control} - A_{sample}}{A_{control}} \times 100$$

$$\%~\text{Scavenging}_{100} = \frac{0.850 - 0.272}{0.850} \times 100$$

$$\%~\text{Scavenging}_{100} = \frac{0.578}{0.850} \times 100 = 68.00\%$$

For 200 μg/mL:

$$\%~\text{Scavenging}_{200} = \frac{0.850 - 0.170}{0.850} \times 100$$

$$\%~\text{Scavenging}_{200} = \frac{0.680}{0.850} \times 100 = 80.00\%$$

For 400 μg/mL:

$$\%~\text{Scavenging}_{400} = \frac{0.850 - 0.085}{0.850} \times 100$$

$$\%~\text{Scavenging}_{400} = \frac{0.765}{0.850} \times 100 = 90.00\%$$

### **Solution: Step 2 — Determine IC₅₀ by Linear Interpolation**

We need the concentration at which scavenging = 50%. Since 100 μg/mL gives 68% (above 50%), we look at the 0–100 μg/mL region using a linear model:

$$IC_{50} = C_{low} + \left( \frac{50 - \%S_{low}}{\%S_{high} - \%S_{low}} \right) \times (C_{high} - C_{low})$$

Assume a linear relation between 0 μg/mL (0% scavenging) and 100 μg/mL (68% scavenging):

$$IC_{50} = 0 + \left( \frac{50 - 0}{68 - 0} \right) \times (100 - 0)$$

$$IC_{50} = \frac{50}{68} \times 100 = 73.53~\mu g/mL$$

**Interpretation:** A lower IC₅₀ indicates higher antioxidant potency. Standard ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) typically shows IC₅₀ ≈ 5–10 μg/mL, so the turmeric extract (IC₅₀ ≈ 73.53 μg/mL) is a moderate antioxidant, likely due to its **curcuminoids** content.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Total Phenolic Content (Folin-Ciocalteu Method)

**Problem Statement:** The total phenolic content of a grape seed extract was determined using the **Folin-Ciocalteu method** with **gallic acid** as standard. The calibration curve gave the equation:

$$A = 0.0012 \times C + 0.005$$

where $A$ = absorbance at 765 nm, $C$ = gallic acid concentration in μg/mL.

For the extract: 1.0 g of grape seed was extracted in 100 mL methanol; 0.5 mL of this extract (after appropriate dilution by 1:10) gave an absorbance of **0.485**. Calculate the **Total Phenolic Content (TPC) in mg GAE/g**.

### **Solution: Step 1 — Find the Gallic Acid Equivalent Concentration in the Test Tube**

From the calibration equation:

$$C = \frac{A - 0.005}{0.0012}$$

$$C = \frac{0.485 - 0.005}{0.0012} = \frac{0.480}{0.0012} = 400~\mu g/mL$$

This is the concentration in the **diluted** (1:10) test tube.

### **Solution: Step 2 — Correct for the Dilution Factor**

Original extract concentration in the test tube = 400 × 10 = **4000 μg/mL = 4 mg/mL**

### **Solution: Step 3 — Calculate TPC in mg GAE/g of Original Sample**

The 0.5 mL taken for assay contained the phenolics. Total phenolics in 100 mL extract:

$$\text{Total phenolics} = 4~\text{mg/mL} \times 100~\text{mL} = 400~\text{mg GAE per 100 mL extract}$$

Since 1.0 g of grape seed was used:

$$\text{TPC} = \frac{400~\text{mg GAE}}{1.0~\text{g sample}} = 400~\text{mg GAE/g}$$

**Interpretation:** Grape seed is one of the richest sources of polyphenols (proanthocyanidins), with typical TPC values of **200–500 mg GAE/g** in commercial extracts. This justifies its widespread use in nutraceutical formulations.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — "Big Four" Antioxidant Assays

| **Feature** | **DPPH** | **ABTS** | **FRAP** | **ORAC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Electron transfer (ET) | Electron transfer (ET) | Electron transfer (ET) | Hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) |
| **Radical** | DPPH• (purple) | ABTS•⁺ (blue-green) | Fe³� → Fe²⁺ complex | Fluorescein + AAPH• |
| **Wavelength (nm)** | **517** | **734** | **593** | Fluorescence (Ex 485, Em 520) |
| **Solvent Compatibility** | Methanol, ethanol | Aqueous + organic | Aqueous | Aqueous (PBS) |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate | High | Moderate | Very high |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Low | High (fluorometer) |
| **Result Expression** | IC₅₀ (μg/mL) | IC₅₀ (μg/mL) | μmol Fe²⁺/g | μmol TE/g (Trolox Equivalents) |
| **KTU Favourite?** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **"DASH"** — **D**PPH (517), **A**BTS (734), **S**ame family (ET), **H**ighlight FRAP (593). Note: Wavelengths for DPPH and FRAP differ by exactly 76 nm — a useful memory hook.
