# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define phytochemicals. How are they different from primary metabolites of plants?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Phytochemicals are **biologically active, non-nutritive secondary metabolites** produced by plants that confer defense against pathogens, UV radiation, and herbivores. They are classified into polyphenols, carotenoids, alkaloids, terpenoids, organosulfur compounds, and phytosterols.

**Distinction from primary metabolites:**

| **Feature** | **Primary Metabolites** | **Phytochemicals (Secondary)** |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Growth, development, reproduction | Defense, signaling, pigmentation |
| Examples | Sugars, amino acids, lipids, nucleic acids | Flavonoids, alkaloids, terpenoids |
| Universality | Present in ALL plants | Species/genus-specific |
| Quantity | Bulk amounts | Trace amounts (μg–mg/g) |
| Nutritional role for humans | Macronutrients, energy | Non-nutritive, health-protective |

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clear comparison table)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any three modern extraction methods used for phytochemical isolation. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Ultrasound-Assisted Extraction (UAE):** Uses cavitation bubbles; **advantage** — faster extraction (15–30 min vs. 24 h for maceration) and lower solvent volume.
2. **Microwave-Assisted Extraction (MAE):** Uses dielectric heating; **advantage** — rapid internal heating, reduces extraction time to minutes, energy-efficient.
3. **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE):** Uses CO₂ above critical point (31.1 °C, 73.8 bar); **advantage** — solvent-free extract (no residue), environmentally green, GRAS status of CO₂.

**(1 mark per method with advantage)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify the major groups of phytochemicals with suitable food source examples. Discuss the role of **polyphenols** in health promotion with at least **three** specific mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle, procedure, and significance of the **DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) free radical scavenging assay** for evaluating antioxidant activity of phytochemicals. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (a):**

**Classification of Phytochemicals (3 marks):**

| **Class** | **Sub-groups** | **Example Compounds** | **Food Source** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polyphenols | Flavonoids, phenolic acids, lignans, stilbenes, tannins | Quercetin, gallic acid, resveratrol, catechin | Tea, berries, grapes, soy |
| Carotenoids | Carotenes, xanthophylls | β-carotene, lycopene, lutein | Carrot, tomato, spinach |
| Alkaloids | Purine, tropane, piperidine | Caffeine, capsaicin, piperine | Coffee, chilli, pepper |
| Terpenoids | Mono, sesqui, di, tri-terpenes | Limonene, menthol, taxol, curcumin | Citrus, mint, turmeric |
| Organosulfur | Glucosinolates, thiosulfinates | Allicin, sulforaphane, indole-3-carbinol | Garlic, cabbage, broccoli |
| Phytosterols & Saponins | Sterols, triterpenoid saponins | β-sitosterol, ginsenoside | Nuts, soybean, ginseng |

**Three Mechanisms of Polyphenols in Health Promotion (4 marks):**

1. **Direct Radical Scavenging (Antioxidant Action):**
   Polyphenols donate hydrogen atoms or electrons from their aromatic hydroxyl (–OH) groups to stabilize reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as superoxide (O₂•⁻), hydroxyl (•OH), and peroxyl (ROO•) radicals. The resulting **phenoxyl radical** is resonance-stabilized by the aromatic ring, terminating the radical chain reaction.

2. **Modulation of Nrf2-ARE Pathway:**
   Polyphenols activate the **Nuclear factor erythroid 2–related factor 2 (Nrf2)** transcription factor, which translocates to the nucleus and binds the **Antioxidant Response Element (ARE)**, upregulating expression of endogenous antioxidant enzymes — **superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), glutathione peroxidase (GPx), and heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1)**.

3. **Inhibition of NF-κB and Pro-inflammatory Cytokines:**
   Polyphenols (e.g., resveratrol, curcumin) suppress the **Nuclear Factor kappa B (NF-κB)** signaling cascade by inhibiting IκB kinase (IKK), thereby preventing the nuclear translocation of NF-κB and reducing transcription of **TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and COX-2**. This results in **anti-inflammatory** and **chemopreventive** effects.

4. *(Optional 4th point)* **Modulation of Gut Microbiota:** Polyphenols act as prebiotics, promoting beneficial *Bifidobacteria* and *Lactobacilli* while inhibiting pathogenic species, contributing to gut health and metabolic regulation.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (b): DPPH Assay**

**Principle (3 marks):**
DPPH is a **stable, synthetic, nitrogen-centered free radical** with a deep violet color (absorption maximum at **517 nm** in methanol). When an antioxidant (AH) from the phytochemical extract donates a hydrogen atom, DPPH• is reduced to **DPPH-H (1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazine)**, a pale yellow non-radical form. The **decrease in absorbance at 517 nm** is directly proportional to the **radical scavenging capacity** of the sample.

$$\text{DPPH}^{\bullet} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{DPPH-H} + \text{A}^{\bullet}$$

The resultant radical A• is stabilized by resonance within the aromatic ring of the polyphenol.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Preparation of DPPH solution:** 0.1 mM DPPH in methanol (stored in dark, freshly prepared).
2. **Sample preparation:** Extract dissolved in methanol at varying concentrations (e.g., 25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL).
3. **Reaction:** 1 mL of DPPH solution + 1 mL of sample; vortexed; incubated in dark at **room temperature for 30 min**.
4. **Measurement:** Absorbance read at **517 nm** using UV-Vis spectrophotometer against methanol blank.
5. **Control:** DPPH + methanol (no sample).
6. **Reference standard:** Ascorbic acid or BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene) at same concentrations.
7. **Calculation:** % scavenging using the formula; **IC₅₀** determined by plotting % scavenging vs. concentration.

**Significance (1 mark):**
- Simple, rapid, economical, requires no specialized equipment.
- Widely used for **screening antioxidant potential** of food extracts, nutraceuticals, and plant-based formulations.
- Limitations: DPPH is not biologically relevant radical; reaction kinetics with carotenoids may be slow; interference from anthocyanins (which absorb at 517 nm) — use proper blanks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **classification of methods** used to study phytochemicals, with specific examples of techniques used at each stage from extraction to structure elucidation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Folin-Ciocalteu method** for estimating **Total Phenolic Content (TPC)**. Include the principle, reagents, procedure, calculation, and expression of results in **mg GAE/g**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (a):**

The methods used to study phytochemicals are systematically organized into a **5-stage pipeline** corresponding to the workflow of phytochemical analysis:

**Stage 1: Extraction Methods (2 marks)**

Extraction is the first critical step, aiming to recover phytochemicals from the solid plant matrix into a suitable solvent based on the principle of **"like dissolves like"** (polarity matching).

- **Maceration:** Soaking powdered plant material in solvent (e.g., 70% ethanol) for 3–7 days with occasional shaking. Simple but slow and solvent-intensive.
- **Soxhlet Extraction:** Continuous hot solvent reflux extraction (6–24 h). Efficient for non-thermolabile compounds.
- **Ultrasound-Assisted Extraction (UAE):** Uses 20–100 kHz ultrasonic waves to create cavitation bubbles that rupture cell walls. Reduces time to 15–60 min.
- **Microwave-Assisted Extraction (MAE):** Dielectric heating causes rapid internal cell rupture. Very fast (5–15 min).
- **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE):** CO₂ above critical point (31.1 °C, 73.8 bar) acts as a tunable solvent. Produces solvent-free, GRAS-quality extracts ideal for nutraceuticals.
- **Enzyme-Assisted Extraction (EAE):** Cellulase, pectinase pretreatment to break down cell wall polysaccharides, releasing bound phytochemicals.

**Stage 2: Isolation / Purification Methods (2 marks)**

- **Solvent Partitioning:** Sequential extraction with solvents of increasing polarity (hexane → chloroform → ethyl acetate → butanol → aqueous).
- **Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC):** Quick analytical separation on silica gel plates; Rf value for identification.
- **Column Chromatography:** Silica gel or Sephadex LH-20 for preparative separation.
- **Flash Chromatography:** Pre-packed silica columns with pressurized solvent for rapid purification.
- **Preparative HPLC:** High-purity milligram-to-gram scale isolation of individual compounds.

**Stage 3: Structural Elucidation (2 marks)**

- **UV-Vis Spectroscopy:** Identifies chromophores (e.g., flavonoid Band I at 300–550 nm, Band II at 240–285 nm).
- **FTIR (Fourier-Transform Infrared):** Functional group identification (–OH, C=O, C=C aromatic).
- **¹H and ¹³C NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance):** Complete structural and stereochemical elucidation; COSY, HSQC, HMBC for connectivity.
- **Mass Spectrometry (MS):** Molecular weight determination and fragmentation pattern; **LC-MS** and **GC-MS** couple separation with identification — most powerful modern technique.
- **X-ray Crystallography:** Absolute 3D structure for crystalline compounds (e.g., taxol).

**Stage 4: Bioactivity Evaluation (1 mark)** *(in vitro, cell culture, in vivo, clinical)* and **Stage 5: Computational Methods** *(molecular docking, ADMET)* complete the modern portfolio.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (b): Folin-Ciocalteu Method for TPC**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The Folin-Ciocalteu (F-C) reagent is a mixture of **phosphomolybdate and phosphotungstate** (yellow complex). In alkaline conditions, phenolics reduce the Mo(VI)/W(VI) to **Mo(V)/W(V)**, producing a **blue-colored phosphomolybdenum-tungsten complex** (λmax = **765 nm**). The intensity of the blue color is **directly proportional to the total phenolic concentration**.

**Reagents (1 mark):**
- **Folin-Ciocalteu reagent** (commercially available, 2N, diluted 1:10 with water before use).
- **20% Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃)** solution — provides alkaline pH.
- **Gallic acid standard** (100 μg/mL stock; serial dilutions: 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 μg/mL).
- Distilled water.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Pipette 0.5 mL of extract / standard into test tubes.
2. Add **2.5 mL of diluted F-C reagent**; mix; allow to stand for **5 min**.
3. Add **2.0 mL of 20% Na₂CO₃** solution; vortex.
4. Incubate in **dark at room temperature for 30 min** (or 40 °C water bath for 15 min).
5. Measure absorbance at **765 nm** using UV-Vis spectrophotometer.
6. Plot **calibration curve** of absorbance vs. gallic acid concentration (μg/mL); obtain linear equation $A = mC + b$.

**Calculation & Result Expression (2 marks):**

The concentration of phenolics in the test tube (C) is read from the calibration curve as **μg GAE/mL** (gallic acid equivalents). TPC of the original sample is then:

$$\text{TPC (mg GAE/g)} = \frac{C \times V \times D}{m \times 1000}$$

where:
- $C$ = concentration from calibration curve (μg/mL),
- $V$ = total volume of extract (mL),
- $D$ = dilution factor,
- $m$ = mass of sample (g),
- 1000 = conversion factor from μg to mg.

**Result Expression:** Results are universally reported as **mg GAE/g dry weight of sample** (or mg GAE/100 g for food composition tables).

**Note on limitations:** The F-C reagent is **not specific to phenolics** — it also reacts with ascorbic acid, reducing sugars, and some amino acids (tyrosine, tryptophan). Hence, the term "Total Phenolics" is preferred over "Polyphenols" for F-C results. More specific methods like **HPLC-DAD** are recommended for individual polyphenol quantification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DPPH wavelength** (517 nm) with FRAP (593 nm) — costs 1 mark easily.
> 2. **Writing "polyphenols" instead of "secondary metabolites"** when defining phytochemicals — both are accepted, but using "secondary metabolites" shows higher Bloom's level (taxonomy level: *Understand* vs. *Apply*).
> 3. **Failing to write the balanced equation** in DPPH question — examiners specifically look for `DPPH• + AH → DPPH-H + A•`.
> 4. **Skipping the dilution factor** in F-C calculations — a common error worth 2 marks in numerical problems.
> 5. **Not specifying wavelength** in spectrophotometric assays — instant ½ mark deduction.
> 6. **Mixing up GAE (Gallic Acid Equivalents)** with **QE (Quercetin Equivalents)** in Total Flavonoid Content.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Methods to Study Phytochemicals & Health Promotion (Introduction)**

- **Phytochemicals** = plant secondary metabolites; **non-nutritive**; **biologically active**; classified into **6 major groups** (polyphenols, carotenoids, alkaloids, terpenoids, organosulfur, phytosterols/saponins).
- **5-Stage Analytical Pipeline:** Sample Prep → Extraction → Isolation → Characterization → Bioactivity Evaluation.
- **Key Extraction Methods:** Maceration, Soxhlet, **UAE, MAE, SFE, EAE** (modern methods are the trend in KTU 2024).
- **Key Characterization Tools:** UV-Vis, FTIR, **NMR (¹H, ¹³C), MS (LC-MS, GC-MS)**.
- **"Big Four" Antioxidant Assays:** **DPPH (517 nm), ABTS (734 nm), FRAP (593 nm), ORAC (fluorescence)**.
- **Total Phenolic Content** = Folin-Ciocalteu = expressed as **mg GAE/g**.
- **Total Flavonoid Content** = AlCl₃ colorimetry = expressed as **mg QE/g** (415 nm).
- **Antimicrobial potency** = expressed as **MIC (μg/mL)** — lower MIC = higher potency.
- **Health Promotion Mechanisms:** **Antioxidant (Nrf2-ARE), Anti-inflammatory (NF-κB inhibition), Cardioprotective (LDL oxidation), Chemopreventive (apoptosis induction), Neuroprotective (BDNF upregulation).**
- **Modern Trend:** Molecular Docking + Network Pharmacology + ADMET prediction are now part of the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- **Bioavailability is the bottleneck:** Many polyphenols have low oral bioavailability (< 5%) — explain using the **"bioavailability paradox"** concept in viva questions.
- **Polypharmacology concept:** One phytochemical (e.g., curcumin) targets **multiple signaling pathways** simultaneously — the "magic shotgun" approach.
- **FSSAI / EFSA regulatory context** is increasingly asked — remember that **health claims ≠ disease treatment claims** (nutraceuticals cannot claim to "cure" diseases).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morphological, Microscopical, Physical, Chemical, and Biological Analysis of Herbal Drugs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Morphological, Microscopical, Physical, Chemical, and Biological Analysis of Herbal Drugs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Analysis of Herbal Drugs"?

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
The analysis of herbal drugs is a systematic, multi-tiered quality control protocol that employs **morphological (macroscopic), microscopical, physical, chemical, and biological** evaluation methods to authenticate, standardize, and establish the safety-efficacy profile of plant-derived medicinal materials before they are incorporated into food, nutraceutical, or pharmaceutical formulations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** This topic forms the analytical backbone of Module 1. Every phytochemical claim made downstream (antioxidant capacity, antimicrobial action, shelf-life extension) **must** be substantiated by passing these five analytical gates. Skipping any tier invalidates the entire quality chain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Five-Stage Airport Security Check"

Imagine a passenger (herbal drug) arriving at an international airport (a nutraceutical/food manufacturing plant):

| Airport Stage | Herbal Drug Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Visual ID check at the gate | **Morphological** | Confirm you look like a valid passenger |
| Hand-luggage X-ray scan | **Microscopical** | Verify internal contents are genuine |
| Body weight & biometric scan | **Physical** | Quantify standard physical parameters |
| Customs chemical swab test | **Chemical** | Detect specific molecular fingerprints |
| Behavioural / risk profiling | **Biological** | Confirm the substance actually works safely |

A passenger who clears all five stages is **cleared for boarding** — likewise, a herbal crude drug that clears all five analyses is **cleared for formulation**.

### 1.3 The Five Analytical Domains at a Glance

**1. Morphological (Macroscopic) Analysis**
Examination of the whole/crushed drug with the **naked eye or 10× lens** for organoleptic characters: shape, size, colour, odour, taste, fracture, texture.

**2. Microscopical Analysis**
Examination of thin sections/powder under **compound microscope** (40×–400×) to reveal cellular structures: stomata, trichomes, vascular bundles, starch grains, calcium oxalate crystals.

**3. Physical Analysis**
Determination of **objective physical constants**: moisture, ash values, extractive values, swelling index, foaming index, melting point, refractive index, specific gravity.

**4. Chemical Analysis**
Qualitative & quantitative phytochemical screening for **secondary metabolites**: alkaloids, flavonoids, tannins, terpenoids, glycosides, saponins, steroids, phenols.

**5. Biological Analysis (Bioassays)**
In-vitro / in-vivo evaluation of **functional activity**: antioxidant (DPPH, ABTS), antimicrobial (agar diffusion, MIC), cytotoxicity (MTT), anti-inflammatory (HRBC membrane stabilization).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why five tiers and not one?** A single test is never definitive. Morphology tells you *what plant*, microscopy tells you *which part*, physics tells you *how pure*, chemistry tells you *what molecules are present*, and biology tells you *whether the molecules actually work*. Together they constitute the **WHO-AYUSH-IPR pharmacopoeial standard** adopted in food-phytochemistry.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Frameworks (Bold Key Constants)

- **WHO** (World Health Organization) guidelines for quality control of herbal medicines.
- **AYUSH** (Ministry of Ayurveda, Yoga & Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha, Homeopathy) — Pharmacopoeial standards.
- **Pharmacopoeial standards:** Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP), British Pharmacopoeia (BP), United States Pharmacopoeia (USP), European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.).
- **Limit values** are drug-specific; **ash values typically < 10%**, **moisture < 10%**, **foreign matter < 2%** for most approved crude drugs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Morphological Analysis — Operational Logic

Step-by-step logic a KTU examiner expects:

1. **Sample preparation:** Collect representative lot, air-dry below **40 °C** to preserve thermolabile constituents.
2. **Sensory documentation:**
   - **Shape:** ovoid, conical, cylindrical, reniform, lanceolate.
   - **Size:** length × width in cm/mm using a calibrated scale.
   - **Colour:** noted under daylight using standard colour charts (e.g., RHS Colour Chart).
   - **Odour:** aromatic, pungent, characteristic, odourless (sniff only briefly — many essential oils are irritant).
   - **Taste:** sweet, bitter, sour, acrid, mucilaginous, astringent (a wetted cotton bud is used; never taste unknown toxic material).
   - **Fracture & texture:** brittle, fibrous, horny, mealy, waxy.
3. **Comparative authentication:** Match against pharmacopoeial monograph descriptions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In food industry QA labs, macroscopic triage is the *first line of detection* for adulteration — e.g., detecting *Cassia angustifolia* (senna) adulterating *Cassia acutifolia*, or detecting exhausted (re-extracted) drugs by their pale, dull colour.

### 2.2 Microscopical Analysis — Operational Logic

1. **Sectioning:** Free-hand sectioning with a razor / microtome (rotary / sledge) at **10–20 µm thickness**.
2. **Staining:** Safranin (red — lignified walls), Fast Green (green — cellulose), Iodine (blue-black — starch), Phloroglucinol-HCl (red — lignin), Ruthenium Red (pink — mucilage).
3. **Mounting:** Canada balsam (permanent) or glycerine-water (temporary).
4. **Examination:** Under **compound light microscope** at 40×, 100×, 400×.
5. **Diagnostic features to record:**
   - **Stomata type:** paracytic, diacytic, anisocytic, anomocytic.
   - **Trichomes:** glandular (capitate, peltate) vs. non-glandular (unicellular, multicellular, stellate, hooked).
   - **Vascular bundles:** arrangement, type (collateral, bicollateral, concentric).
   - **Ergastic cell contents:** starch grains (hilum, striations), calcium oxalate crystals (prisms, raphides, druses, styloids, microsphenoids), aleurone grains, oil globules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 high-yield point:** For leaf drugs, look for **venation pattern + stomatal index**; for bark drugs, look for **periderm + medullary rays**; for rhizome/root drugs, look for **vascular bundle arrangement + starch type**.

### 2.3 Physical Analysis — The Quantitative Backbone

This is the **most formula-heavy** sub-topic. KTU frequently tests numerical problem-solving here.

#### A. Moisture Content (Loss on Drying)

$$ \%MC = \frac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100 $$

Where $W_i$ = initial weight, $W_f$ = weight after drying to constant mass (typically at **105 °C** in a hot-air oven until two consecutive weighings differ by ≤ 0.5 mg).

#### B. Ash Values

$$ \%Total\,Ash = \frac{W_{ash}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 $$

- **Acid-insoluble ash** = ash boiled with dilute HCl, filtered, re-ignited — measures **silica & soil contamination**.
- **Water-soluble ash** = ash boiled with water, filtered, re-ignited — measures **alkali salts**.
- **Sulphated ash** = sample treated with H₂SO₄ before ignition — converts metals to **sulphates**; useful for **inorganic element profiling**.

#### C. Extractive Values

$$ \%Extractive = \frac{W_{extract\,residue}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 $$

- **Water-soluble extractive** (cold & hot maceration).
- **Alcohol-soluble extractive** (ethanol strength is drug-specific: 70%, 90%).
- **Ether-soluble / chloroform-soluble extractive** (for fixed oils, alkaloids, resins).

#### D. Swelling Index (SI)

$$ SI = \frac{V_{swollen\,drug}}{V_{initial\,dry\,drug}} $$$

- Applied to **mucilaginous drugs** (e.g., *Plantago ovata* husk, *Sterculia* gum). SI typically > 10 for husk materials.

#### E. Foaming Index (FI)

$$ FI = \frac{1000}{a} $$

where $a$ = ml of decoction required to produce a persistent 1 cm foam column (10 sec persistence). Used to detect **saponins**.

#### F. Other Physical Constants

- **Melting point** (capillary tube method, digital melting point apparatus).
- **Refractive index** (Abbe refractometer, used for fixed/volatile oils).
- **Specific gravity** (pycnometer).
- **Optical rotation** (polarimeter — used for sugars, alkaloids, glycosides).
- **Viscosity** (Ostwald viscometer).
- **Acid value, Saponification value, Iodine value, Ester value** (for fixed oils).

### 2.4 Chemical Analysis — Phytochemical Screening Logic

| Secondary Metabolite | Confirmatory Reagent | Positive Observation |
|---|---|---|
| **Alkaloids** | Mayer's / Wagner's / Dragendorff's | Cream / reddish-brown / orange-brown ppt |
| **Flavonoids** | Shinoda test (Mg + HCl), Alkaline reagent | Pink / red / crimson colour |
| **Tannins** | Ferric chloride (FeCl₃) | Blue-black (hydrolysable) or green-black (condensed) |
| **Phenols** | Folin–Ciocalteu reagent | Blue colour |
| **Saponins** | Foam test (shake with water) | Persistent honeycomb foam > 1 cm |
| **Glycosides** | Borntrager's (anthraquinones), Legal's / Keller–Kiliani (cardiac) | Pink / red / brown ring |
| **Steroids / Triterpenoids** | Liebermann–Burchard / Salkowski | Green / red / violet colour |
| **Carbohydrates** | Molisch's test | Purple ring at interface |
| **Proteins / Amino acids** | Biuret, Ninhydrin | Violet / purple colour |
| **Fixed oils & Fats** | Spot / Stain test | Translucent oily spot on filter paper |

#### Quantification Formulas (Bioactive Content)

**Total Phenolic Content (TPC) — Folin–Ciocalteu method:**

$$ TPC\,(mg\,GAE/g) = \frac{A_{sample} \times C_{standard} \times V}{A_{standard} \times W_{sample}} $$

GAE = Gallic Acid Equivalents.

**Total Flavonoid Content (TFC) — Aluminium chloride method:**

$$ TFC\,(mg\,QE/g) = \frac{A_{sample} \times C_{standard} \times V}{A_{standard} \times W_{sample}} $$

QE = Quercetin Equivalents.

### 2.5 Biological Analysis (Bioassays) — The "Does It Work?" Stage

**A. Antioxidant Activity — DPPH Radical Scavenging Assay**

$$ \%Scavenging = \frac{A_{control} - A_{sample}}{A_{control}} \times 100 $$

- DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) absorbs at **517 nm**.
- $IC_{50}$ = concentration of sample that scavenges **50%** of DPPH radicals (lower $IC_{50}$ = stronger antioxidant).

**B. ABTS Radical Cation Decolorization Assay**

$$ \%Scavenging = \frac{A_{control} - A_{sample}}{A_{control}} \times 100 $$

ABTS⁺ absorbs at **734 nm**.

**C. Antimicrobial Activity — Agar Well / Disc Diffusion**

$$ \%Inhibition = \frac{D_{sample} - D_{solvent}}{D_{standard}} \times 100 $$

Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) and Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC) are determined by **broth micro-dilution** with serial two-fold dilutions.

**D. Cytotoxicity — MTT Assay**

$$ \%Viability = \frac{A_{sample} - A_{blank}}{A_{control} - A_{blank}} \times 100 $$

MTT (yellow) is reduced by mitochondrial dehydrogenases of live cells to **formazan** (purple, read at **570 nm**).

**E. Anti-inflammatory — HRBC Membrane Stabilization**

$$ \%Membrane\,Stabilization = \frac{A_{control} - A_{sample}}{A_{control}} \times 100 $$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and stick it inside your notebook cover.** KTU 2024 numericals are 80% formula-substitution; rote-memorizing these expressions guarantees Part-A marks.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content | $\%MC = \frac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$ | % w/w, dried at 105 °C |
| 2 | Total ash | $\%Ash = \frac{W_{ash}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % w/w, ignited at 450 °C |
| 3 | Acid-insoluble ash | $\%AIA = \frac{W_{AIA}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % w/w |
| 4 | Water-soluble ash | $\%WSA = \frac{W_{ash} - W_{water\,residue}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % w/w |
| 5 | Extractive value | $\%Extractive = \frac{W_{residue}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % w/w |
| 6 | Swelling index | $SI = \frac{V_{swollen}}{V_{dry}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | Foaming index | $FI = \frac{1000}{a}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | TPC | $TPC = \frac{A_s \times C_q \times V}{A_q \times W}$ | mg GAE/g |
| 9 | TFC | $TFC = \frac{A_s \times C_q \times V}{A_q \times W}$ | mg QE/g |
| 10 | DPPH scavenging | $\%S = \frac{A_c - A_s}{A_c} \times 100$ | Read at 517 nm |
| 11 | ABTS scavenging | $\%S = \frac{A_c - A_s}{A_c} \times 100$ | Read at 734 nm |
| 12 | MTT viability | $\%V = \frac{A_s - A_b}{A_c - A_b} \times 100$ | Read at 570 nm |
| 13 | HRBC stabilization | $\%Stab = \frac{A_c - A_s}{A_c} \times 100$ | % |
| 14 | Zone-of-inhibition | $ZI = D_{sample} - D_{solvent}$ | mm |

> **Note on table syntax:** In the rows above, the absolute-value `|...|` notation has been written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. KTU values inside the table are expressed with forward-slash fractions to remain table-safe.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food industry:** Phytochemical quantification is essential for **functional food** label claims (e.g., "rich in antioxidants", "supports immunity").
- **Shelf-life extension:** Antioxidant-rich herbal extracts (rosemary, green tea) replace synthetic preservatives (BHA, BHT) — their IC₅₀ values determine dosage.
- **Authentication:** Adulteration in spice markets (e.g., paprika adulterated with red lead, turmeric with metanil yellow) is detected by **ash value + microscopy + HPTLC fingerprinting**.
- **Regulatory compliance:** FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) mandates **ash, moisture, microbial load** limits for every herbal food ingredient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical — Total Ash Value Determination (KTU-style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 4.0 g powdered herbal sample is incinerated in a silica crucible. After complete ignition, the ash weighs 0.32 g. The ash is then boiled with dilute HCl, filtered, washed, and re-ignited to give 0.08 g of acid-insoluble residue. Calculate the **total ash %** and **acid-insoluble ash %**. Comment on the purity of the drug.

#### Step 1 — Identify the inputs

$$ W_{sample} = 4.0\,g, \quad W_{total\,ash} = 0.32\,g, \quad W_{AIA} = 0.08\,g $$

#### Step 2 — Apply the total ash formula

$$ \%Total\,Ash = \frac{W_{total\,ash}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 $$

$$ = \frac{0.32}{4.0} \times 100 $$

$$ = 0.08 \times 100 $$

$$ = 8.0\,\%\,w/w $$

#### Step 3 — Apply the acid-insoluble ash formula

$$ \%AIA = \frac{W_{AIA}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 $$

$$ = \frac{0.08}{4.0} \times 100 $$

$$ = 0.02 \times 100 $$

$$ = 2.0\,\%\,w/w $$

#### Step 4 — Comment on the purity

A total ash of 8.0% and acid-insoluble ash of 2.0% suggest the drug has **moderate inorganic content**, with 2.0% of that being siliceous/soil contamination. For a typical root drug (e.g., *Withania somnifera*), pharmacopoeial limit is total ash < 7% and AIA < 2%. This sample **fails the AIA limit** and indicates possible soil contamination or adulteration with earthy matter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The acid-insoluble ash is a *better* indicator of adulteration than total ash, because genuine plant cells contain only trace silica.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — DPPH Antioxidant Scavenging (KTU-style)

**Problem Statement:**
An herbal extract was tested for DPPH scavenging. The control (DPPH + methanol) gave absorbance $A_c = 0.985$ at 517 nm. The sample (DPPH + 100 µg/mL extract) gave $A_s = 0.342$. Calculate **% scavenging** and decide whether further dilution to determine $IC_{50}$ is justified.

#### Step 1 — Apply the DPPH formula

$$ \%Scavenging = \frac{A_c - A_s}{A_c} \times 100 $$

$$ = \frac{0.985 - 0.342}{0.985} \times 100 $$

$$ = \frac{0.643}{0.985} \times 100 $$

$$ = 65.28\,\% $$

#### Step 2 — Decide on dilution strategy

Since % scavenging = **65.28%** at 100 µg/mL, the $IC_{50}$ will lie below 100 µg/mL. To obtain a robust $IC_{50}$ curve, the test should be repeated at **five serial concentrations** — e.g., 10, 25, 50, 100, 200 µg/mL — and a dose-response curve plotted on semi-log paper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's trap:** Many students forget to subtract the solvent control. Always run a **blank with methanol only** and a **negative control with DPPH + methanol** in every antioxidant assay.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Analytical Matrix — Phytochemical Screening Flow

The matrix below is the **complete KTU 2024 reference map** you can reproduce in any exam answer. It is exhaustive and intentionally un-summarized.

#### A. Alkaloids

- **Mayer's reagent:** K₂[HgI₄] + KI → cream / yellowish precipitate.
- **Wagner's reagent:** I₂ + KI → reddish-brown precipitate.
- **Dragendorff's reagent:** KBiI₄ → orange-brown precipitate.
- **Hager's reagent:** Picric acid → yellow precipitate.
- **Tannic acid test:** Buff-coloured precipitate.

#### B. Flavonoids

- **Shinoda test:** Mg ribbon + conc. HCl → pink, red, magenta, or violet colour.
- **Alkaline reagent (NaOH):** yellow → colourless on adding dilute acid.
- **Lead acetate (10%):** yellow precipitate.
- **AlCl₃ (1%):** yellow fluorescence under UV.

#### C. Tannins & Phenolics

- **FeCl₃ (5%):** blue-black (gallotannins) or green-black (condensed tannins).
- **Lead acetate (10%):** bulky white precipitate.
- **Bromine water:** decolourization indicates phenols.
- **Vanillin-HCl:** red/pink for catechins and condensed tannins.
- **Folin–Ciocalteu:** blue colour, quantified at 765 nm.

#### D. Saponins

- **Foam test:** 1 g extract + 20 mL water, shake 15 min → persistent honeycomb foam (≥ 1 cm) for 30 min.
- **Haemolysis test:** Saponins lyse RBCs → red zone on blood agar.
- **Liebermann–Burchard:** after acid hydrolysis, gives green colour.

#### E. Glycosides

- **Borntrager's (anthraquinone):** sample + dilute H₂SO₄, boil, filter; add benzene; shake; add NH₄OH → pink, red, or violet in ammoniacal layer.
- **Legal's (cardiac):** extract + pyridine + sodium nitroprusside + NaOH → deep red (after alkalinization).
- **Keller–Kiliani (cardiac, deoxy-sugars):** extract + FeCl₃ + glacial acetic acid + H₂SO₄ → reddish-brown ring + green upper layer.
- **Foam test (saponin glycosides):** persistent foam.

#### F. Steroids & Triterpenoids

- **Liebermann–Burchard:** CHCl₃ extract + acetic anhydride + conc. H₂SO₄ → green / blue / violet ring.
- **Salkowski:** extract + CHCl₃ + conc. H₂SO₄ → red lower layer (sterols).
- **Rosenthaler:** extract + CHCl₃ + trichloroacetic acid → pink → purple.

#### G. Carbohydrates

- **Molisch's:** α-naphthol + conc. H₂SO₄ → purple/violet ring at interface.
- **Fehling's:** reducing sugars → brick-red Cu₂O precipitate.
- **Benedict's:** reducing sugars → orange-red precipitate.
- **Barfoed's:** monosaccharides → red Cu₂O within 1 min.

#### H. Proteins & Amino Acids

- **Biuret:** peptide bonds → violet / purple colour with CuSO₄ + NaOH.
- **Ninhydrin:** free amino acids → purple / blue / yellow (proline) colour.
- **Xanthoproteic:** aromatic amino acids → orange with HNO₃ → yellow → orange on NH₃.

#### I. Fixed Oils, Fats & Waxes

- **Spot / stain test:** oily translucent spot on filter paper.
- **Saponification test:** ethanolic KOH hydrolyses oil; titration with HCl determines saponification value.
- **Acid value:** titration of free fatty acids with KOH.
- **Iodine value (Wij's):** measures unsaturation — C=C double bonds react with ICl.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical — Moisture + Ash + Extractive Triple Check (Full KTU-style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 5.0 g powdered rhizome is dried in a hot-air oven at 105 °C to constant mass. The dried sample weighs 4.55 g. A 2.0 g portion of the dried powder is ignited to give 0.18 g of ash. The ash treated with dilute HCl yields 0.04 g of insoluble residue. A 3.0 g portion of dried powder is macerated in 100 mL of 90% ethanol for 24 h, filtered, evaporated, and the dried residue weighs 0.39 g. Calculate:
(a) % moisture
(b) % total ash
(c) % acid-insoluble ash
(d) % alcohol-soluble extractive

#### (a) Moisture Content

$$ \%MC = \frac{5.0 - 4.55}{5.0} \times 100 = \frac{0.45}{5.0} \times 100 = 9.0\,\% $$

#### (b) Total Ash

$$ \%Ash = \frac{0.18}{2.0} \times 100 = 9.0\,\% $$

#### (c) Acid-Insoluble Ash

$$ \%AIA = \frac{0.04}{2.0} \times 100 = 2.0\,\% $$

#### (d) Alcohol-Soluble Extractive

$$ \%Extractive = \frac{0.39}{3.0} \times 100 = 13.0\,\% $$

#### Interpretation

- Moisture at 9.0% — borderline acceptable (most pharmacopoeias stipulate < 10%).
- Total ash at 9.0% — slightly high; warrants AIA check.
- AIA at 2.0% — indicates 2% siliceous/earthy matter.
- Alcohol-soluble extractive at 13.0% — reasonable; suggests appreciable polar constituents (resins, glycosides, alkaloids, flavonoids).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-revise this question:** It combines three formulae into one problem. KTU frequently frames a "**composite numerical**" where you must simultaneously evaluate four parameters. Practice the units carefully — KTU deducts full marks for *missing units* even when the number is right.

### 3.5 Sensitivity & Specificity Considerations (Engineering Insight)

- **Sensitivity** of a phytochemical test = lowest detectable amount of metabolite (µg/mL).
- **Specificity** = ability to detect only the target metabolite in presence of interferents.
- **False positives** are common with FeCl₃ (any phenol reacts). Confirm with **TLC + Rf comparison** against standard.
- **False negatives** occur when the metabolite concentration is below the test's detection limit — pre-concentrate the extract (rotary evaporation, lyophilization).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Five-Tier Herbal Drug Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Herbal Drug Sample] --> B[Step 1: Morphological Analysis]
    B --> C[Step 2: Microscopical Analysis]
    C --> D[Step 3: Physical Analysis]
    D --> E[Step 4: Chemical Analysis]
    E --> F[Step 5: Biological Analysis]
    F --> G{All Criteria Met?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Certified Standardized Extract]
    G -->|No| I[Reject Lot / Re-validate]
    B --> B1[Shape Size Colour Odour Taste Texture]
    C --> C1[Stomata Trichomes Vascular Bundles Ergastic Contents]
    D --> D1[Moisture Ash Extractive SI FI]
    E --> E1[Alkaloids Flavonoids Tannins Saponins Glycosides]
    F --> F1[Antioxidant Antimicrobial Cytotoxicity Anti-inflammatory]
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style I fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style G fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
```

### 4.2 Microscopy Sub-Block — Diagnostic Features Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Compound Light Microscope 40x to 400x] --> M2[Stomata Type]
    M1 --> M3[Trichomes]
    M1 --> M4[Vascular Bundles]
    M1 --> M5[Ergastic Cell Contents]
    M2 --> M21[Paracytic]
    M2 --> M22[Diacytic]
    M2 --> M23[Anisocytic]
    M2 --> M24[Anomocytic]
    M3 --> M31[Glandular: Capitate Peltate]
    M3 --> M32[Non Glandular: Unicellular Stellate Hooked]
    M4 --> M41[Collateral]
    M4 --> M42[Bicollateral]
    M4 --> M43[Concentric]
    M5 --> M51[Starch Grains]
    M5 --> M52[Calcium Oxalate Crystals]
    M5 --> M53[Oil Globules]
    M5 --> M54[Aleurone Grains]
    style M1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
```

### 4.3 Chemical Screening Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Plant Extract] --> C1{Class of Metabolite?}
    C1 -->|Alkaloid| C11[Mayer Wagn Dragend Hager]
    C1 -->|Flavonoid| C12[Shinoda Alkaline AlCl3 PbAc]
    C1 -->|Tannin Phenol| C13[FeCl3 Folin Ciocalteu Vanillin]
    C1 -->|Saponin| C14[Foam Haemolysis Liebermann Burchard]
    C1 -->|Glycoside| C15[Borntrager Legal Keller Kiliani]
    C1 -->|Steroid Terpenoid| C16[Liebermann Burchard Salkowski]
    C1 -->|Carbohydrate| C17[Molisch Fehling Benedict Barfoed]
    C1 -->|Protein Amino Acid| C18[Biuret Ninhydrin Xanthoproteic]
    style C0 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#827717
    style C1 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
```

### 4.4 Bioassay Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BA0[Bioassay Decision] --> BA1{Type of Activity?}
    BA1 -->|Antioxidant| BA11[DPPH 517nm]
    BA1 -->|Antioxidant| BA12[ABTS 734nm]
    BA1 -->|Antioxidant| BA13[FRAP 593nm]
    BA1 -->|Antimicrobial| BA21[Agar Well Disc Diffusion]
    BA1 -->|Antimicrobial| BA22[Broth Microdilution MIC]
    BA1 -->|Cytotoxicity| BA31[MTT 570nm]
    BA1 -->|Anti inflammatory| BA41[HRBC Membrane Stabilization 560nm]
    style BA0 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note (Block-Level Adaptation):** Mermaid is being used here to render functional architecture rather than physical microscope drawings, which aligns with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 schematic safeguard. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compiler safety rule.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the following terms in the context of herbal drug analysis:**
**(a) Acid-insoluble ash (b) Foaming index (c) Extractive value.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Acid-Insoluble Ash (AIA):**
Acid-insoluble ash is the residue obtained when the **total ash** of a crude drug is boiled with dilute hydrochloric acid, filtered, washed with hot water, and re-ignited to constant weight. It is expressed as **% w/w** of the air-dried drug. AIA is a **purity parameter** — it quantifies the **siliceous/earthy contamination** (e.g., soil, dust, mineral adulterants) that resists acid digestion. Pharmacopoeial limit is generally < 1–2% for most crude drugs.

**(b) Foaming Index (FI):**
Foaming index is a numerical value used to detect **saponins** in plant materials. It is defined as the reciprocal of the smallest concentration (in mL of decoction, diluted to 100 mL) that produces a **persistent 1 cm foam column** for 10 minutes. The formula is:

$$ FI = \frac{1000}{a} $$

where $a$ is the volume in mL of decoction giving the persistent foam. **FI > 100** indicates significant saponin content.

**(c) Extractive Value:**
The extractive value represents the **amount of soluble constituents** (alkaloids, glycosides, flavonoids, sugars, resins, fixed/volatile oils) extracted by a specific solvent (water, alcohol, ether, chloroform) under defined conditions. It is calculated as:

$$ \%Extractive = \frac{W_{residue}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 $$

It indicates the **chemical richness** and **solvent selectivity** of the drug.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Do **not** confuse "extractive value" with "ash value". A common student error is to write the ash formula here. Memorize the formulae separately.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between:**
**(a) Glandular and non-glandular trichomes (b) Total ash and acid-insoluble ash.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Glandular vs Non-Glandular Trichomes**

| Feature | Glandular Trichomes | Non-Glandular Trichomes |
|---|---|---|
| **Secretory function** | Secrete essential oils, resins, mucilage | Purely mechanical / protective |
| **Structure** | Head + stalk; head bears secretory cells | Single or multi-celled, no head |
| **Examples** | Mint (peltate), Cannabis (capitate) | Cotton (stellate), Nettle (stinging hooked) |
| **Pharmacognostic value** | Source of volatile oils | Diagnostic of plant family |

**(b) Total Ash vs Acid-Insoluble Ash**

| Feature | Total Ash | Acid-Insoluble Ash |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Residue after complete ignition at 450 °C | Residue of total ash insoluble in dilute HCl |
| **What it measures** | Total inorganic content | Silica, sand, soil |
| **Indicates** | Crude drug quality + inherent minerals | Adulteration with earthy matter |
| **Limit (typical)** | < 10% | < 1–2% |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**[(a) 7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **microscopical evaluation of a powdered herbal drug**. Include sectioning, staining procedures, and key diagnostic features used to authenticate leaf, bark, and root drugs.

**[(b) 7 Marks]** Explain the **DPPH radical scavenging assay** in detail. Write the principle, procedure, formula, and interpretation of $IC_{50}$.

##### Model Answer to Q-A(a)

**Microscopical Evaluation of a Powdered Herbal Drug:**

1. **Sample preparation:** A small amount of powdered drug is placed on a glass slide, mixed with a drop of chloral hydrate (clearing agent) or glycerine-water (50:50), and covered with a coverslip. For permanent mounts, Canada balsam is used after dehydration through an alcohol series (30% → 50% → 70% → 90% → absolute).

2. **Sectioning (for unground tissues):** Free-hand sections (10–20 µm) are cut with a razor blade held between the thumb and forefinger. Microtomes (rotary, sledge, cryostat) are used for finer work. The section is gently lifted with a brush and transferred to a watch glass containing water.

3. **Staining procedures:**
   - **Safranin** (1% in 50% ethanol) → stains lignified cells / xylem red.
   - **Fast Green** (0.5% in 95% ethanol) → stains cellulose green.
   - **Phloroglucinol + conc. HCl** → stains lignified walls red (Wiesner reaction).
   - **Iodine (I₂/KI)** → stains starch granules blue-black.
   - **Ruthenium Red** → stains mucilage pink.
   - **Sudan III** → stains fixed oils / cuticle orange-red.

4. **Diagnostic features for authentication:**

   - **Leaf drugs:** Stomatal type (paracytic, diacytic, anisocytic, anomocytic), stomatal index, trichome type, palisade ratio, vein islet number, calcium oxalate crystals.
   - **Bark drugs:** Periderm (cork), medullary rays (uni/multiseriate), phloem fibres, stone cells, starch grains, presence/absence of rhytidome.
   - **Root/rhizome drugs:** Vascular bundle arrangement (radial in roots, conjoint in rhizomes), endodermis, pericycle, xylem vessels (pitted, reticulate, scalariform), starch grain type, inulin spherocrystals (in place of starch in *Inula*).

5. **Examination:** Under compound light microscope at 40×, 100× (oil immersion if needed), and 400×. Photomicrographs are taken for permanent record.

##### Model Answer to Q-A(b)

**DPPH (2,2-Diphenyl-1-Picrylhydrazyl) Radical Scavenging Assay:**

**Principle:**
DPPH is a stable, deep-violet free radical with an absorption maximum at **517 nm**. In the presence of an antioxidant (AH), the DPPH radical is reduced to a colourless (or pale yellow) non-radical form (DPPH-H), and the absorbance decreases.

$$ DPPH^{\bullet} + AH \rightarrow DPPH-H + A^{\bullet} $$

**Reagents:**
- 0.1 mM DPPH in methanol.
- Sample extracts at varying concentrations (e.g., 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 µg/mL).
- Ascorbic acid as standard.

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare 1 mL of each sample concentration.
2. Add 1 mL of 0.1 mM DPPH solution.
3. Vortex, incubate in dark for **30 min** at room temperature.
4. Read absorbance at **517 nm** against methanol blank.
5. Control: 1 mL methanol + 1 mL DPPH.
6. Plot **% scavenging vs. concentration** to determine $IC_{50}$.

**Formula:**

$$ \%Scavenging = \frac{A_{control} - A_{sample}}{A_{control}} \times 100 $$

**$IC_{50}$ Interpretation:**
The $IC_{50}$ (half-maximal inhibitory concentration) is the concentration of the extract required to scavenge 50% of DPPH radicals. It is determined graphically from the dose-response curve. **Lower $IC_{50}$ = stronger antioxidant.** For reference, ascorbic acid typically has $IC_{50}$ in the range 5–15 µg/mL.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part B:** Students often omit the **control** in the procedure. Always state: control = DPPH + methanol; blank = methanol only. Forgetting to subtract the blank costs **2 marks** out of 7.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]**

**[(a) 7 Marks]** Discuss the **morphological evaluation of crude drugs** with suitable examples. Why is it considered the first and most economical step in herbal authentication?

**[(b) 7 Marks]** A 4.0 g powdered sample of a root drug was analysed. After drying at 105 °C, the mass became 3.68 g. A 2.0 g portion of dried powder gave 0.22 g of total ash, of which 0.04 g was acid-insoluble. A 5.0 g portion yielded 0.65 g of alcohol-soluble extract. Calculate the **% moisture, % total ash, % acid-insoluble ash, and % alcohol-soluble extractive**. Comment on the **purity** of the drug using pharmacopoeial standards.

##### Model Answer to Q-B(a)

**Morphological Evaluation of Crude Drugs:**

Morphological (macroscopic) evaluation is the **first and most economical** step in quality control of herbal drugs. It requires **no sophisticated instrument** — only the naked eye, a 10× hand lens, a millimetre scale, and a set of pharmacopoeial monographs. The examiner checks:

1. **Shape and size:** Roots (tap root, adventitious, nodular, conical, cylindrical), leaves (ovate, lanceolate, cordate), barks (curved, flat, channelled), seeds (reniform, globose, ovoid), fruits (capsule, berry, drupe).
2. **Colour:** External and internal (cut-surface). E.g., *Curcuma longa* rhizome — deep orange-yellow internally; *Cinnamomum verum* bark — reddish-brown internally.
3. **Odour:** Aromatic, pungent, characteristic, or odourless. Essential oil-rich drugs (clove, fennel, cumin) have strong odours.
4. **Taste:** Sweet (glycyrrhizin in *Glycyrrhiza glabra*), bitter (quinine from *Cinchona*), mucilaginous (*Plantago* husk), acrid, astringent.
5. **Fracture & texture:** Brittle, fibrous, horny, mealy, waxy, splintery.
6. **Surface characteristics:** Wrinkled, smooth, warty, annulated, lenticellate.

**Why is it the first and most economical step?**
- Requires no chemicals, instruments, or trained microscopist.
- Detects gross adulteration and substitution within minutes.
- Acts as a **triage filter** before spending money on chromatography/HPLC.
- WHO recommends morphology as a **mandatory first step** in herbal authentication.

**Examples:**
- *Zingiber officinale* (ginger) rhizome — laterally compressed, decorticated or undecorticated, fibrous fracture, pungent taste, aromatic odour.
- *Senna alexandrina* leaf — lanceolate, pale green, asymmetric base, characteristic faint odour, mucilaginous bitter taste.
- *Cinnamomum verum* (cinnamon) bark — compound quills, reddish-brown, aromatic odour, warm sweet taste.

##### Model Answer to Q-B(b)

**Given:**
- $W_{sample,\,drying} = 4.0\,g$, $W_{dried} = 3.68\,g$
- $W_{ash\,sample} = 2.0\,g$, $W_{total\,ash} = 0.22\,g$
- $W_{AIA} = 0.04\,g$
- $W_{extractive\,sample} = 5.0\,g$, $W_{extract} = 0.65\,g$

**(i) % Moisture Content**

$$ \%MC = \frac{4.0 - 3.68}{4.0} \times 100 = \frac{0.32}{4.0} \times 100 = 8.0\,\% $$

**(ii) % Total Ash**

$$ \%Ash = \frac{0.22}{2.0} \times 100 = 11.0\,\% $$

**(iii) % Acid-Insoluble Ash**

$$ \%AIA = \frac{0.04}{2.0} \times 100 = 2.0\,\% $$

**(iv) % Alcohol-Soluble Extractive**

$$ \%Extractive = \frac{0.65}{5.0} \times 100 = 13.0\,\% $$

**Comment on Purity:**

- **Moisture (8.0%)** — within pharmacopoeial limit (typically < 10%); drug is reasonably dry and safe from microbial spoilage.
- **Total ash (11.0%)** — **above the typical limit (< 10%)** for most root drugs, indicating either excess inherent mineral content or possible adulteration.
- **Acid-insoluble ash (2.0%)** — borderline; suggests the presence of 2% siliceous/earthy matter.
- **Alcohol-soluble extractive (13.0%)** — within acceptable range, indicating adequate polar phytochemical content.

**Overall verdict:** The drug has acceptable moisture and extractive values, but the **ash profile is suspicious**. Recommend **microscopic and HPTLC re-evaluation** to confirm identity and rule out earthy adulteration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Q-B(b):** Two common errors:
> 1. Using the **dried weight (3.68 g)** in the ash/extractive denominators instead of the **as-taken weight (2.0 g, 5.0 g)**. Read the wording carefully — "a 2.0 g portion of dried powder" still means the *as-taken* weight is 2.0 g.
> 2. Omitting **units (%)** in the final answer. Marks are deducted even when the number is correct.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Mark-Loss Warning — Topic-Wide

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls Across the Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "ash" and "extractive" formulas** — memorize them as separate entities.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract blank** in DPPH/MTT calculations.
> 3. **Writing "taste" in morphological analysis without any safety caveat** — examiners specifically want you to say "taste only after a preliminary safety check; never taste unknown/toxic material."
> 4. **Ignoring units** in numerical answers.
> 5. **Mixing up stomata types** — diacytic (two subsidiary cells) is NOT the same as paracytic (parallel subsidiary cells).
> 6. **Omitting the "why" in bioassays** — KTU awards marks for explaining *why* DPPH is purple (delocalized radical) and *how* antioxidants reduce it (hydrogen atom transfer / single electron transfer).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This Block:**

- **Morphological** = naked-eye / 10× lens; check shape, size, colour, odour, taste, fracture, surface.
- **Microscopical** = compound microscope; check stomata, trichomes, vascular bundles, ergastic contents (starch, CaOx, oil globules, aleurone).
- **Physical** = moisture, total ash, AIA, WSA, extractive, swelling index, foaming index, melting point, RI, optical rotation, viscosity, saponification/acid/iodine values.
- **Chemical** = qualitative screening for alkaloids (Mayer/Wagner/Dragendorff), flavonoids (Shinoda), tannins (FeCl₃/Folin–Ciocalteu), saponins (foam), glycosides (Borntrager/Legal/Keller–Kiliani), steroids (Liebermann–Burchard), carbohydrates (Molisch/Fehling), proteins (Biuret/Ninhydrin).
- **Biological** = DPPH (517 nm), ABTS (734 nm), FRAP, agar diffusion, MIC, MTT (570 nm), HRBC stabilization.
- **Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $\%MC = \frac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$
  - $\%Ash = \frac{W_{ash}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$
  - $\%Extractive = \frac{W_{residue}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$
  - $SI = \frac{V_{swollen}}{V_{dry}}$
  - $FI = \frac{1000}{a}$
  - $\%S = \frac{A_c - A_s}{A_c} \times 100$ (DPPH / ABTS / MTT)
  - $TPC = \frac{A_s \times C_{std} \times V}{A_{std} \times W}$ (mg GAE/g)
  - $TFC = \frac{A_s \times C_{std} \times V}{A_{std} \times W}$ (mg QE/g)
- **Pharmacopoeial limits to remember:** Moisture < 10%, total ash < 10% (drug-specific), AIA < 1–2%, foreign matter < 2%.
- **Standard reagents to remember:** Mayer = K₂[HgI₄]; Wagner = I₂/KI; Dragendorff = KBiI₄; Folin–Ciocalteu = phosphomolybdate / phosphotungstate.
- **Wavelengths:** DPPH = 517 nm; ABTS = 734 nm; MTT = 570 nm; Folin–Ciocalteu = 765 nm.
- **Always run a control and a blank** in every bioassay.
- **Acid-insoluble ash** is a stricter purity indicator than total ash.
- **Swelling index** is specific to mucilaginous drugs (*Plantago* husk, *Sterculia* gum).
- **Foaming index > 100** indicates significant saponin content.
- **Five-tier analytical flow** = morphological → microscopical → physical → chemical → biological; **no tier can be skipped** in a KTU full-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sugars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sugars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Sugars** are the simplest, water-soluble, crystalline members of the carbohydrate family, chemically defined as **polyhydroxy aldehydes (aldoses) or polyhydroxy ketones (ketoses)**, and their derivatives. They constitute the most fundamental class of phytochemicals derived from photosynthesis in plants and are characterized by the general empirical formula **Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ**, from which the broader class name "carbohydrate" is historically derived.

In the strictest chemical sense, a **sugar (or saccharide)** refers to a **monosaccharide** (single sugar unit), while the terms *disaccharide*, *oligosaccharide*, and *polysaccharide* refer to polymers of two, three-to-ten, and many monosaccharide units, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box (Board Exam Favorite):**
> Sugars are optically active, low-molecular-weight carbohydrates possessing a sweet taste, free or potentially free aldehyde (–CHO) or ketone (>C=O) groups, and multiple hydroxyl (–OH) groups attached to a carbon backbone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **LEGO® brick set**:
- **Monosaccharides** are the individual LEGO bricks (glucose, fructose, galactose). You cannot break them into smaller "sugar" pieces by hydrolysis.
- **Disaccharides** are two LEGO bricks snapped together (sucrose = glucose + fructose; lactose = glucose + galactose).
- **Oligosaccharides** are 3–10 bricks linked — short chains.
- **Polysaccharides** are huge LEGO towers (starch, cellulose) — long chains providing structural or storage functions.

When you "eat" starch, your digestive enzymes act like someone **snipping the LEGO bricks apart one-by-one**, ultimately releasing the individual glucose units, which your body oxidizes for energy (≈ **4 kcal/g** or **17 kJ/g**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Constant (KTU must-know):** All digestible carbohydrates (sugars and starches) yield approximately **17 kJ/g (4 kcal/g)**. The Atwater factor for carbohydrates is **4 kcal/g** (rounded), but the precise physiological fuel value is **17 kJ/g**.

### 1.3 Classification Hierarchy (Syllabus Snapshot)

| Class | Degree of Polymerization (DP) | Sweetness Relative to Sucrose | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monosaccharides** | 1 | Variable | Glucose (0.74), Fructose (1.73), Galactose (0.32) |
| **Disaccharides** | 2 | Variable | Sucrose (1.00), Maltose (0.33), Lactose (0.16) |
| **Oligosaccharides** | 3–10 | Low to none | Raffinose, Stachyose |
| **Polysaccharides** | > 10 | None | Starch, Cellulose, Glycogen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sweetness Benchmark:** **Sucrose = 1.00** is the international reference standard. Fructose is the sweetest natural sugar (**1.73 × sucrose**), while lactose is the least sweet (**0.16 × sucrose**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Monosaccharides — The Fundamental Building Blocks

Monosaccharides are the **"glyconic" core** of every sugar in food. They are classified on **two independent axes**:

**Axis 1 — By Functional Group:**
- **Aldoses**: Contain an **aldehyde group (–CHO)** at C-1 → e.g., Glucose, Galactose, Mannose, Ribose.
- **Ketoses**: Contain a **ketone group (>C=O)** at C-2 → e.g., Fructose, Sorbose, Xylulose.

**Axis 2 — By Number of Carbons:**
- **Triose** (3 C), **Tetrose** (4 C), **Pentose** (5 C), **Hexose** (6 C), **Heptose** (7 C).

The hexoses (6 carbons) are the most abundant dietary sugars.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "ose" suffix rule:** A sugar ending in "-ose" with a "gluc-" prefix is an aldohexose (e.g., glucose); with "fruct-" or "ul-" prefixes it is a ketohexose (e.g., fructose).

#### 2.1.1 D- and L-Configuration
The D/L designation refers to the configuration of the **chiral carbon farthest from the carbonyl group**. The vast majority of natural sugars (glucose, fructose, galactose) are in the **D-configuration**. L-sugars are rare in nature and largely non-metabolizable.

#### 2.1.2 Ring (Cyclic) Structure — Haworth Projection
In aqueous solution, pentoses and hexoses exist predominantly (>99%) as **cyclic hemiacetals** (aldoses) or **hemiketals** (ketoses). A 6-membered ring = **pyranose**; a 5-membered ring = **furanose**.

For example, glucose cyclizes between C-1 (carbonyl) and C-5 (hydroxyl) → **glucopyranose**. Fructose cyclizes between C-2 (carbonyl) and C-5 (hydroxyl) → **fructofuranose**; or between C-2 and C-6 → **fructopyranose**.

The C-1 (anomeric carbon) of an aldose can have its –OH below the ring plane (α-anomer) or above the ring plane (β-anomer). In aqueous solution, α and β forms interconvert via the open-chain form → this is **mutarotation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mutarotation Equation (Board Exam Favorite):**
> When a sugar is dissolved in water, the optical rotation changes until it reaches an **equilibrium value**. This change in specific rotation with time = **mutarotation**.

### 2.2 Disaccharides — Glycosidic Bonds

A disaccharide is formed when the **anomeric –OH** of one monosaccharide condenses with an **–OH** of another, releasing **H₂O** (a condensation / dehydration synthesis). The C–O–C linkage is a **glycosidic bond**.

| Disaccharide | Monomers | Glycosidic Linkage | Source / Occurrence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sucrose** | α-D-Glucose + β-D-Fructose | α(1→2)β | Cane sugar, beet sugar, fruits |
| **Maltose** | α-D-Glucose + α-D-Glucose | α(1→4) | Starch hydrolysis (malt, germinating cereals) |
| **Lactose** | β-D-Galactose + α-D-Glucose | β(1→4) | Milk and dairy (≈ 4.8% in cow's milk) |
| **Trehalose** | α-D-Glucose + α-D-Glucose | α(1→1)α | Mushrooms, yeast, insect hemolymph |
| **Cellobiose** | β-D-Glucose + β-D-Glucose | β(1→4) | Cellulose hydrolysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sucrose is a NON-REDUCING sugar** because BOTH anomeric carbons (C-1 of glucose and C-2 of fructose) are engaged in the glycosidic bond → no free –CHO or hemiacetal available.
> **Maltose and Lactose are REDUCING sugars** because they retain a free anomeric carbon.

### 2.3 Polysaccharides — Long-Chain Polymers

#### 2.3.1 Starch (Plant Storage Polysaccharide)
Starch is a mixture of two glucose polymers:
- **Amylose** (≈ 20–30%): Linear chains of α(1→4)-linked D-glucose; forms a **helical coil** (6 glucose units per turn); water-soluble at high temperatures; responsible for the **iodine test (deep blue color)**.
- **Amylopectin** (≈ 70–80%): Highly branched; backbone of α(1→4) linkages with α(1→6) branches every 24–30 glucose units; gives a **red-violet** color with iodine.

#### 2.3.2 Glycogen (Animal Storage)
Glycogen is the animal equivalent of amylopectin but with **more frequent α(1→6) branches** (every 8–12 glucose units), yielding a more compact, dendritic structure. Found in liver (≈ 6%) and muscle (≈ 1%).

#### 2.3.3 Cellulose (Plant Structural)
- Linear polymer of β(1→4)-linked D-glucose.
- Humans **lack the enzyme cellulase** → cellulose is a **dietary fiber** (indigestible).
- The β-linkage forces the chain into a straight, ribbon-like conformation that hydrogen-bonds into **microfibrils** → provides plant structural rigidity.

#### 2.3.4 Other Polysaccharides of Food Importance
- **Inulin**: Fructose polymer (β(2→1) linkages); found in chicory, Jerusalem artichoke, garlic, onion; a **prebiotic fiber**.
- **Pectins**: Galacturonic acid polymers; gel-formers (jams).
- **Chitin**: N-acetylglucosamine polymer; crustacean shells, fungal cell walls.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA REFERENCE (use `\vert` in place of `|` for absolute value, never raw `|` inside markdown tables):**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical formula of carbohydrates | Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ | n = number of carbons |
| 2 | General combustion of glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂ → 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O + **17 kJ/g** | Aerobic respiration |
| 3 | Atwater fuel factor (carbohydrate) | 4 kcal/g ≈ **17 kJ/g** | Standard physiological value |
| 4 | Specific rotation | $[\alpha] = \dfrac{\alpha}{l \cdot c}$ | α = observed rotation (°), l = path length (dm), c = concentration (g/mL); reported at **589 nm (sodium D-line, 20 °C)** |
| 5 | Mutarotation | $\alpha_{t} = \alpha_{\infty} + (\alpha_{0} - \alpha_{\infty}) \, e^{-kt}$ | First-order kinetics; t = time, k = rate constant |
| 6 | Sucrose inversion (1st order) | $\dfrac{d[S]}{dt} = -k[S]$ | k depends on pH, temperature |
| 7 | Relative sweetness | $R = \dfrac{C_{sucrose}}{C_{sugar}}$ (at iso-sweet threshold) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Degree of polymerization (DP) | $DP = \dfrac{\text{Anhydroglucose units}}{\text{Reducing ends}}$ | n+1 for n glycose units |
| 9 | Glycosidic bond water release | $C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} + C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \to C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_{2}O$ | Disaccharide formation |
| 10 | Iodine test (qualitative) | Amylose-I₂ complex: **deep blue** (\(\lambda_{max} \approx 620\) nm) | Amylopectin-I₂: red-violet |
| 11 | Fehling's / Benedict's reduction | $\mathrm{Cu^{2+}} \to \mathrm{Cu^+}$ (red Cu₂O) | Reducing sugars only |
| 12 | Aldose → Aldonic acid | –CHO → –COOH (oxidation) | E.g., Glucose → Gluconic acid |
| 13 | Aldose → Alditol | –CHO → –CH₂OH (reduction) | E.g., Glucose → Sorbitol |
| 14 | Maillard reaction rate | Doubles for every **10 °C** rise | Non-enzymatic browning |
| 15 | Caramelization temperature | Sucrose: ≈ **160 °C**; Glucose: ≈ 160 °C; Fructose: ≈ 110 °C | Dry heat, no amino acid needed |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

1. **Sugar Crystallization** (confectionery): Controlled by **supersaturation ratio** and **temperature cycling**; grain size of sucrose crystals determines texture of fondant and fudge.
2. **Sugar Inversion** (jams, jellies): Acid + heat cleaves sucrose into glucose + fructose → prevents crystallization, enhances sweetness (because fructose is sweeter), gives a smoother texture.
3. **Maillard Browning** (bakery, roasted coffee, seared meat): Reaction between reducing sugars and amino acids/ proteins → melanoidins (brown pigments) and flavor compounds.
4. **Caramelization** (syrups, candies): Thermal degradation of sugars without amino acids → distinctive caramel aroma and color.
5. **Glass Transition (Tg) Control**: Sugars are excellent **plasticizers** in dried foods, lowering Tg → affects crispness of cookies, chips, and freeze-dried products.
6. **Water Activity (a_w) Reduction**: Sugars bind water → lowers a_w → inhibits microbial growth (intermediate-moisture foods like jams, dried fruits).
7. **Fermentation** (yogurt, bread, alcoholic beverages): Yeast metabolizes glucose/fructose to CO₂ + ethanol; lactic acid bacteria convert lactose to lactic acid.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Combustion Energy of Glucose (Calorific Value of Sugars)

The standard enthalpy of combustion of glucose is well-established. We derive the energy per gram released when glucose is oxidized to CO₂ and water, which underlies the **Atwater factor of 4 kcal/g**.

**Step 1: Write the balanced combustion reaction.**

The aerobic metabolism of glucose follows the equation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_{6(s)} + 6\,O_{2(g)} \rightarrow 6\,CO_{2(g)} + 6\,H_2O_{(l)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard enthalpies of formation (ΔH°f).**

Standard values (at 25 °C, 1 atm) are:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ\,[C_6H_{12}O_{6(s)}] = -1274.4\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^\circ\,[O_{2(g)}] = 0\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^\circ\,[CO_{2(g)}] = -393.5\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^\circ\,[H_2O_{(l)}] = -285.8\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3: Apply Hess's Law.**

$$\Delta H_{comb}^\circ = \sum n_p \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum n_r \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta H_{comb}^\circ = \left[6(-393.5) + 6(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-1274.4) + 6(0)\right]$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the products term.**

$$6(-393.5) + 6(-285.8) = -2361.0 + -1714.8 = -4075.8\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the reactants term.**

$$-1274.4 + 0 = -1274.4\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6: Compute the final difference.**

$$\Delta H_{comb}^\circ = -4075.8 - (-1274.4) = -2801.4\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$

The negative sign indicates **energy released (exothermic)**. Therefore, complete combustion of 1 mole of glucose releases **2801.4 kJ**.

**Step 7: Convert to per-gram energy (Atwater factor derivation).**

The molar mass of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is:

$$M = 6(12.01) + 12(1.008) + 6(16.00) = 72.06 + 12.096 + 96.00 = 180.16\ \mathrm{g/mol}$$

Energy per gram:

$$E = \dfrac{2801.4\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}}{180.16\ \mathrm{g/mol}} = 15.55\ \mathrm{kJ/g}$$

**Step 8: Account for physiological inefficiencies.**

The human body is not 100% efficient; digestion losses, urinary nitrogen losses (for proteins), and incomplete oxidation reduce the metabolizable energy. The empirically derived **Atwater factor** for carbohydrates (with adjustments) is:

$$E_{physio} \approx 17\ \mathrm{kJ/g} \approx 4\ \mathrm{kcal/g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 17 kJ/g and not 15.55 kJ/g?** The factor 17 kJ/g (4 kcal/g) is an empirically adjusted Atwater value that accounts for average digestibility (~ 97%) and metabolizable energy of mixed dietary carbohydrates. The bomb-calorimeter value (15.55 kJ/g) is gross energy; Atwater's factor is **physiological** energy.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Relative Sweetness of Fructose

The **relative sweetness (R)** of a sugar is defined as the ratio of the concentration of a reference sucrose solution to the concentration of the test sugar solution that gives the **same perceived sweetness threshold or iso-sweet intensity**.

**Step 1: State the defining equation.**

$$R = \dfrac{C_{sucrose}}{C_{test\ sugar}}$$

where C is the concentration (g per 100 mL water, or % w/v) at the iso-sweet point.

**Step 2: Insert experimental threshold values.**

The threshold concentration of **sucrose** (lowest concentration at which sweetness is detected) is approximately:

$$C_{sucrose} = 0.50\ \mathrm{g/100\ mL}$$

The threshold concentration of **fructose** is approximately:

$$C_{fructose} = 0.29\ \mathrm{g/100\ mL}$$

**Step 3: Compute the relative sweetness.**

$$R_{fructose} = \dfrac{0.50}{0.29} = 1.72 \approx 1.73$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Fructose is **1.73 times sweeter than sucrose**. This is why high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS-55, with 55% fructose) achieves the same sweetness as sucrose with fewer total calories.

### 3.3 Derivation III — First-Order Kinetics of Sucrose Inversion

Sucrose hydrolysis (inversion) in acidic aqueous solution follows **first-order kinetics**:

$$Sucrose + H_2O \xrightarrow{H^+} Glucose + Fructose$$

**Step 1: Write the rate law.**

$$-\dfrac{d[S]}{dt} = k[S]$$

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\int_{[S]_0}^{[S]_t} \dfrac{d[S]}{[S]} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 3: Solve the integral.**

$$\ln[S]_t - \ln[S]_0 = -kt$$

$$\ln\dfrac{[S]_t}{[S]_0} = -kt$$

**Step 4: Exponentiate to obtain the integrated form.**

$$[S]_t = [S]_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

**Step 5: Express in terms of fraction inverted (α).**

The fraction of sucrose inverted at time t is:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{[S]_0 - [S]_t}{[S]_0} = 1 - e^{-kt}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the half-life of inversion.**

At t = t₁/₂, α = 0.5, so:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-k t_{1/2}} \implies e^{-k t_{1/2}} = 0.5$$

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$$

**Step 7: Numerical illustration.**

At pH ≈ 2 and 80 °C, the inversion rate constant of sucrose is approximately k ≈ 0.046 min⁻¹. Therefore:

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.046} \approx 15.1\ \mathrm{min}$$

This means **half the sucrose inverts in about 15 minutes** under these conditions — a critical consideration in jam manufacture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it called "inversion"?** Before hydrolysis, sucrose rotates plane-polarized light to the **right** (dextro, [α] = +66.5°). The equimolar mixture of glucose (+52.7°) and fructose (–92.0°) has a net rotation of:

$$[\alpha]_{mix} = \dfrac{+52.7 + (-92.0)}{2} = -19.65^\circ$$

> The sign of rotation **inverts from positive to negative** → hence "inversion" of sucrose.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Degree of Polymerization (DP) of Polysaccharides

For a linear polysaccharide of **n** anhydroglucose units, the number of **glycosidic bonds** is **n − 1**, and the number of **reducing ends** is **1** (only the terminal anomeric carbon is reducing).

**Step 1: Define DP by the IUPAC relation.**

$$DP = \dfrac{\text{Total anhydroglucose units}}{\text{Reducing ends}}$$

**Step 2: For a linear chain.**

$$DP = \dfrac{n}{1} = n$$

**Step 3: For a branched polysaccharide (e.g., amylopectin).**

If a molecule has **n** anhydroglucose units and **b** branch points, the number of non-reducing ends is **b + 1** (each branch point creates an additional chain end). The DP remains:

$$DP = \dfrac{n}{1} = n$$

since the molecule is still measured from its single reducing end.

**Step 4: Practical measurement.**

DP can be measured experimentally by:

$$DP = \dfrac{M_w}{M_0}$$

where M_w is the weight-average molecular weight of the polymer and M_0 is the molecular weight of the anhydroglucose unit (**162.14 g/mol** for starch).

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For amylose with M_w = 200,000 g/mol:
> $$DP = \dfrac{200{,}000}{162.14} \approx 1233$$
> So this amylose chain contains about **1233 glucose units**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Reducing vs Non-Reducing Sugar Tests

| Test | Reagent | Positive Result | Detects | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Benedict's** | Cu²⁺ + alkali citrate | Red Cu₂O precipitate | All reducing sugars | Non-reducing → negative |
| **Fehling's** | Cu²⁺ + K-Na tartrate | Red Cu₂O precipitate | Reducing sugars | Same as Benedict's |
| **Barfoed's** | Cu²⁺ in mild acid | Red ppt (within 1–2 min) | Monosaccharides (distinguishes from disaccharides) | Time-sensitive |
| **Molisch's** | α-naphthol + H₂SO₄ | Purple-violet ring | **All** carbohydrates | General test |
| **Seliwanoff's** | Resorcinol + HCl | Cherry red (ketose) | **Ketoses** (fructose) | Aldoses give weak color |
| **Bial's** | Orcinol + FeCl₃ | Green (pentose) | **Pentoses** | Specific |
| **Iodine test** | I₂/KI | Blue-black (amylose) | Starch (amylose specifically) | Distinguishes amylose vs amylopectin |
