<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amino Acid (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** An **α-amino acid** is an organic compound that contains both a basic **amino group (–NH₂)** and an acidic **carboxyl group (–COOH)** attached to the same **α-carbon atom**, along with a variable side chain (**R group**) and a hydrogen atom. In phytochemistry and food science, amino acids are the fundamental building blocks (monomers) of proteins and serve as precursors for numerous bioactive secondary metabolites found in plants.

The generalized structure of an α-amino acid is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{General Formula: } &\ \ H_2N-\underset{\underset{H}{|}}{\overset{\overset{R}{|}}{C}}-COOH \\
\text{Where:} &\ \ R = \text{Variable Side Chain} \\
&\ \ \alpha\text{-carbon is chiral (except in Glycine)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — "The Lego Brick of Life"**
> Imagine you are building a long toy train (the **protein**) out of individual Lego bricks (the **amino acids**). Each Lego brick has a **top connector** (the amino group, –NH₂) and a **bottom connector** (the carboxyl group, –COOH). These connectors click together to form a long chain. What makes each brick unique and gives the train its color, shape, and function is the **sticker on the side** — this is the **R group (side chain)**. Twenty different stickers (20 standard amino acids) create infinite combinations, just like 20 amino acids create the vast diversity of proteins in food and living systems.

## 1.3 Key Structural Highlights

The **α-carbon** in every standard amino acid (except proline) is bonded to **four** distinct groups:
- **1.** A hydrogen atom (–H)
- **2.** An amino group (–NH₂)
- **3.** A carboxyl group (–COOH)
- **4.** A variable R-group (side chain)

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Out of the 20 standard α-amino acids encoded by the genetic code, **9 are classified as Essential Amino Acids** in human nutrition because the human body cannot synthesize them — they must be obtained from dietary plant sources (legumes, cereals, nuts) and animal products.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Number of standard (proteinogenic) amino acids | **20** |
| Number of essential amino acids (adults) | **9** |
| pH of intracellular fluid (typical) | **~7.2 – 7.4** |
| Isoelectric point (neutral amino acids) | **~5.5 – 6.5** |
| Average molecular weight of an amino acid residue | **~110 Da** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Amino Acids (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Amino acids can be classified based on **four** major criteria, each important for the KTU exam.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on R-Group Polarity (Most Frequently Asked)

1. **Non-polar (Hydrophobic) R-groups:** Glycine, Alanine, Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine, Proline, Phenylalanine, Tryptophan, Methionine.
2. **Polar Uncharged R-groups:** Serine, Threonine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Asparagine, Glutamine.
3. **Positively Charged (Basic) R-groups:** Lysine, Arginine, Histidine.
4. **Negatively Charged (Acidic) R-groups:** Aspartate (Asp), Glutamate (Glu).

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Nutritional Requirement

| Category | Definition | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Essential** | Cannot be synthesized by the body | Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys, His |
| **Non-essential** | Synthesized by the body | Gly, Ala, Ser, Cys, Tyr, Asp, Glu, Asn, Gln, Pro, Arg* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Essential Amino Acids:** *"PVT TIM HALL"*
> **P**he, **V**al, **T**hr, **T**rp, **I**le, **M**et, **H**is, **A**rg*, **L**eu, **L**ys

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Metabolic Fate

- **Glucogenic:** Converted to glucose via gluconeogenesis (e.g., Ala, Asp, Glu).
- **Ketogenic:** Converted to ketone bodies (e.g., Leu, Lys — exclusively ketogenic).
- **Both Glucogenic and Ketogenic:** Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Thr.

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on Structure of R-Group

- **Aliphatic:** Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile
- **Aromatic:** Phe, Tyr, Trp
- **Heterocyclic:** Pro, His, Trp
- **Sulfur-containing:** Cys, Met
- **Hydroxy-containing:** Ser, Thr, Tyr
- **Acidic (with extra –COOH):** Asp, Glu
- **Basic (with extra –NH₂):** Lys, Arg, His
- **Amide-containing:** Asn, Gln

## 2.2 Acid-Base Behavior — The Zwitterion Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zwitterion Definition:** A **zwitterion** (German: "hybrid ion") is a molecule that contains both a **positive charge** and a **negative charge** but is overall **electrically neutral**. In aqueous solution at physiological pH, amino acids predominantly exist as zwitterions.

The equilibrium in solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_2N-CHR-COOH \ &\xrightleftharpoons[pH \approx 2-3]{+\ H^+}\ ^{+}H_3N-CHR-COOH \\
^{+}\text{(Cation)} &\xrightleftharpoons[pH \approx 7-9]{- H^+}\ ^{+}H_3N-CHR-COO^{-} \\
^{+}\text{(Cation)} &\xrightleftharpoons[pH \approx 9-11]{- H^+}\ H_2N-CHR-COO^{-} \\
^{-}\text{(Zwitterion)} &\xrightleftharpoons[pH \approx 9-11]{- H^+}\ \text{(Anion)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear almost every KTU cycle!**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation** | $pH = pK_a + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$ | Relates pH, pKa, and buffer ratio |
| **Isoelectric Point (Neutral AA)** | $pI = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}$ | For amino acids with ionizable R-group (Acidic/Neutral/Basic) |
| **Isoelectric Point (Acidic AA)** | $pI = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{aR}}{2}$ | For Asp, Glu (where R-group is acidic) |
| **Isoelectric Point (Basic AA)** | $pI = \dfrac{pK_{a2} + pK_{aR}}{2}$ | For Lys, Arg, His (where R-group is basic) |
| **Degree of Dissociation (α)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}] + [\text{A}^-]}$ | Fraction of molecules dissociated |
| **Mole Fraction Ratio** | $\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} = 10^{(pH - pK_a)}$ | From Henderson-Hasselbalch |
| **Absorbance (Beer-Lambert)** | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | Used in ninhydrin colorimetric assay |

> **Note on units:** pKa values are dimensionless; concentrations in **mol/L (M)**; absorbance is dimensionless; $\varepsilon$ in **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**; path length $l$ in **cm**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Food Science Utility

- **Food Industry:** Amino acid profiling determines the **nutritional quality** of plant proteins (e.g., lysine content in wheat — first limiting amino acid).
- **Fortification:** Lysine and threonine are added to cereal-based foods to correct deficiencies.
- **Shelf-life & Flavor:** Amino acids participate in **Maillard Reaction** with reducing sugars, producing flavor and brown pigments in baked/fried foods.
- **Bitterness & Taste:** Hydrophobic amino acids (Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr) contribute to bitterness in protein hydrolysates.
- **Phytochemical Extraction:** Amino acid analysis (HPLC) helps in identifying bioactive peptides from plant sources.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Isoelectric Point (pI) Formula for Neutral Amino Acids

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the pI formula for a neutral amino acid like **Glycine** from first principles.

### Step 1 — Identify the dissociation stages

Glycine undergoes **two** proton-dissociation steps (since it has no ionizable R-group):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (Carboxyl group):} &\ \ ^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH \ \xrightleftharpoons{\ }\ ^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-} + H^{+} \\
\text{Stage 2 (Amino group):} &\ \ ^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-} \ \xrightleftharpoons{\ }\ H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-} + H^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding equilibrium constants are $K_{a1}$ and $K_{a2}$, where **$pK_{a1} \approx 2.34$** and **$pK_{a2} \approx 9.60$** for glycine.

### Step 2 — Write the equilibrium expressions

For **Stage 1** (loss of $H^+$ from –COOH):

$$
K_{a1} = \dfrac{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}][H^{+}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH]}
$$

For **Stage 2** (loss of $H^+$ from –NH₃⁺):

$$
K_{a2} = \dfrac{[H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}][H^{+}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}]}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the pI condition (Net charge = 0)

At the **isoelectric point**, the amino acid carries **zero net charge**, meaning the concentration of cationic form equals the concentration of anionic form:

$$
[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH] = [H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}]
$$

### Step 4 — Derive the pI equation

Take the ratio of the two Ka expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{K_{a2}}{K_{a1}} &= \dfrac{[H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}][H^{+}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}]} \cdot \dfrac{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}][H^{+}]} \cdot \dfrac{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}]} \\[8pt]
\Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{a2}}{K_{a1}} &= \dfrac{[H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the pI condition

Since at pI, $[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH] = [H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}]$, we have:

$$
\dfrac{K_{a2}}{K_{a1}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_{a2} = K_{a1} \cdot \dfrac{[H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH]}
$$

Now multiply the two Ka expressions and substitute the pI condition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} &= \dfrac{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}][H^{+}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH]} \cdot \dfrac{[H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}][H^{+}]}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}]} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{[H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}] \cdot [H^{+}]^{2}}{[^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $[H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}] = [^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH]$ at pI:

$$
K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} = [H^{+}]^{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Take the negative logarithm

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\log(K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2}) &= -2\log[H^{+}] \\[6pt]
pK_{a1} + pK_{a2} &= 2 \cdot pH_{pI} \\[6pt]
pI &= \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Q.E.D.** — The famous **pI formula** is derived! For Glycine: $pI = (2.34 + 9.60)/2 = 5.97$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The pKa values for the amino acid **Alanine** are: $pK_{a1} = 2.34$ (–COOH) and $pK_{a2} = 9.69$ (–NH₃⁺). Calculate the **isoelectric point (pI)** and the **net charge** at pH 7.0.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Alanine is a **neutral** amino acid (no ionizable R-group). Use:

$$
pI = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2} = \dfrac{2.34 + 9.69}{2} = \dfrac{12.03}{2} = 6.015
$$

**Step 2:** At pH 7.0, apply Henderson-Hasselbalch for each group:

For the –COOH group:

$$
pH - pK_{a1} = 7.0 - 2.34 = 4.66
$$

$$
\dfrac{[A^{-}]}{[HA]} = 10^{4.66} \approx 45{,}709
$$

So the carboxyl group is **>99.99% deprotonated** → –COO⁻ (charge = **−1**)

For the –NH₃⁺ group:

$$
pH - pK_{a2} = 7.0 - 9.69 = -2.69
$$

$$
\dfrac{[A^{-}]}{[HA]} = 10^{-2.69} \approx 0.00204
$$

So the amino group is **~99.8% protonated** → –NH₃⁺ (charge = **+1**)

**Step 3:** Net charge = $(+1) + (-1) = 0$. Alanine exists as a **zwitterion** at pH 7.0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** At physiological pH (7.4), most neutral amino acids carry a net charge close to **zero** because pH 7.4 is near their pI. This is critical for understanding protein solubility and electrophoretic mobility.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common pKa and pI Values

| Amino Acid | Type | $pK_{a1}$ (–COOH) | $pK_{a2}$ (–NH₃⁺) | $pK_{aR}$ (R-group) | **pI** |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Glycine | Neutral | 2.34 | 9.60 | — | **5.97** |
| Alanine | Neutral | 2.34 | 9.69 | — | **6.01** |
| Valine | Neutral | 2.32 | 9.62 | — | **5.97** |
| Aspartic Acid | Acidic | 1.88 | 9.60 | 3.65 | **2.77** |
| Glutamic Acid | Acidic | 2.19 | 9.67 | 4.25 | **3.22** |
| Histidine | Basic | 1.82 | 9.17 | 6.00 | **7.59** |
| Lysine | Basic | 2.18 | 8.95 | 10.53 | **9.74** |
| Arginine | Basic | 2.17 | 9.04 | 12.48 | **10.76** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This table is essential for designing **buffer systems** in food processing, protein purification protocols, and isoelectric focusing techniques used in phytochemical analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalized Structure of an α-Amino Acid

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Alpha Carbon Center] --> B[Amino Group -NH2]
    A --> C[Carboxyl Group -COOH]
    A --> D[Hydrogen Atom -H]
    A --> E[Variable R Group Side Chain]
    E --> F[Determines Identity and Properties]
    B --> G[Basic Functional Group]
    C --> H[Acidic Functional Group]
    D --> I[Always Present]
    F --> J[20 Standard Variants in Nature]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    K[Amino Acids] --> L[Based on R Group Polarity]
    K --> M[Based on Nutrition]
    K --> N[Based on Metabolism]
    K --> O[Based on Structure]

    L --> L1[Non Polar Hydrophobic]
    L --> L2[Polar Uncharged]
    L --> L3[Positively Charged Basic]
    L --> L4[Negatively Charged Acidic]

    M --> M1[Essential 9]
    M --> M2[Non Essential 11]

    N --> N1[Glucogenic]
    N --> N2[Ketogenic]
    N --> N3[Both]

    O --> O1[Aliphatic]
    O --> O2[Aromatic]
    O --> O3[Heterocyclic]
    O --> O4[Sulfur Containing]
```

## 4.3 Acid-Base Behavior and Zwitterion Formation Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Low pH Fully Protonated Cation] -->|Lose H from COOH| Q[Zwitterion Net Zero]
    Q -->|Lose H from NH3+| R[High pH Fully Deprotonated Anion]
    P --> P1[Charge +1]
    Q --> Q1[Charge 0]
    R --> R1[Charge -1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As pH increases from acidic to basic, the amino acid transitions from **cation (+1)** → **zwitterion (0)** → **anion (−1)**. The point where net charge = 0 defines the **isoelectric point (pI)**.

## 4.4 Peptide Bond Formation Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Amino Acid 1] --> T[Condensation Reaction]
    U[Amino Acid 2] --> T
    T --> V[Release of H2O]
    T --> W[Dipeptide]
    W --> X[Polypeptide Chain]
    X --> Y[Protein Tertiary Structure]
    Y --> Z[Bioactive Function in Phytochemicals]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block Architecture Insight:** A **peptide bond** (–CO–NH–) is a covalent amide linkage formed by a **condensation (dehydration) reaction** between the –COOH of one amino acid and the –NH₂ of another, releasing one water molecule. This is the foundational reaction for all plant proteins, including bioactive phytochemical peptides.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]

**Define the term "Zwitterion" with respect to amino acids. Why do amino acids exist predominantly as zwitterions at physiological pH?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **zwitterion** is a dipolar ion carrying both a **positive charge** (on the protonated amino group, –NH₃⁺) and a **negative charge** (on the deprotonated carboxyl group, –COO⁻) on the same molecule, resulting in a **net charge of zero**.

> At physiological pH (~7.4), the pH is well above the pKa of the carboxyl group (~$pK_{a1} \approx 2$), so it exists as –COO⁻. The pH is also well below the pKa of the amino group (~$pK_{a2} \approx 9$), so it remains protonated as –NH₃⁺. Hence, the zwitterionic form is the **predominant species**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]

**List any six essential amino acids and explain why they are called "essential."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Essential amino acids** (cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained from the diet):
> 1. Valine
> 2. Leucine
> 3. Isoleucine
> 4. Lysine
> 5. Threonine
> 6. Tryptophan

> They are called "essential" because the human body **lacks the necessary enzymes** to synthesize their carbon skeletons and therefore they **must be supplied through dietary sources** such as cereals, pulses, nuts, and legumes — making them nutritionally critical in food science.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to use the **correct pKa values** (i.e., using $pK_{a1}, pK_{a2}$ for neutral AAs, but $pK_{a1}, pK_{aR}$ for acidic and $pK_{a2}, pK_{aR}$ for basic AAs) → **Lose 2 marks**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not drawing the **titration curve** when asked → **Lose 3 marks**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the net charge calculation incorrectly at a given pH → **Lose 2 marks**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **essential** vs **non-essential** amino acid examples → **Lose 1 mark**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**a)** Define the isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid. With a neat labeled titration curve, explain the protonation states of **Glycine** as pH is increased from 1 to 12. State the predominant ionic form at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Aspartic acid has the following pKa values: $pK_{a1} = 1.88$, $pK_{a2} = 9.60$, $pK_{aR} = 3.65$. Calculate its **isoelectric point (pI)** and predict the **net charge** at pH 5.0. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **isoelectric point (pI)** is the **pH at which an amino acid (or protein) carries zero net electrical charge** in solution. It is also the pH at which the amino acid does not migrate in an electric field.

**Titration Curve of Glycine (5 marks):**

| pH Range | Predominant Form | Net Charge |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| pH < 2.34 | ${}^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH$ | **+1** (Cation) |
| pH = 5.97 (pI) | ${}^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COO^{-}$ | **0** (Zwitterion) |
| pH > 9.60 | $H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}$ | **−1** (Anion) |

**Stages (describe in answer):**
- **Stage 1 (pH ~1):** Fully protonated cation ${}^{+}H_3N-CH_2-COOH$
- **Buffer Region 1 (pH 2.34):** Carboxyl group loses $H^+$ → zwitterion begins forming
- **Midpoint 1:** pH = $pK_{a1}$ = 2.34 (50% cation, 50% zwitterion)
- **Stage 2 (pH 5.97):** Predominantly zwitterion (pI)
- **Buffer Region 2 (pH 9.60):** Amino group loses $H^+$ → anion begins forming
- **Stage 3 (pH ~12):** Fully deprotonated anion $H_2N-CH_2-COO^{-}$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Identify amino acid type (1 mark)**

Aspartic acid is an **acidic** amino acid — its R-group is ionizable (–CH₂–COOH with $pK_{aR} = 3.65$).

**Step 2: Apply correct pI formula for acidic amino acids (2 marks)**

$$
pI = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{aR}}{2} = \dfrac{1.88 + 3.65}{2} = \dfrac{5.53}{2} = 2.765
$$

**Step 3: Net charge at pH 5.0 (4 marks)**

Apply Henderson-Hasselbalch for each ionizable group:

**Carboxyl group (–COOH, $pK_{a1}$ = 1.88):**

$$
pH - pK_{a1} = 5.0 - 1.88 = 3.12
$$

$$
\dfrac{[\text{COO}^-]}{[\text{COOH}]} = 10^{3.12} \approx 1318
$$

→ Carboxyl group is **deprotonated** (–COO⁻), charge = **−1**

**R-group (–CH₂–COOH, $pK_{aR}$ = 3.65):**

$$
pH - pK_{aR} = 5.0 - 3.65 = 1.35
$$

$$
\dfrac{[\text{COO}^-]}{[\text{COOH}]} = 10^{1.35} \approx 22.4
$$

→ R-group is **deprotonated** (–COO⁻), charge = **−1**

**Amino group (–NH₃⁺, $pK_{a2}$ = 9.60):**

$$
pH - pK_{a2} = 5.0 - 9.60 = -4.60
$$

$$
\dfrac{[\text{NH}_2]}{[\text{NH}_3^+]} = 10^{-4.60} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

→ Amino group is **fully protonated** (–NH₃⁺), charge = **+1**

**Net Charge Calculation:**

$$
\text{Net Charge} = (+1)_{\text{amino}} + (-1)_{\alpha\text{-carboxyl}} + (-1)_{\text{R-group carboxyl}} = -1
$$

> **Final Answer:** $pI = 2.77$; Net charge at pH 5.0 = **−1**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Alternative Pattern]

**a)** Classify the 20 standard amino acids based on the **polarity of their R-groups**. Give at least two examples for each class. Explain how the R-group determines the **physicochemical properties** of an amino acid. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write short notes on the following reactions of amino acids:
- **(i)** Ninhydrin reaction
- **(ii)** Sanger's reagent (FDNB) reaction
- **(iii)** Maillard reaction with reducing sugars **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Classification (4 marks):**

1. **Non-polar (Hydrophobic) R-groups** — Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Trp, Met
   - *Properties:* Hydrophobic, tend to be buried inside protein cores, low solubility in water.

2. **Polar Uncharged R-groups** — Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln
   - *Properties:* Hydrophilic, can form H-bonds, located on protein surface.

3. **Positively Charged (Basic) R-groups** — Lys, Arg, His
   - *Properties:* Carry +1 charge at physiological pH, basic in nature, pI > 7.

4. **Negatively Charged (Acidic) R-groups** — Asp, Glu
   - *Properties:* Carry −1 charge at physiological pH, acidic in nature, pI < 7.

**R-group determines properties (3 marks):**
- **Solubility:** Polar/charged R-groups increase water solubility.
- **pI Value:** Ionizable R-groups (acidic/basic) shift the pI.
- **Biological Function:** R-groups provide active sites (e.g., Ser–OH in enzyme catalysis).
- **Reactivity:** Functional groups like –SH (Cys) form disulfide bonds; –OH (Ser) participates in phosphorylation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Ninhydrin Reaction (2 marks):**

> Amino acids (especially those with **free α-amino group**) react with **ninhydrin** (2,2-dihydroxyindane-1,3-dione) on heating to produce:
> - **Ruhemann's Purple** (a deep blue/violet color, $\lambda_{max} = 570$ nm)
> - **CO₂ + H₂O + Aldehyde** (as by-products)
>
> This is the **standard colorimetric test** for quantitative estimation of amino acids (Beer-Lambert's law applied with $\varepsilon$ at 570 nm). Proline gives a yellow color instead, due to its secondary amine.

**(ii) Sanger's Reagent (FDNB) Reaction (2 marks):**

> **1-Fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene (FDNB)** reacts with the **free α-amino group** of amino acids/peptides under mild alkaline conditions to form a **yellow DNP-amino acid derivative**. This was developed by **Frederick Sanger** to identify the N-terminal amino acid of a peptide chain. Sanger was awarded the **Nobel Prize (1958)** for this work on insulin structure.

**(iii) Maillard Reaction (3 marks):**

> The **Maillard reaction** is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between the **free amino group of amino acids/proteins** and the **carbonyl group (C=O) of reducing sugars** (e.g., glucose). It occurs during:
> - Baking of bread
> - Roasting of coffee
> - Frying/grilling of meat
>
> **Stages:** Initial condensation → Amadori rearrangement → Maillard intermediates → **Melanoidins** (brown pigments) + **Aromatic flavor compounds** (e.g., pyrazines, furans).
>
> **Significance in Food Science:** While it enhances flavor, excessive Maillard reaction can reduce the **nutritional quality** of food by destroying **essential amino acids like Lysine** (its ε-amino group is highly reactive).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Amino Acids**

- **General Structure:** $\alpha$-carbon bonded to –NH₂, –COOH, –H, and –R.
- **Total Standard Amino Acids:** 20 (proteinogenic); of which **9 are essential** for adults.
- **Glycine is the simplest** and the **only non-chiral** standard amino acid (no asymmetric carbon).
- **Proline** is unique — it has a **secondary amino group** (imino acid).
- **Zwitterion** is the predominant form at physiological pH; net charge = 0 at pI.
- **pI Formulas:**
  - Neutral AA → $pI = (pK_{a1} + pK_{a2})/2$
  - Acidic AA → $pI = (pK_{a1} + pK_{aR})/2$
  - Basic AA → $pI = (pK_{a2} + pK_{aR})/2$
- **Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation:** $pH = pK_a + \log([A^-]/[HA])$ — must apply to **each ionizable group separately**.
- **Ninhydrin test** gives **purple/violet** color (570 nm) for most AAs; **yellow** for proline.
- **Sanger's reagent (FDNB)** identifies the **N-terminal** amino acid of a peptide.
- **Edman degradation** is a modern sequential method to identify amino acids from N-terminus.
- **Peptide bond** is an **amide linkage** (–CO–NH–) formed by **condensation** with loss of H₂O.
- **Maillard reaction** is critical in food processing — it produces flavor but reduces lysine availability.
- **Optical Activity:** All standard AAs (except Glycine) are **chiral** and exhibit optical isomerism (L-form is biologically predominant).
- **Classification by R-group** has 4 main classes: Non-polar, Polar uncharged, Positive (basic), Negative (acidic).
- **Limiting amino acids** in cereals: **Lysine** (in wheat/rice); in legumes: **Methionine** (S-containing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flavonoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flavonoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌿 FLAVONOIDS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flavonoids** are a major class of low-molecular-weight **polyphenolic secondary metabolites** of plants, chemically characterized by a **diphenylpropane (C₆–C₃–C₆) skeleton** consisting of two aromatic rings (**A-ring** and **B-ring**) joined by a **three-carbon heterocyclic pyran ring (C-ring)**. They are ubiquitous in fruits, vegetables, grains, bark, roots, stems, flowers, tea, and wine, and contribute to the color, flavor, and pharmacological activity of plant-derived foods.

The general molecular formula framework can be expressed as:

$$\text{C}_{6}\text{–C}_{3}\text{–C}_{6} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 15 \text{ carbon atoms (C}_6\text{C}_3\text{C}_6\text{)}$$

The **IUPAC base name** of the parent flavonoid skeleton is **2-phenyl-1,4-benzopyrone** (flavone) or **2-phenylchroman** (flavan).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Sandwich"

> [!NOTE]
> 🍔 **The Molecular Sandwich Analogy:**
> Imagine a flavonoid as a **"molecular sandwich"**:
> - The **top bun** 🍞 = the **A-ring** (resorcinol/phloroglucinol-derived aromatic ring)
> - The **bottom bun** 🍞 = the **B-ring** (catechol-derived aromatic ring)
> - The **filling** 🥬 = the **C-ring** (oxygen-containing heterocyclic pyran ring) that holds the two buns together
>
> The **filling can be "squeezed"** (oxidation state changes — flavanones, flavones, flavonols), **"torn open"** (chalcones, where the C-ring opens up), or **"duplicated"** (anthocyanidins, which form colored pigments). The **position and number of hydroxyl groups** on the buns (–OH substitutions) determine the **flavor, color, and bioactivity** of the sandwich!

## 1.3 Why Flavonoids Matter in Food Science

| Property | Significance in Foods |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Color** | Anthocyanidins → red/blue/purple in berries; Chalcones/Aurones → yellow in flowers |
| **Astringency & Bitterness** | Flavan-3-ols (catechins) → tea, cocoa astringency |
| **Antioxidant activity** | Scavenge free radicals; protect lipids from oxidation |
| **UV protection** | Absorb UV-B (280–315 nm); shield plant tissues |
| **Defense phytoalexins** | Antimicrobial action against pathogens |
| **Health-promoting** | Anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic, cardioprotective |

## 1.4 Basic Structural Features

The flavan nucleus has **four key reactive positions**:

- **C-2**: Where the B-ring attaches (stereocenter in flavanones)
- **C-3**: Hydroxylation gives flavonols; B-ring attachment gives isoflavones
- **C-4**: Carbonyl in flavones/flavonols
- **C-5, C-7**: A-ring hydroxyls (common glycosylation sites)

**Numbering convention** (IUPAC):

```
        8    O     2
         \  / \   /
          \/   \ /
     7 ——— 6    3
    /          / \
   5 ——— 4    (B-ring attaches at C-2)
    \    /
     (A-ring)    (C-ring)    (B-ring)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flavonoids (KTU High-Yield Table)

Flavonoids are classified based on:
1. **Oxidation state of the central C-ring**
2. **Position of the B-ring attachment** (C-2 vs. C-3)
3. **Degree of unsaturation and substitution**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Major Subclasses of Flavonoids** — This is the **most frequently asked topic** in KTU exams.

| Subclass | C-ring State | B-ring Position | Key Example | Food Source | Color |
|----------|-------------|----------------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| **Flavone** | Δ²,₃; C=O at C-4 | C-2 | Apigenin, Luteolin | Parsley, celery, chamomile | Pale yellow |
| **Flavonol** | 3-OH; Δ²,₃; C=O at C-4 | C-2 | Quercetin, Kaempferol, Myricetin | Onion, broccoli, tea, apples | Pale yellow |
| **Flavanone** | Saturated C-ring; C=O at C-4 | C-2 | Naringenin, Hesperetin | Citrus fruits (orange, lemon) | Colorless |
| **Isoflavone** | Δ²,₃; C=O at C-4 | C-3 | Genistein, Daidzein | Soybean, legumes | Colorless |
| **Anthocyanidin** | Flavyllium cation (no C=O; C-ring aromatic) | C-2 | Cyanidin, Delphinidin, Pelargonidin | Berries, grapes, red cabbage | Red/blue/purple |
| **Flavan-3-ol (Catechins)** | No C=O; 3-OH; saturated | C-2 | Catechin, Epicatechin, EGCG | Tea, cocoa, grapes | Colorless |
| **Chalcone** | Open C-ring (no heterocycle) | Linear | Butein, Isoliquiritigenin | Tomatoes, licorice | Yellow |
| **Aurone** | 5-membered C-ring; C=O | C-2 | Sulfuretin, Maritimetin | Yellow flowers (snapdragon) | Yellow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick (KTU favourite):** **"F-I-A-F-C-F"** = Flavone, Isoflavone, Anthocyanidin, Flavanone, Chalcone, Flavonol (3-ol variant).

## 2.2 Biosynthesis — The Shikimate + Acetate Pathways

Flavonoid biosynthesis draws from **two convergent pathways**:

**Pathway 1 — Shikimate Pathway (gives B-ring):**
$$\text{Phenylalanine} \xrightarrow{\text{PAL}} \text{Cinnamic acid} \xrightarrow{\text{C4H}} \text{p-Coumaric acid}$$

**Pathway 2 — Acetate/Malonate Pathway (gives A-ring):**
$$3 \text{ Malonyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{CHS}} \text{Tetrahydroxychalcone}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Coupling reaction:** **Chalcone Synthase (CHS)** is the **gateway enzyme** — it condenses **1 coumaroyl-CoA** (from shikimate) with **3 malonyl-CoA** (from acetate) to form **naringenin chalcone** (the universal flavonoid precursor).

### 2.2.1 Stepwise Biosynthesis Flow

1. **Phenylalanine Ammonia-Lyase (PAL)** → deaminates phenylalanine → *trans*-cinnamic acid
2. **Cinnamate-4-Hydroxylase (C4H)** → *trans*-cinnamic acid → *p*-coumaric acid
3. **4-Coumarate:CoA Ligase (4CL)** → activates to **4-coumaroyl-CoA**
4. **Chalcone Synthase (CHS)** → condensation with 3 × malonyl-CoA → **naringenin chalcone** (ring closure)
5. **Chalcone Isomerase (CHI)** → naringenin chalcone → **naringenin** (first flavanone)
6. **Flavanone 3-Hydroxylase (F3H)** → naringenin → **dihydrokaempferol**
7. **Flavonoid 3'-Hydroxylase (F3'H)** / **Flavonoid 3',5'-Hydroxylase (F3'5'H)** → B-ring hydroxylation patterns
8. **Dihydroflavonol 4-Reductase (DFR)** → leucoanthocyanidins
9. **Anthocyanidin Synthase (ANS)** + **UDP-Glucose:Flavonoid 3-O-Glucosyltransferase (UFGT)** → **anthocyanins** (colored)
10. **Flavonol Synthase (FLS)** → flavonols (kaempferol, quercetin)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the most important structural/formula concepts for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| Concept | Structural/Chemical Representation | Significance |
|---------|------------------------------------|--------------|
| **General flavan skeleton** | $\text{C}_{6}\text{–C}_{3}\text{–C}_{6}$ (15 C) | Backbone of all flavonoids |
| **Flavone (apigenin)** | 5,7,4'-trihydroxyflavone | Aglycone in parsley |
| **Flavonol (quercetin)** | 3,5,7,3',4'-pentahydroxyflavonol | Most abundant flavonol in diet |
| **Flavanone (naringenin)** | 5,7,4'-trihydroxyflavanone | Citrus aglycone |
| **Isoflavone (genistein)** | 5,7,4'-trihydroxyisoflavone (B-ring at C-3) | Phytoestrogen |
| **Anthocyanidin (cyanidin)** | 3,5,7,3',4'-pentahydroxyflavyllium | Red/blue pigments |
| **Catechin** | 3,5,7,3',4'-pentahydroxyflavan-3-ol (trans) | Tea polyphenol |
| **B-ring hydroxylation pattern** | OH at 3', 4', 5' positions | Determines antioxidant potency |

| **UV absorption λmax** | Band I: 300–550 nm; Band II: 240–285 nm | Structural identification by UV-Vis |

> [!NOTE]
> **B-ring hydroxylation** at the **3' and 4'** positions (catechol B-ring) is the **single most important determinant of antioxidant activity**. Quercetin (3',4'-diOH) is more potent than kaempferol (4'-OH only).

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Biosynthetic Derivation — From Phenylalanine to Anthocyanin

We will derive the **complete enzymatic conversion** step by step, writing every reaction explicitly.

### Step 1: Phenylalanine → *trans*-Cinnamic Acid (PAL reaction)

$$\text{L-Phenylalanine} \xrightarrow{\text{PAL (PLP cofactor)}} \textit{trans}\text{-Cinnamic acid} + \text{NH}_3$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{PAL catalyzes the non-oxidative deamination of the α-amino group of L-phenylalanine using pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) as a cofactor, releasing ammonia and forming the α,β-unsaturated (E)-cinnamic acid.}$$

### Step 2: *trans*-Cinnamic Acid → *p*-Coumaric Acid (C4H reaction)

$$\textit{trans}\text{-Cinnamic acid} + \text{NADPH} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{C4H (CYP73A)}} \textit{p}\text{-Coumaric acid} + \text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{Cinnamate-4-hydroxylase is a cytochrome P450-dependent monooxygenase that introduces a hydroxyl group at the para position of the aromatic ring, using molecular oxygen and NADPH as the electron donor.}$$

### Step 3: *p*-Coumaric Acid → 4-Coumaroyl-CoA (4CL activation)

$$\textit{p}\text{-Coumaric acid} + \text{CoA-SH} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{4CL}} 4\text{-Coumaroyl-CoA} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{The carboxylic acid group is activated as a thioester using ATP, releasing pyrophosphate. This activation is essential for the subsequent condensation reaction catalyzed by CHS.}$$

### Step 4: Condensation with Malonyl-CoA (CHS reaction — the gateway)

$$4\text{-Coumaroyl-CoA} + 3\,\text{Malonyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{CHS}} \text{Naringenin chalcone} + 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{CoA-SH}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CHS reaction produces one tetraketide intermediate which cyclizes to form the A-ring (from 3 malonyl-CoA units) and attaches the B-ring (from coumaroyl-CoA).** Three decarboxylations (loss of 3 CO₂) occur during the Claisen-type condensation.

### Step 5: Chalcone → Flavanone (CHI reaction)

$$\text{Naringenin chalcone} \xrightarrow{\text{CHI}} \text{Naringenin (flavanone)}$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{Chalcone isomerase catalyzes a stereospecific intramolecular Michael-type cyclization, closing the C-ring and forming the chiral C-2 center. The product is (2S)-naringenin.}$$

### Step 6: Flavanone → Dihydroflavonol (F3H)

$$\text{Naringenin} + \text{2-oxoglutarate} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{F3H}} \text{Dihydrokaempferol} + \text{succinate} + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{F3H is a non-heme Fe}^{2+}\text{/2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase that introduces the 3-OH group.}$$

### Step 7: B-ring Hydroxylation (F3'H, F3'5'H)

$$\text{Dihydrokaempferol} \xrightarrow{\text{F3'H}} \text{Dihydroquercetin (taxifolin)}$$

$$\text{Dihydrokaempferol} \xrightarrow{\text{F3'5'H}} \text{Dihydromyricetin}$$

### Step 8: Dihydroflavonol → Leucoanthocyanidin (DFR)

$$\text{Dihydroquercetin} + \text{NADPH} \xrightarrow{\text{DFR}} \text{Leucocyanidin} + \text{NADP}^+$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{DFR reduces the C-4 carbonyl of dihydroflavonol to give the leucoanthocyanidin (4-hydroxy flavan-3,4-diol).}$$

### Step 9: Leucoanthocyanidin → Anthocyanidin (ANS)

$$\text{Leucocyanidin} + \text{NADPH} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{ANS}} \text{Cyanidin (flavyllium cation)} + \text{NADP}^+ + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{ANS (also called LDOX — leucoanthocyanidin dioxygenase) introduces a C-2–C-3 double bond and converts the 4-hydroxy group to a C=O, generating the colored anthocyanidin.}$$

### Step 10: Glycosylation (UFGT — final activation step)

$$\text{Cyanidin} + \text{UDP-glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{UFGT}} \text{Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (anthocyanin)} + \text{UDP}$$

$$\text{Logic: } \text{Glycosylation at C-3 stabilizes the flavyllium cation, increases water solubility, and enables vacuolar sequestration.}$$

### Complete Linearized Pathway

$$\text{Phe} \rightarrow \text{Cinnamic acid} \rightarrow \textit{p}\text{-Coumaric acid} \rightarrow \text{Coumaroyl-CoA} \rightarrow \text{Chalcone} \rightarrow \text{Flavanone} \rightarrow \text{Dihydroflavonol} \rightarrow \text{Leucoanthocyanidin} \rightarrow \text{Anthocyanidin} \rightarrow \text{Anthocyanin}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Antioxidant Activity Determinants

| Structural Feature | Effect on Antioxidant Activity | KTU Exam Tip |
|-------------------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| **B-ring catechol (3',4'-diOH)** | ↑↑↑ Highest activity (e.g., quercetin) | **Most important** determinant |
| **C-ring 2,3-double bond** | ↑↑ Conjugation extends to B-ring | Compare flavan-3-ol vs. flavonol |
| **C-ring 3-OH** | ↑↑ Stabilizes radical at C-3 | Flavonols > flavones |
| **A-ring 5,7-diOH** | ↑ Moderate contribution | Common in flavonols |
| **Glycosylation at C-3** | ↓ Decreases activity (steric hindrance) | Aglycones > glycosides |
| **O-Methylation (–OCH₃)** | ↓↓ Decreases free –OH | Methylated forms are weaker |
| **Polymerization (tannins)** | Variable | Procyanidins: protein binding > antioxidant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative antioxidant ranking (in vitro DPPH assay):**
> Quercetin > Myricetin > Luteolin > Catechin > Kaempferol > Apigenin > Naringenin

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define flavonoids. Mention their general structural feature and any two biological functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Flavonoids are a large group of naturally occurring polyphenolic compounds found abundantly in fruits, vegetables, grains, and beverages, characterized by a C₆–C₃–C₆ skeleton.
2. **Structural feature (1 mark):** They consist of two aromatic rings (A and B) linked by a three-carbon heterocyclic oxygen-containing pyran ring (C-ring), with the B-ring attached at C-2 (or C-3 in isoflavones).
3. **Biological functions (1 mark — any two):**
   - Potent **antioxidants** that scavenge free radicals (e.g., quercetin)
   - **UV-absorbing** pigments that protect plant tissues
   - **Defense compounds** (phytoalexins) against microbial pathogens
   - Contribute to **food color** (anthocyanins) and **astringency** (catechins)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between flavones and flavonols with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Flavone | Flavonol |
|---------|---------|----------|
| C-ring structure | C=O at C-4; **no** 3-OH; Δ²,³ double bond | C=O at C-4; **3-OH** present; Δ²,³ double bond |
| Example | **Apigenin, Luteolin** | **Quercetin, Kaempferol** |
| Food source | Parsley, celery, chamomile | Onion, broccoli, tea, apples |
| Color | Pale yellow (less common in diet) | Pale yellow (more widespread) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key one-line difference:** Flavonols = Flavones + **3-OH group on the C-ring.**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Classify flavonoids with suitable examples and their dietary sources. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the biosynthetic pathway of flavonoids starting from phenylalanine, highlighting the role of key enzymes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Classification of Flavonoids (7 marks)

Flavonoids are classified into **seven major subclasses** based on the oxidation state of the C-ring, position of the B-ring, and degree of substitution:

1. **Flavones** — Apigenin (parsley), Luteolin (celery). C-ring: C=O at C-4, Δ²,³ double bond, no 3-OH.
2. **Flavonols** — Quercetin (onion, apple), Kaempferol (broccoli), Myricetin (berries). C-ring: 3-OH present in addition to flavone structure.
3. **Flavanones** — Naringenin (grapefruit), Hesperetin (orange). C-ring: saturated (no Δ²,³), C=O at C-4; predominantly in citrus.
4. **Isoflavones** — Genistein, Daidzein (soybean, legumes). B-ring attached at C-3 instead of C-2; exhibit phytoestrogenic activity.
5. **Anthocyanidins** — Cyanidin, Delphinidin, Pelargonidin (berries, red grapes). Flavyllium cation; responsible for red/blue/purple colors.
6. **Flavan-3-ols (Catechins)** — Catechin, Epicatechin, EGCG (tea, cocoa, grapes). C-ring: saturated, 3-OH, no C=O; polymeric forms = proanthocyanidins (tannins).
7. **Chalcones & Aurones** — Chalcones have **open** C-ring (Butein); Aurones have 5-membered C-ring (Sulfuretin). Both are yellow pigments.

**Minor flavonoids:** Biflavonoids, prenylated flavonoids, methylated flavonoids (e.g., isorhamnetin).

**Dietary significance table (to be drawn in exam):**
- **Citrus** → Flavanones
- **Berries/Grapes** → Anthocyanidins
- **Onion/Apple** → Flavonols
- **Soy** → Isoflavones
- **Tea/Cocoa** → Flavan-3-ols

### Part (b) — Biosynthesis of Flavonoids (7 marks)

The biosynthetic pathway involves **two convergent metabolic routes:**

**(1) Shikimate pathway** (provides the B-ring precursor — phenylalanine)

**(2) Acetate/malonate pathway** (provides the A-ring precursor — malonyl-CoA)

**Key steps:**

1. **PAL (Phenylalanine Ammonia-Lyase):** Phenylalanine → *trans*-cinnamic acid + NH₃ (gateway enzyme between primary and secondary metabolism).

2. **C4H (Cinnamate-4-Hydroxylase):** Cinnamic acid → *p*-coumaric acid (P450-dependent hydroxylation).

3. **4CL (4-Coumarate:CoA Ligase):** *p*-Coumaric acid + CoA + ATP → 4-coumaroyl-CoA + AMP + PPᵢ (thioester activation).

4. **CHS (Chalcone Synthase):** 4-Coumaroyl-CoA + 3 Malonyl-CoA → **Naringenin chalcone** + 3 CO₂ + 4 CoA (the **gateway enzyme** of flavonoid biosynthesis; condenses shikimate-derived C₉ unit with three acetate-derived C₂ units).

5. **CHI (Chalcone Isomerase):** Naringenin chalcone → Naringenin (first flavanone; stereospecific cyclization).

6. **F3H (Flavanone 3-Hydroxylase):** Naringenin → Dihydrokaempferol (3-OH introduction).

7. **F3'H and F3'5'H:** B-ring hydroxylation → Dihydroquercetin / Dihydromyricetin.

8. **DFR (Dihydroflavonol 4-Reductase):** Dihydroquercetin → Leucocyanidin (C-4 carbonyl reduction).

9. **ANS / LDOX (Anthocyanidin Synthase):** Leucocyanidin → **Cyanidin** (colored anthocyanidin; introduces Δ²,³ and C=O).

10. **UFGT (UDP-Glucose:Flavonoid 3-O-Glucosyltransferase):** Cyanidin → **Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside** (stable anthocyanin pigment).

**Branching points:**
- FLS (Flavonol Synthase) diverts dihydroflavonols → flavonols (quercetin, kaempferol)
- LAR (Leucoanthocyanidin Reductase) diverts leucoanthocyanidins → catechins

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram to draw in exam:** Linear pathway flow chart showing all 10 enzymes, substrate/product pairs, and the CHS step labeled as "GATEWAY."

---

### 🅱️ Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the structure-activity relationship of flavonoids as antioxidants, citing specific structural features that enhance antioxidant potency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on anthocyanins — their chemistry, color expression, pH-dependent stability, and food applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Structure-Activity Relationship (SAR) of Flavonoids as Antioxidants (7 marks)

Flavonoids neutralize free radicals (R•, ROO•) primarily through **hydrogen atom transfer (HAT)** and **single electron transfer (SET)** mechanisms:

$$\text{Flav-OH} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Flav-O}^{\bullet} + \text{RH}$$

**Key structural determinants of antioxidant activity:**

1. **B-ring catechol (3',4'-diOH) group** — **Most critical feature.** Allows formation of an **ortho-semiquinone radical** stabilized by intramolecular H-bonding. Example: **Quercetin > Kaempferol** (which lacks 3'-OH).

2. **C-ring 2,3-double bond conjugated with C-4 carbonyl** — Extends π-conjugation into the B-ring, delocalizing the resulting radical. Flavonols > flavanones.

3. **3-OH group on C-ring** — Donates a third H-atom; provides **optimal geometry for radical scavenging** and metal chelation. Example: Quercetin (3-OH) > Luteolin (no 3-OH).

4. **5-OH and 7-OH on A-ring** — Contribute moderate H-donating capacity and participate in **iron chelation** (forms Fe³⁺–flavonoid complex at 3-OH and 5-OH; 3-OH, 4-C=O; or 3'-OH, 4'-OH).

5. **Glycosylation decreases activity** — Bulky sugar moieties at C-3 (e.g., rutin = quercetin-3-O-rutinoside) **sterically hinder** the radical target. Aglycones are more potent.

6. **O-Methylation decreases activity** — Removal of free –OH groups (e.g., isorhamnetin from quercetin) reduces H-donating capacity.

**Antioxidant ranking (in vitro DPPH IC₅₀, lower = better):**
$$\text{Quercetin} < \text{Myricetin} < \text{Luteolin} < \text{Catechin} < \text{Kaempferol} < \text{Apigenin} < \text{Naringenin}$$

**Real-world food implications:**
- Onion (rich in quercetin) → strong lipid peroxidation inhibitor
- Citrus flavanones → moderate antioxidants but excellent metal chelators
- Anthocyanins → combine antioxidant + UV-absorbing properties

### Part (b) — Anthocyanins: Chemistry, Color, pH Stability, Applications (7 marks)

**1. Chemistry:**
Anthocyanins are **glycosylated anthocyanidins**. The aglycone (anthocyanidin) is a **flavyllium cation (2-phenyl-1-benzopyrylium)**, with substitution patterns at C-3, C-5, C-7 (A-ring) and C-3', C-4', C-5' (B-ring). Glycosylation occurs primarily at C-3 (e.g., cyanidin-3-O-glucoside).

**2. Color expression:**
- The **flavyllium cation** (AH⁺) is **red** in acidic pH
- Upon deprotonation → **carbinol pseudo-base** (colorless) at neutral pH
- Further deprotonation → **quinonoidal base** (blue/purple) at alkaline pH
- The B-ring hydroxylation pattern determines hue: **Pelargonidin (1 OH) = orange-red; Cyanidin (2 OH) = red/magenta; Delphinidin (3 OH) = blue/violet**

**3. pH-dependent stability (4 structural forms):**

| pH Range | Predominant Form | Color |
|----------|-----------------|-------|
| < 3 (strongly acidic) | Flavyllium cation (AH⁺) | **Red** |
| 4–5 (mildly acidic) | Carbinol pseudo-base (B) | Colorless/pale |
| 6–7 (neutral) | Quinonoidal base (A) | Purple/blue |
| > 7 (alkaline) | Chalcone (open ring) | Yellow/brown (irreversible degradation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chalcone formation at pH > 7 is IRREVERSIBLE** and leads to loss of pigmentation — a critical limitation in fortifying neutral-pH foods with anthocyanins.

**4. Stabilization strategies:**
- **Co-pigmentation** with flavonoids, alkaloids, or metal ions (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺)
- **Acylation** with aromatic acids (e.g., *p*-coumaroyl) — intramolecular stacking
- **Encapsulation** in liposomes, cyclodextrins, or maltodextrins
- **Crosslinking with proteins** to form stable complexes

**5. Food applications:**
- **Natural red/blue colorants** in beverages, confectionery, dairy (replacing Red 40, Brilliant Blue)
- **pH indicators** in smart packaging
- **Functional ingredients** — anthocyanin-rich extracts (berry anthocyanins) added to yogurts, juices
- **Wine color stabilization** — malvidin-3-O-glucoside and its acylated derivatives
- **Nutraceutical capsules** — high-ORAC berry extracts

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing anthocyanidins (aglycones) with anthocyanins (glycosides)** — both terms appear in questions; write clearly which one you mean.
> 2. **Forgetting CHS is the gateway enzyme** — if asked "name the first committed step of flavonoid biosynthesis," do NOT say PAL (that is the gateway to phenylpropanoid metabolism). The **first committed enzyme of flavonoid biosynthesis** is **CHS**.
> 3. **Mixing up isoflavone B-ring position** — In isoflavones, the B-ring is at **C-3** (not C-2). Drawing it at C-2 loses full marks.
> 4. **Neglecting pH-dependence of anthocyanin color** — KTU questions on anthocyanins ALWAYS test pH stability. Draw the **four structural forms** in your answer.
> 5. **Spelling:** "flavanone" (not "flavonone"), "flavonol" (not "flavonol"), "anthocyanidin" (not "anthocyanadin").
> 6. **Omitting the difference between aglycones and glycosides** — always clarify whether the example is the aglycone (e.g., quercetin) or the glycoside (e.g., rutin = quercetin-3-rutinoside).

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary for last-minute revision before KTU ESE:**

- ✅ Flavonoids = C₆–C₃–C₆ polyphenolic secondary metabolites
- ✅ Two aromatic rings (A & B) + one heterocyclic C-ring (pyran)
- ✅ **Seven major subclasses:** Flavone, Flavonol, Flavanone, Isoflavone, Anthocyanidin, Flavan-3-ol, Chalcone
- ✅ B-ring position: **C-2** (most) or **C-3** (isoflavones only)
- ✅ Key enzymes in pathway (in order): **PAL → C4H → 4CL → CHS → CHI → F3H → F3'H → DFR → ANS → UFGT**
- ✅ **CHS = gateway enzyme** of flavonoid biosynthesis
- ✅ **PAL = gateway to phenylpropanoid metabolism** (not flavonoid-specific)
- ✅ **B-ring 3',4'-catechol** = strongest antioxidant determinant
- ✅ Quercetin = 3,5,7,3',4'-pentahydroxyflavonol = most abundant dietary flavonol
- ✅ Anthocyanidins = flavyllium cations; **color depends on pH**: red (acid) → colorless (neutral) → blue (alkaline) → yellow (irreversible chalcone)
- ✅ Anthocyanin = glycosylated anthocyanidin (C-3 glycoside, e.g., cyanidin-3-O-glucoside)
- ✅ Isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) = **phytoestrogens**; mainly in soybean
- ✅ Flavan-3-ols (catechins, EGCG) = astringent, colorless, in tea and cocoa
- ✅ Flavanones (naringenin, hesperetin) = predominantly in **citrus**
- ✅ Proanthocyanidins = polymeric flavan-3-ols = **tannins** (responsible for astringency in wine, beer, tea)
- ✅ Glycosylation ↓ antioxidant activity (aglycone > glycoside)
- ✅ O-Methylation ↓ free –OH → ↓ antioxidant activity
- ✅ C-ring 2,3-double bond + 3-OH + 4-C=O = optimal conjugation for antioxidant
- ✅ **Isoflavone B-ring at C-3** is the diagnostic structural feature
- ✅ **Chalcones have an open C-ring** (linear); aurones have a **5-membered C-ring**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anthocyanins and Polyphenolics - Flavonoid Biosynthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Anthocyanins and Polyphenolics - Flavonoid Biosynthesis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Anthocyanins** (from Greek *anthos* = flower + *kyanos* = blue) are water-soluble vacuolar pigments belonging to the **flavonoid** class of secondary plant metabolites (polyphenolics). Chemically, they are **glycosylated polyhydroxy derivatives of 2-phenylbenzopyrylium (flavylium) cation**, responsible for the red, purple, and blue colors observed in fruits, vegetables, flowers, and grains. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Flavonoid biosynthesis refers to the metabolic pathway (the *phenylpropanoid / flavonoid branch*) by which plants convert the aromatic amino acid **L-phenylalanine** (and in some legumes **L-tyrosine**) into the three-ring C₆–C₃–C₆ flavonoid skeleton, with subsequent branch-point modifications leading to flavonols, anthocyanins, proanthocyanidins, flavones, and isoflavones. ## 1.2 The Universal Flavonoid Skeleton — C₆–C₃–C₆ Every flavonoid is built on three rings: | Ring | Carbon Atoms | Origin | Common Name | |------|--------------|--------|-------------| | **A-ring** | C₆ (6 carbons) | Derived via **malonyl-CoA** condensation (3 acetate units) | Aromatic A-ring | | **C-ring** | C₃ (3 carbons) | Derived from **p-coumaroyl-CoA** | Heterocyclic pyran | | **B-ring** | C₆ (6 carbons) | Derived from **p-coumaroyl-CoA** | Aromatic B-ring | $$ \text{C}_6\text{–C}_3\text{–C}_6 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Ring A (C6) – Ring C (C3) – Ring B (C6)} $$ ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Three-Story Building of Flavonoids" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy for First-Time Learners:** > Imagine flavonoid biosynthesis as a **factory assembly line** with three "floors": > - **Ground Floor (General Phenylpropanoid):** Receives the raw material — the amino acid *phenylalanine* from the warehouse (primary metabolism). It does basic carpentry, producing standard wooden planks called *p-coumaroyl-CoA*. > - **First Floor (Core Flavonoid):** The planks are joined with metal trusses (from *malonyl-CoA*) to form a **three-room house** (the C₆–C₃–C₆ skeleton) called *naringenin chalcone → naringenin*. This is the central hub. > - **Second Floor (Branches):** From the central house, multiple **side wings** are constructed — flavonols (yellow), anthocyanins (red/blue/purple), proanthocyanidins (tannins), flavones, and isoflavones. Each wing is built by a different "specialist contractor" (branch-point enzyme). Thus, **anthocyanins are one of the "side wings"** of this factory, painted red/blue/purple, decorated with sugar molecules (glycosylation) and other groups to lock in color. ## 1.4 Classification of Flavonoids (Position of Anthocyanins) | Sub-class | C-ring Oxidation State | Color in Plants | Dietary Examples | |-----------|------------------------|-----------------|------------------| | Anthocyanidins / Anthocyanins | Oxidation state +1 (flavylium) | **Red, Purple, Blue** | Blueberry, black grape, red cabbage | | Flavonols | Oxidation state +3 | Pale yellow / colorless (UV-absorbing) | Onion, kale, tea | | Flavan-3-ols | Oxidation state +2 | Colorless | Green tea, cocoa | | Flavanones | Oxidation state +2 | Colorless | Citrus | | Isoflavones | Oxidation state +2 (B-ring at C-3) | Colorless | Soybean | | Proanthocyanidins (Condensed tannins) | Polymer of flavan-3-ols | Brown / astringent | Grape seed, cocoa | | Aurones | Oxidation state +2 (with C-ring exocyclic) | Yellow | Snapdragon | | Chalcones / Dihydrochalcones | Open C-ring | Yellow | Apple, tomato | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Among these, **anthocyanins** are the only class that produces the **vivid red-to-blue color range** in the visible spectrum (λ_max ≈ **465–560 nm**), making them the principal **pigment class** in red/purple fruits. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Flavonoid Biosynthetic Pathway — Step-by-Step Logic The pathway is conventionally divided into **three stages**: ### **Stage 1: The General Phenylpropanoid Pathway (up to p-Coumaroyl-CoA)** This stage converts the aromatic amino acid phenylalanine into the central activated precursor *p*-coumaroyl-CoA. **Step 1 — Phenylalanine Ammonia-Lyase (PAL)** - Substrate: **L-Phenylalanine** - Product: ***trans*-Cinnamic acid** + NH₃ - Reaction: Non-oxidative deamination. **Step 2 — Cinnamate-4-Hydroxylase (C4H)** - Substrate: *trans*-Cinnamic acid - Product: ***p*-Coumaric acid** (4-hydroxycinnamic acid) - Reaction: NADPH-dependent hydroxylation at C-4 of the aromatic ring; catalyzed by a **cytochrome P450 monooxygenase (CYP73A)**. **Step 3 — 4-Coumarate:CoA Ligase (4CL)** - Substrate: *p*-Coumaric acid + CoA + ATP - Product: ***p*-Coumaroyl-CoA** (the central activated precursor) - Reaction: Thioester bond formation. > [!NOTE] > **Why is *p*-Coumaroyl-CoA the "branch point"?** It is the activated, high-energy thioester that delivers the B-ring + 3-carbon unit to the flavonoid pathway. In legumes, an alternative enzyme, **tyrosine ammonia-lyase (TAL)**, can bypass PAL+C4H by directly converting L-tyrosine into *p*-coumaric acid. ### **Stage 2: The Core Flavonoid Pathway (Naringenin Chalcone → Naringenin)** **Step 4 — Chalcone Synthase (CHS) — THE GATEKEEPER ENZYME** - Substrate: 1 × *p*-Coumaroyl-CoA + **3 × Malonyl-CoA** - Product: **Naringenin chalcone** (a tetrahydroxychalcone) - Reaction: Sequential Claisen-type condensations decarboxylating 3 malonyl units. **Step 5 — Chalcone Isomerase (CHI)** - Substrate: Naringenin chalcone - Product: **Naringenin** (a flavanone) - Reaction: Stereospecific cyclization forming the heterocyclic C-ring. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Naringenin is the central branch-point metabolite.** From this single molecule, the plant constructs ALL flavonoid classes. Hence, **CHS is the rate-limiting enzyme** of the entire pathway. ### **Stage 3: Branch-Specific Pathways** **Step 6 — Flavanone 3-Hydroxylase (F3H)** - Substrate: Naringenin - Product: **Dihydrokaempferol (DHK)** — a dihydroflavonol - Reaction: 2-oxoglutarate/Fe(II)-dependent dioxygenase (2-ODD). **Step 7 — Dihydroflavonol 4-Reductase (DFR)** - Substrate: Dihydrokaempferol (DHK) / Dihydroquercetin (DHQ) / Dihydromyricetin (DHM) - Product: **Leucoanthocyanidins** (leucopelargonidin, leucocyanidin, leucodelphinidin) - Reaction: NADPH-dependent reduction of the C-4 keto group. **Step 8 — Anthocyanidin Synthase (ANS) / Leucoanthocyanidin Dioxygenase (LDOX)** - Substrate: Leucoanthocyanidin - Product: **Anthocyanidins** (pelargonidin, cyanidin, delphinidin) - Reaction: 2-ODD enzyme; introduces the flavylium double bond at C2-C3. **Step 9 — UDP-Glucose:Flavonoid 3-O-Glucosyltransferase (UFGT / 3GT)** - Substrate: Anthocyanidin + UDP-glucose - Product: **Anthocyanin** (e.g., Cyanidin-3-*O*-glucoside) - Reaction: Glycosylation at the C-3 position, stabilizing the flavylium cation. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas, stoichiometries, and parameters are **high-yield** for KTU board examinations. Memorize the enzymes, substrates, and cofactors. ### A. Core Stoichiometric Equations $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(1) PAL:} \quad & \text{L-Phe} \xrightarrow{\text{PAL}} \text{trans-Cinnamic acid} + \text{NH}_3 \\[4pt] \text{(2) C4H:} \quad & \text{trans-Cinnamic acid} + \text{O}_2 + \text{NADPH} + \text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{C4H}} \text{p-Coumaric acid} + \text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt] \text{(3) 4CL:} \quad & \text{p-Coumaric acid} + \text{CoA} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{4CL}} \text{p-Coumaroyl-CoA} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i \\[4pt] \text{(4) CHS:} \quad & \text{p-Coumaroyl-CoA} + 3 \, \text{Malonyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{CHS}} \text{Naringenin chalcone} + 3 \, \text{CO}_2 + 3 \, \text{CoA} \\[4pt] \text{(5) CHI:} \quad & \text{Naringenin chalcone} \xrightarrow{\text{CHI}} \text{Naringenin} \\[4pt] \text{(6) F3H:} \quad & \text{Naringenin} + \text{2-OG} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{F3H}} \text{Dihydrokaempferol} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Succinate} \\[4pt] \text{(7) DFR:} \quad & \text{Dihydroflavonol} + \text{NADPH} \xrightarrow{\text{DFR}} \text{Leucoanthocyanidin} + \text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt] \text{(8) ANS:} \quad & \text{Leucoanthocyanidin} + \text{2-OG} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{ANS}} \text{Anthocyanidin} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Succinate} \\[4pt] \text{(9) UFGT:} \quad & \text{Anthocyanidin} + \text{UDP-Glc} \xrightarrow{\text{UFGT}} \text{Anthocyanin} + \text{UDP} \end{aligned} $$ ### B. Key Constants & Spectral Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Molecular formula of Cyanidin-3-glucoside | **C₂₁H₂₁O₁₁** | MW ≈ **449.4 g/mol** | | λ_max (anthocyanins, pH 1) | **465 – 560 nm** | Visible range — red to blue | | pKa of flavylium cation | ≈ **2.5 – 4.0** | Color stable at pH < 4 | | Carbon units from malonyl-CoA | **3 × (C2 + CO2 release)** | Generates 6 carbons for A-ring | | Total ATP equivalents to 1 anthocyanin | ≈ **9 ATP** (incl. PAL, 4CL, glycosylation) | Approximate metabolic cost | | Optimal vacuolar pH for color | ≈ **3.5 – 5.0** | Affects co-pigmentation | ### C. Regulatory TFs (Transcription Factors) | Regulator | Family | Role | |-----------|--------|------| | **MYB** | R2R3-MYB | Primary activator (e.g., *AtMYB75/PAP1*) | | **bHLH** | Basic Helix-Loop-Helix | Partner of MYB (e.g., *AtTT8*) | | **WD40** | β-transducin repeat | Stabilizes MBW complex (e.g., *AtTTG1*) | | **MBW complex** | MYB–bHLH–WD40 | Master switch activating late biosynthetic genes | ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Pathway - **Food Color Industry:** Recombinant *E. coli* strains carrying **4CL, CHS, CHI, F3H, DFR, ANS** genes have been engineered to overproduce anthocyanins (e.g., **Delphinol®** from blackcurrant as nutraceutical). - **Plant Breeding:** CRISPR-editing of regulatory *MYB* loci yields anthocyanin-enriched "purple" tomatoes, rice, and maize (e.g., **ANT1** tomato). - **Stability Engineering:** Researchers mutate *UFGT* / acyltransferase genes to produce acylated anthocyanins stable in neutral pH beverages. - **Nutraceuticals:** Anthocyanins as antioxidants with **ORAC values of ~5,000–15,000 µmol TE/g** (e.g., elderberry, bilberry). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Tracking Carbon Atoms from Phenylalanine to Cyanidin-3-O-Glucoside > **Objective:** Demonstrate the precise carbon origin for each ring of the final anthocyanin, a classic 7-mark derivation question. ### Step 1 — L-Phenylalanine (9 carbons) $$ \text{C}_9 \text{ skeleton of L-Phe: } \;\; \text{C}_6\text{(ring)} - \text{C}_3\text{(chain)} - \text{NH}_2 - \text{COOH} $$ PAL removes the amino group as NH₃, releasing *trans*-cinnamic acid. ### Step 2 — Cinnamic Acid (C₉) $$ \text{C}_9 \text{ skeleton: } \;\; \text{C}_6\text{(A-ring precursor)} - \text{CH=CH-COOH} $$ Carbon count remains **9 carbons**, but the C-2/C-3 of the chain will later become part of the C-ring. ### Step 3 — *p*-Coumaric Acid (C₉, +1 O) Hydroxylation at C-4 of the ring (does not change carbon count). $$ \text{C}_9: \;\; \text{HO-C}_6\text{H}_3 - \text{CH=CH-COOH} $$ ### Step 4 — *p*-Coumaroyl-CoA (C₉) Activation by CoA: carbon count **9 (organic) + CoA (high-energy carrier)**. ### Step 5 — Naringenin Chalcone (C₁₅) CHS adds **3 × Malonyl-CoA** (= 6 carbons, 3 CO₂ released). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Carbons from p-coumaroyl-CoA} & = 9 \\ \text{Carbons from 3 malonyl-CoA} & = 3 \times 2 = 6 \\ \text{Total C in substrate} & = 9 + 6 = 15 \\ \text{CO}_2 \text{ released (3 decarboxylations)} & = 3 \times 1 = 3 \\ \text{Carbons in product (Naringenin chalcone)} & = 15 - 3 = \mathbf{C_{15}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Naringenin (C₁₅) Cyclization by CHI: **no carbon change**, just ring closure forming the C-ring. ### Step 7 — Dihydrokaempferol (C₁₅) F3H adds a 3-OH group: **no carbon change**. ### Step 8 — Leucocyanidin (C₁₅) DFR removes one O (4-keto reduced): **no carbon change**. ### Step 9 — Cyanidin (C₁₅, the anthocyanidin) ANS introduces the flavylium double bond (loss of 2H, gain of +1 oxidation state). **Carbon count remains 15**. ### Step 10 — Cyanidin-3-O-Glucoside (C₂₁, the anthocyanin) UFGT adds **glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** via UDP-Glc. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C in cyanidin} & = 15 \\ \text{C in glucose} & = 6 \\ \text{Total C in cyanidin-3-O-glucoside} & = 15 + 6 = \mathbf{C_{21}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Mass-Balance Equation for Cyanidin-3-O-Glucoside:** > > $$\text{1 L-Phe} \; (C_9) \; + \; 3 \; \text{Malonyl-CoA} \; (6C, \; 3 \, CO_2 \text{ lost}) \; + \; 1 \; \text{UDP-Glc} \; (6C) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cyanidin-3-O-Glucoside} \; (C_{21}) \; + \; 3 \, CO_2$$ > > **Net carbon input:** 9 + 6 + 6 = **21 C** (excluding the 3 CO₂ released). > **Carbon in product:** **21 C** ✓ (verified). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Anthocyanin Color vs. B-ring Substitution > [!NOTE] > The **color of an anthocyanin** is dictated by the **number of hydroxyl groups** and the **degree of methylation** on the **B-ring**. This is a recurring 5-mark question in KTU exams. | Anthocyanidin | B-ring Substitution Pattern | Color in Plants | Common Sources | |---------------|----------------------------|-----------------|----------------| | Pelargonidin (Pg) | 4′-OH | **Orange / Brick red** | Strawberry, radish | | Cyanidin (Cy) | 3′,4′-diOH | **Magenta / Red** | Cherry, raspberry, blackberry | | Peonidin (Pn) | 3′-OCH₃, 4′-OH | **Rose / Pink-red** | Cranberry, plum | | Delphinidin (Dp) | 3′,4′,5′-triOH | **Blue / Purple** | Blueberry, black grape, eggplant | | Petunidin (Pt) | 3′-OCH₃, 4′,5′-diOH | **Violet** | Petunia, red wine | | Malvidin (Mv) | 3′,5′-diOCH₃, 4′-OH | **Deep purple / Mauve** | Red wine (Vitis vinifera) | ### Color Rule (Kite Rule) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Color shift toward BLUE} \;& \Longleftrightarrow \; \text{More OH groups on B-ring} \; (+ \text{methylation of adjacent OH}) \\ \text{Color shift toward RED} \;& \Longleftrightarrow \; \text{Fewer OH groups on B-ring (or B-ring shifted)} \\ \text{λ}_{max} \;& \approx \; 465 \text{ nm (Pg)} < 520 \text{ nm (Cy)} < 540 \text{ nm (Dp)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Stability of Anthocyanins — pH-Dependent Structural Equilibria Anthocyanins in aqueous solution exist in **four structural forms** depending on pH: $$ \text{AH}^+ \; (\text{flavylium cation, red}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{A} \; (\text{quinonoidal base, blue}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{B} \; (\text{carbinol pseudobase, colorless}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C} \; (\text{chalcone, pale yellow}) $$ ### Quantitative Approximation (Henderson-Hasselbalch for Flavylium) For the equilibrium **AH⁺ ⇌ A + H⁺**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \frac{[\text{A}]}{[\text{AH}^+]} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Example:** - Given pKa = **3.5** (typical for cyanidin-3-glucoside), compute the percentage of flavylium cation at **pH 3.0** (typical of fruit pulp). $$ \begin{aligned} \log \frac{[\text{A}]}{[\text{AH}^+]} & = \text{pH} - \text{p}K_a = 3.0 - 3.5 = -0.5 \\ \frac{[\text{A}]}{[\text{AH}^+]} & = 10^{-0.5} \approx 0.316 \\ [\text{AH}^+] & = \frac{1}{1 + 0.316} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{76.0\%} \; (\text{red flavylium dominant}) \\ [\text{A}] & \approx \mathbf{24.0\%} \; (\text{blue quinonoidal}) \end{aligned} $$ - At **pH 5.0** (cytoplasmic/vacuolar after cell damage): $$ \begin{aligned} \log \frac{[\text{A}]}{[\text{AH}^+]} & = 5.0 - 3.5 = 1.5 \\ \frac{[\text{A}]}{[\text{AH}^+]} & = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.6 \\ [\text{AH}^+] & = \frac{1}{1 + 31.6} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{3.07\%} \end{aligned} $$ Thus, at pH 5, only ~3% of the red flavylium cation remains — explaining **why anthocyanins lose color during juice pasteurization / baking**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** To preserve color in anthocyanin-rich food products, formulations must maintain **pH < 3.5**, add **co-pigments** (flavonols, alkaloids), or use **acylated anthocyanins**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Complete Flavonoid Biosynthetic Pathway — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[("L-Phenylalanine (Phe)
Primary Metabolism Precursor")] A1[/"PAL
Phenylalanine Ammonia-Lyase"/] A2[/"C4H

CENTRAL BRANCH POINT"] P6["Dihydrokaempferol DHK"] P7["Leucocyanidin"] P8["Cyanidin Anthocyanidin"] P9["Cyanidin-3-O-Glucoside
ANTHOCYANIN PIGMENT"] B1[/"IFS

MYB + bHLH + WD40"/] ENV[/"Environmental Cues
Light, Sugar, Stress, Cold"/] A0 --> A1 --> P1 P1 --> A2 --> P2 P2 --> A3 --> P3 P3 --> A4 --> P4 P4 --> A5 --> P5 P5 --> A6 --> P6 P6 --> A7 --> P7 P7 --> A8 --> P8 P8 --> A9 --> P9 P5 --> B1 --> P10 P6 --> B2 --> P11 P5 --> B3 --> P12 P5 --> B4 --> P13 ENV --> REG REG -. Activates .-> A9 style A0 fill:#fff5e1,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000 style P3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#e67300,color:#000 style P5 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style P9 fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000 style A4 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style REG fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style ENV fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style P10 fill:#fff0cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000 style P11 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style P12 fill:#e6ccb3,stroke:#663300,color:#000 style P13 fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc9966,color:#000 ``` **Figure 1.** Block-level architecture of the flavonoid biosynthetic pathway showing the general phenylpropanoid (PAL/C4H/4CL), core flavonoid (CHS/CHI), and branch-specific stages (F3H/DFR/ANS/UFGT) leading to anthocyanins, with parallel branches to isoflavones, flavonols, proanthocyanidins, and flavones. Regulatory control by the MBW (MYB–bHLH–WD40) transcription factor complex is shown by dashed activator arrows. ## 4.2 B-ring Hydroxylation Pattern & Anthocyanidin Color Map ```mermaid flowchart LR DHK["Dihydrokaempferol DHK
4'-OH only"] DHQ["Dihydroquercetin DHQ

BRICK RED"] AC["Cyanidin

BLUE PURPLE"] DHK -- "via F3'H" --> DHQ DHQ -- "via F3'5'H" --> DHM DHK --> DFR --> LP --> AP DHQ --> DFR --> LC --> AC DHM --> DFR --> LD --> AD style AP fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style AC fill:#cc0066,stroke:#660033,color:#fff style AD fill:#3333cc,stroke:#000066,color:#fff style DHK fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style DHQ fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style DHM fill:#ffff66,stroke:#999900,color:#000 ``` **Figure 2.** B-ring hydroxylation pattern of anthocyanidins driven by F3′H and F3′5′H cytochromes P450, demonstrating how increasing B-ring hydroxylation shifts color from red → magenta → blue. ## 4.3 Anthocyanin Stability & pH-Dependent Structural Forms ```mermaid flowchart LR AHp["AH+ Flavylium Cation
RED
