- Carotenoids also **physically quench singlet oxygen (¹O₂)** by accepting its excitation energy and dissipating it as heat.
- **Order of antioxidant strength** (in lipid systems): **Astaxanthin > Lutein > β-Carotene > Lycopene > α-Tocopherol** (astaxanthin has both polar ends, allowing it to span cell membranes).

---

**Q.A. (b) Discuss the major food sources, functions, and stability of carotenoids during food processing. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Major Food Sources (2 marks):**

| Carotenoid | Color | Rich Sources |
|---|---|---|
| β-Carotene | Orange | Carrot, sweet potato, pumpkin, mango |
| α-Carotene | Pale orange | Carrot, pumpkin |
| Lycopene | Deep red | Tomato, watermelon, pink guava, papaya |
| Lutein | Yellow | Spinach, kale, broccoli, marigold petals |
| Zeaxanthin | Yellow | Corn, egg yolk, peppers |
| β-Cryptoxanthin | Orange | Papaya, persimmon, oranges |
| Astaxanthin | Pink-red | Salmon, trout, shrimp, microalgae (*Haematococcus pluvialis*) |

**(ii) Functions in Food & Health (2.5 marks):**
- **Colorant (natural pigment)**: Used as a clean-label alternative to synthetic dyes (e.g., β-carotene in margarine, paprika oleoresin in sausages, annatto in cheese).
- **Pro-vitamin A**: β-carotene is the major dietary source of vitamin A, critical for vision, immunity, and epithelial integrity.
- **Antioxidant**: Protects membrane lipids from peroxidation; protects eye macula (lutein/zeaxanthin) from blue light damage.
- **Bioactive**: Epidemiological studies link lycopene intake to **reduced prostate cancer risk**; lutein/zeaxanthin to **reduced age-related macular degeneration (AMD)**.

**(iii) Stability During Processing (2.5 marks):**
- **Heat**: Causes **cis-trans isomerization** (trans → cis), reducing color intensity and provitamin A activity. **Severe heat** causes oxidative cleavage forming **volatile aroma compounds** (β-ionone, damascenone).
- **Light**: Triggers **photo-oxidation** via triplet-state generation.
- **Oxygen**: Most destructive; carotenoids react with **¹O₂** and **peroxyl radicals**, especially in dry powders.
- **pH**: Acidic conditions promote **epoxide formation** (e.g., 5,6-epoxide of β-carotene).
- **Metal ions (Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺)**: Catalyze oxidation.
- **Protective strategies**: Encapsulation (maltodextrin, gum arabic wall materials), antioxidant co-addition (BHA, BHT, ascorbic acid), modified atmosphere packaging (low O₂).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **confuse "carotene" and "carotenoid"** — *carotenoid* is the broader term, *carotene* refers only to hydrocarbons.
> 2. Many students incorrectly state that **lycopene has provitamin A activity** — it does **not** (no β-ionone ring).
> 3. Students frequently **omit the role of cis-isomers** when discussing color loss during processing.
> 4. **Mismatched units** in Beer–Lambert problems (mg/L vs mol/L) is a **favourite deduction point** for examiners.
> 5. Do not write "carotenoids are **carbohydrates**" — they are **terpenoids**, not sugars.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q.B. (a) Explain the biosynthetic pathway of carotenoids in plants, starting from the MEP pathway. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) MEP Pathway (2 marks):**
- Carotenoids are synthesized in the **plastids (chloroplasts/chromoplasts)**.
- The **methylerythritol phosphate (MEP) pathway** converts **pyruvate + glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate** into the C5 building blocks **isopentenyl diphosphate (IPP)** and its isomer **dimethylallyl diphosphate (DMAPP)**.
- This pathway is independent of the mevalonate pathway used for sterols in the cytosol.

**(ii) GGPP Formation (1.5 marks):**
- Sequential head-to-tail condensation of **3 IPP + 1 DMAPP** by **GGPP synthase** produces the **C20 isoprenoid — geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate (GGPP)**.
- Intermediates: DMAPP → GPP (C10) → FPP (C15) → GGPP (C20).

**(iii) Carotenoid-Specific Pathway (3.5 marks):**
- **Phytoene synthase (PSY)** catalyzes the **head-to-head condensation of 2 GGPP** → **phytoene** (C40, colorless, 3 conjugated DB).
- **Phytoene desaturase (PDS)** and **ζ-carotene desaturase (ZDS)** introduce additional double bonds:
  - Phytoene → phytofluene → ζ-carotene → neurosporene → **lycopene** (red, 11 conjugated DB).
- **Lycopene β-cyclase (LCYB)** cyclizes both ends → **β-carotene**.
- **Lycopene ε-cyclase (LCYE)** + LCYB → **α-carotene** (asymmetric).
- **Hydroxylases and epoxidases** convert carotenes into **xanthophylls** (e.g., β-carotene → zeaxanthin → violaxanthin → neoxanthin).

> **Regulatory note:** PSY is the **rate-limiting enzyme** and is the major target for **biofortification** strategies (e.g., Golden Rice, high-lycopene tomato).

---

**Q.B. (b) Describe the methods used for extraction, separation, and quantification of carotenoids from food matrices. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Extraction (2.5 marks):**
- Carotenoids are **lipophilic**, hence extracted using **non-polar organic solvents**: **hexane, acetone, methanol, petroleum ether, chloroform, or tetrahydrofuran (THF)**.
- **Saponification** with KOH/methanol is often performed to **hydrolyze chlorophylls and remove lipids** that would otherwise co-elute.
- **Protection during extraction**: addition of **0.1% BHT or ascorbic acid** to prevent oxidation; extraction under **dim light and N₂ atmosphere** to prevent cis-isomerization.
- Modern green methods: **supercritical CO₂ extraction** (eco-friendly, no solvent residue).

**(ii) Separation (2 marks):**
- **Column chromatography** (open column with MgO, silica, or alumina) for preparative separation.
- **Thin-layer chromatography (TLC)** for qualitative identification (Rf values: lycopene ~ 0.95, β-carotene ~ 0.70, lutein ~ 0.40, chlorophyll ~ 0.20 in petroleum ether:acetone).
- **High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)** with **C18 or C30 reversed-phase columns** is the **gold standard** for analytical separation; **C30 columns** are specifically designed to resolve carotenoid **cis-trans isomers**.
- Detection: **photodiode array (PDA) detector** at 450 nm.

**(iii) Quantification (2.5 marks):**
- **UV-Vis Spectrophotometry** at **λmax = 450 nm** (single wavelength) using the Beer–Lambert Law:
$$c = \frac{A \cdot V \cdot 10^6}{\varepsilon \cdot l \cdot m}$$
where $V$ = extract volume (mL), $m$ = sample mass (g), $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient.

- **HPLC quantification** uses **external standard calibration curves** or **internal standard** (e.g., β-apo-8'-carotenal).
- Results reported as **μg/g fresh weight** or **μg RAE/100 g** (for provitamin A).
- **Modern methods**: **LC-MS/MS** for identification based on molecular mass and fragmentation pattern; **HPLC-DAD-APCI-MS** is widely used.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Students often **forget to include the dilution factor (V)** in their Beer–Lambert calculation, leading to answers off by 10–100×. Always write the **full equation** with all terms.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points (MUST remember for KTU ESE):**

- ⭐ Carotenoids = **C40 tetraterpenoids** = 8 isoprene units.
- ⭐ Built from **two GGPP (C20)** molecules condensing head-to-head → **phytoene** (first carotenoid).
- ⭐ Classification: **Carotenes** (hydrocarbons, e.g., β-carotene, lycopene) vs **Xanthophylls** (oxygenated, e.g., lutein, zeaxanthin, astaxanthin).
- ⭐ **Conjugated double bond system** is the **key structural feature** — governs color, antioxidant capacity, and provitamin A activity.
- ⭐ More conjugated DB → **redder color, stronger antioxidant** (lycopene = 11 DB → red; phytoene = 3 DB → colorless).
- ⭐ **β-Ionone ring + intact polyene chain (≥ 11 DB)** = essential for **provitamin A activity**.
- ⭐ **Lycopene has NO provitamin A activity** (no β-ionone ring).
- ⭐ **Cis-isomers** have lower provitamin A activity and a **hypsochromic shift** (~ 4–10 nm).
- ⭐ **Astaxanthin** is the **strongest natural antioxidant** known (6000× stronger than vitamin C).
- ⭐ **Stability order** (best to worst): Xanthophyll esters > all-trans carotenes > cis-isomers; protected in oily matrices.
- ⭐ **Extraction solvents**: hexane, acetone, methanol, THF; **saponification** removes interfering lipids/chlorophylls.
- ⭐ **Quantification**: Spectrophotometry at **450 nm** using Beer–Lambert Law; **HPLC with C30 column** for isomer separation.
- ⭐ **β-Carotene → 2 retinal** via **β-carotene 15,15'-monooxygenase** in enterocytes.
- ⭐ **Degradation triggers**: heat, light, oxygen, acid, metal ions.
- ⭐ **Biofortification examples**: **Golden Rice (β-carotene)**, **high-lycopene tomato**, **orange-fleshed sweet potato**.
- ⭐ **PSY (phytoene synthase)** is the **rate-limiting enzyme** in carotenoid biosynthesis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seed Oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Seed Oils # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Seed Oils** are lipophilic phytochemical-rich matrices extracted from the seeds (embryonic storage organs) of oil-bearing plants, composed predominantly of triacylglycerols (TAGs, 95–98%), accompanied by minor bioactive constituents such as tocopherols, tocotrienols, phytosterols, phospholipids, squalene, carotenoids, lignans (e.g., secoisolariciresinol diglucoside – SDG), and phenolic compounds. In the context of the **PEFTT417 – Phytochemicals in Food** syllabus, seed oils are categorized as **functional food ingredients** and **nutraceutical carriers** because their non-glyceride fraction contains potent **lipid-soluble phytochemicals** with demonstrated antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, hypocholesterolemic, and chemopreventive activities. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vitamin Capsule Inside a Seed" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition for First-Time Learners:** Imagine a sunflower seed as a tiny "time capsule" wrapped by nature. Inside this capsule, the plant has packed two distinct things: (1) **caloric fuel** in the form of fatty acids (the triacylglycerols that germinate into a new plant), and (2) **protective guardians** — the phytochemicals that prevent the fats from going rancid while the seed lies dormant. These "guardians" (tocopherols, sterols, lignans) are precisely the phytochemicals we are studying in this module. ## 1.3 Why Seed Oils Matter in Food Science | Sector | Application of Seed Oil Phytochemicals | |---|---| | **Functional Foods** | Fortified margarines, omega-3 enriched dairy alternatives | | **Nutraceuticals** | Soft-gel capsules (evening primrose, flaxseed oil) | | **Cosmeceuticals** | Natural tocopherol-based antioxidants in anti-aging creams | | **Pharmaceuticals** | Carrier oils for lipid-soluble drugs | | **Food Preservation** | Natural antioxidant additives replacing synthetic BHA/BHT | ## 1.4 Classification of Seed Oils by Fatty Acid Dominance > [!NOTE] > **Standard Nomenclature:** Seed oils are classified based on the **predominant fatty acid** in their triacylglycerol backbone. This classification is critical because it dictates the oil's nutritional value, oxidative stability, and culinary applications. 1. **Lauric Acid Oils** (C12:0 dominant, saturated) — e.g., Coconut oil 2. **Oleic–Linoleic Acid Oils** (monounsaturated + polyunsaturated balance) — e.g., Sunflower, Safflower, Cottonseed 3. **Linolenic Acid Oils** (omega-3 rich) — e.g., Flaxseed (Linseed), Perilla 4. **Erucic Acid Oils** (C22:1 dominant) — e.g., Rapeseed/Mustard (low-erucic cultivars = Canola) 5. **Conjugated Linoleic Acid (CLA) Oils** — e.g., Pomegranate seed oil, Tung oil 6. **Specialty/Gourmet Oils** — e.g., Evening primrose (γ-linolenic acid), Borage, Blackcurrant # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemical Architecture of Seed Oils ### 2.1.1 Triacylglycerol (TAG) Backbone A triacylglycerol molecule consists of a **glycerol-3-phosphate** scaffold esterified with three fatty acid chains at the sn-1, sn-2, and sn-3 positions. The molecular architecture can be represented as: $$\text{TAG} = \text{Glycerol} + 3 \times \text{Fatty Acid} - 3 \times \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ $$\text{Molecular Formula of Triacylglycerol: } \text{C}_{3}\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR}_1)(\text{OOCR}_2)(\text{OOCR}_3)$$ Where **R₁, R₂, R₃** represent the hydrocarbon chains of three fatty acids. The position-specific distribution of fatty acids (sn-1, sn-2, sn-3) is called the **stereospecific structure** and is determined by enzymatic acylation during seed development. ### 2.1.2 The Glycerol Backbone and Regiochemistry > [!IMPORTANT] > **Regiochemistry Insight:** The **sn-2 (middle) position** in seed oils is preferentially occupied by **unsaturated fatty acids** (e.g., linoleic acid in sunflower oil), while the **sn-1 and sn-3 (terminal) positions** tend to hold **saturated fatty acids**. This non-random distribution is genetically programmed by **lysophosphatidic acid acyltransferases (LPAT)** and has major nutritional implications during digestion by pancreatic lipase. ### 2.1.3 Minor Bioactive Constituents (The Phytochemical Fraction) The 2–5% non-TAG fraction of seed oils contains the "phytochemical treasure" of interest in this module: | Bioactive Class | Example Compounds | Oil Source | Functional Role | |---|---|---|---| | **Tocopherols** | α-, β-, γ-, δ-tocopherol | Sunflower, Wheatgerm, Safflower | Lipid antioxidant (Vitamin E activity) | | **Tocotrienols** | α-, γ-tocotrienol | Palm, Rice bran | Neuroprotective, cholesterol-lowering | | **Phytosterols** | β-sitosterol, campesterol, stigmasterol | Corn, Soybean, Rapeseed | LDL-cholesterol reduction | | **Lignans** | Secoisolariciresinol diglucoside (SDG) | Flaxseed | Phytoestrogen, antioxidant | | **Polyphenols** | Ferulic acid, p-coumaric acid | Rice bran, Sesame | UV protection, antioxidant | | **Phospholipids** | Phosphatidylcholine, Phosphatidylethanolamine | Soybean lecithin | Emulsifier, membrane integrity | | **Squalene** | Squalene (C₃₀H₅₀) | Amaranth, Olive | Oxygen carrier, anticancer | | **Carotenoids** | β-carotene, lutein | Palm oil | Provitamin A, photoprotection | | **Oryzanol** | γ-oryzanol | Rice bran | Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory | ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Seed Oil Quality Indices ### 2.2.1 Iodine Value (IV) — Measure of Unsaturation > [!NOTE] > **Why It Matters:** The Iodine Value quantifies the degree of unsaturation in a seed oil. Higher IV = more double bonds = greater susceptibility to oxidation. This is a critical quality parameter for predicting shelf-life. $$IV = \frac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$ Where: - $B$ = Volume (mL) of blank titration - $S$ = Volume (mL) of sample titration - $N$ = Normality of sodium thiosulfate solution - $W$ = Mass of oil sample (g) - $12.69$ = Equivalent weight of iodine $\times 100$ / 1000 ### 2.2.2 Saponification Value (SV) — Indicator of Molecular Weight $$SV = \frac{(B - S) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$ Where **56.1** = equivalent weight of KOH. Higher SV indicates shorter fatty acid chains (lower molecular weight TAGs). ### 2.2.3 Acid Value (AV) — Free Fatty Acid Indicator $$AV = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$ A high acid value indicates **lipolytic hydrolysis** during seed storage, releasing free fatty acids (FFA) that accelerate rancidity. ### 2.2.4 Peroxide Value (PV) — Primary Oxidation Product $$PV = \frac{(S - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$$ Expressed in **milliequivalents of active oxygen per kilogram of oil (meq O₂/kg)**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Iodine Value (IV)** | $IV = \frac{(B-S) \cdot N \cdot 12.69}{W}$ | g I₂ / 100 g oil | Drying/non-drying oil classification | | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $SV = \frac{(B-S) \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH / g oil | Average molecular weight of TAGs | | **Acid Value (AV)** | $AV = \frac{V \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH / g oil | FFA content → oil freshness | | **Peroxide Value (PV)** | $PV = \frac{(S-B) \cdot N \cdot 1000}{W}$ | meq O₂ / kg oil | Primary oxidation status | | **Thiobarbituric Acid (TBA)** | $TBA = \frac{A_{532} \times 1000}{W}$ | mg MDA / kg oil | Secondary oxidation (malondialdehyde) | | **Degree of Unsaturation (DU)** | $DU = \text{C}_{18:1}\% + 2 \cdot \text{C}_{18:2}\% + 3 \cdot \text{C}_{18:3}\%$ | dimensionless | Predicts oxidative stability | | **Oxidative Stability Index (OSI)** | Empirical (Rancimat, t = induction time) | hours | Time to rapid oxidation | | **Calculated PV (C-Value)** | $C = \frac{1 \cdot [Oleic] + 2 \cdot [Linoleic] + 3 \cdot [Linolenic]}{100}$ | % | Linear correlation with shelf life | | **ω-6/ω-3 Ratio** | $\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{LA} \%}{\text{ALA} \%}$ | dimensionless | Nutritional balance index | | **Atherogenic Index (AI)** | $AI = \frac{\text{C}_{12:0} + 4 \cdot \text{C}_{14:0} + \text{C}_{16:0}}{\Sigma \text{UFA}}$ | dimensionless | Cardiovascular risk indicator | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shelf-Life Engineering:** Using IV and PUFA%, food engineers design packaging (oxygen-barrier films, nitrogen flushing) and antioxidant systems for seed oils. - **Quality Control in Oil Refineries:** SV and AV are used in **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, **FSSAI**, and **Codex Alimentarius** grading of edible oils. - **Biodiesel Engineering:** High IV oils (linseed) are unsuitable for biodiesel due to polymerization; thus IV is used in feedstock selection. - **Cosmetic Formulation:** SV guides soap-making (Na-KOH) for natural seed oil soaps. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Calculating Oxidative Stability from Fatty Acid Profile ### Problem Context A cold-pressed flaxseed oil sample is reported to contain the following fatty acid composition: - **C16:0 (Palmitic):** 6% - **C18:0 (Stearic):** 3% - **C18:1 (Oleic):** 20% - **C18:2 (Linoleic):** 16% - **C18:3 (α-Linolenic):** 55% **Task:** Calculate the **Iodine Value (IV)** using the standard Wijs method conversion factors, and comment on the oxidative stability of the oil. ### Step 1: Recall the Empirical IV Formula The iodine value can be **predicted** from the fatty acid composition using the empirical equation: $$IV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \%FA_i \times IV_i \right)$$ Where $\%FA_i$ is the percentage of each fatty acid and $IV_i$ is the iodine value of that pure fatty acid. ### Step 2: Identify the Standard IV Factors for Each Fatty Acid | Fatty Acid | Symbol | $IV_i$ (g I₂ / 100 g FA) | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Palmitic acid | C16:0 | 0.0 | Saturated (no double bonds) | | Stearic acid | C18:0 | 0.0 | Saturated | | Oleic acid | C18:1 | 89.9 | 1 double bond | | Linoleic acid | C18:2 | 181.0 | 2 double bonds | | α-Linolenic acid | C18:3 | 273.5 | 3 double bonds | ### Step 3: Substitute Each Value into the Equation $$ \begin{aligned} IV &= (6 \times 0.0) + (3 \times 0.0) + (20 \times 89.9) + (16 \times 181.0) + (55 \times 273.5) \\ &= 0 + 0 + 1798.0 + 2896.0 + 15042.5 \\ &= 19736.5 \, / \, 100 \\ &= 197.4 \, \text{g I}_2 \, / \, 100 \, \text{g oil} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Comment on Oxidative Stability A IV of **197.4** classifies this oil as a **"drying oil"** (IV > 130). The high proportion of α-linolenic acid (55%) means: - **ω-6/ω-3 ratio = 16/55 ≈ 0.29** (excellent nutritional balance, below the recommended 4:1) - **Predicted OSI < 4 hours** (highly unstable, must be stored in dark amber glass under nitrogen) - Recommended **cold chain (4°C)** and addition of **mixed tocopherols (≥ 500 ppm)** for stability. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Calculating the Degree of Unsaturation (DU) ### Problem Context Calculate the **Degree of Unsaturation (DU)** for sunflower oil with the following composition: Oleic 28%, Linoleic 60%, Linolenic 1%, Saturated 11%. ### Step 1: Apply the DU Formula $$ \begin{aligned} DU &= (\% \text{Oleic} \times 1) + (\% \text{Linoleic} \times 2) + (\% \text{Linolenic} \times 3) \\ DU &= (28 \times 1) + (60 \times 2) + (1 \times 3) \\ DU &= 28 + 120 + 3 \\ DU &= 151 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Interpret the Result A DU of **151** indicates that sunflower oil has moderate oxidative stability — better than linseed oil (DU ~220) but lower than olive oil (DU ~85). It correlates with an **estimated OSI of 8–12 hours** under accelerated Rancimat conditions (110°C, 20 L/h air). ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Atherogenic Index (AI) Calculation ### Problem Context For palm olein (C12:0 = 0.2%, C14:0 = 1.0%, C16:0 = 44%, C18:0 = 4.5%, UFA = 50.3%), calculate the **Atherogenic Index**. ### Step 1: Apply the AI Formula $$ \begin{aligned} AI &= \frac{[\text{C12:0}] + (4 \times [\text{C14:0}]) + [\text{C16:0}]}{\Sigma \text{UFA}} \\ AI &= \frac{0.2 + (4 \times 1.0) + 44.0}{50.3} \\ AI &= \frac{0.2 + 4.0 + 44.0}{50.3} \\ AI &= \frac{48.2}{50.3} \\ AI &= 0.958 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Nutritional Interpretation An AI of **0.96** indicates that palm olein is **pro-atherogenic** due to its high palmitic acid content. This contrasts with high-oleic sunflower oil (AI < 0.2) or flaxseed oil (AI < 0.1), which are cardioprotective. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major Seed Oils | Seed Oil | C16:0 | C18:0 | C18:1 | C18:2 | C18:3 | IV | Primary Phytochemical | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Sunflower (linoleic)** | 6% | 4% | 28% | 60% | 0.2% | 130 | α-Tocopherol (40 mg/100g) | Cooking oil, salad dressings | | **Sunflower (high-oleic)** | 4% | 4% | 82% | 8% | 0.1% | 85 | α-Tocopherol (30 mg/100g) | Frying, industrial frying | | **Safflower** | 6% | 2% | 14% | 75% | 0.1% | 145 | α-Tocopherol | Salad oil, margarine | | **Cottonseed** | 23% | 2% | 17% | 55% | 0.2% | 105 | Gossypol (toxic – removed by refining) | Cooking, shortening | | **Sesame** | 9% | 5% | 38% | 42% | 0.3% | 110 | Sesamin, Sesamolin (lignans) | Health oil, Ayurveda | | **Flaxseed (Linseed)** | 6% | 3% | 20% | 16% | 55% | 197 | SDG (lignan), Lignans | Functional food, ω-3 | | **Rapeseed/Canola** | 4% | 2% | 62% | 22% | 10% | 110 | Canolol, Phytosterols | Cooking, spreads | | **Soybean** | 11% | 4% | 23% | 53% | 7% | 130 | Isoflavones, γ-Tocopherol | Refined oil, lecithin | | **Mustard** | 2% | 1% | 11% | 15% | 5% | 110 | Allyl isothiocyanate | Condiment, Ayurvedic oil | | **Rice Bran** | 17% | 2% | 39% | 36% | 1% | 100 | γ-Oryzanol (1.5%) | Health oil, antioxidants | | **Evening Primrose** | 6% | 2% | 9% | 71% | 0.2% | 155 | GLA (γ-linolenic, 9%) | Pharmaceutical, PMS, eczema | | **Perilla** | 6% | 2% | 17% | 14% | 58% | 200 | Rosmarinic acid, ALA | East Asian cuisine | | **Pomegranate** | 4% | 3% | 7% | 10% | 0.5% | 80 | Punicic acid (CLA, 70%) | Nutraceutical, cosmetics | | **Hemp** | 6% | 3% | 12% | 55% | 22% | 170 | CBD, tocopherols | Functional food, supplements | | **Amaranth** | 19% | 4% | 23% | 47% | 2% | 130 | Squalene (6–8%) | Cosmetic, pharmaceutical | | **Coconut (seed kernel)** | 9% | 3% | 6% | 2% | 0% | 10 | Lauric acid (50%) | Cooking, MCT formulations | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Seed Oil Composition Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A[SEED OIL
Total Lipid Extract] --> B[Triacylglycerols
95 to 98 percent] A --> C[Minor Bioactive Fraction
2 to 5 percent] B --> B1[Saturated FA
Palmitic Stearic] B --> B2[Monounsaturated FA
Oleic Acid] B --> B3[Polyunsaturated FA
Linoleic Linolenic] C --> D1[Tocopherols and Tocotrienols
Vitamin E family] C --> D2[Phytosterols
Beta sitosterol Campesterol] C --> D3[Phospholipids
Lecithin Cephalin] C --> D4[Squalene and Sterols] C --> D5[Lignans
SDG Sesamin] C --> D6[Phenolic Compounds
Ferulic Acid Canolol] C --> D7[Carotenoids
Beta carotene Lutein] C --> D8[Volatiles and Waxes] D1 --> E1[Antioxidant Protection
Lipid Peroxidation Inhibition] D2 --> E2[Cholesterol Modulation
LDL Reduction] D3 --> E3[Emulsification in Foods] D4 --> E4[Cosmetic and Pharmaceutical Use] D5 --> E5[Phytoestrogenic Activity] D6 --> E6[Antioxidant and UV Protection] D7 --> E7[Provitamin A Activity] ``` ## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Seed Oil Extraction and Refining Stages ```mermaid graph TD A[Raw Oilseeds
Sunflower Flaxseed Mustard] --> B[Cleaning and Dehulling] B --> C[Conditioning
Moisture and Heat Adjustment] C --> D[Mechanical Pressing
Expeller or Screw Press] D --> E[Crude Seed Oil] D --> F[Oil Cake Press Residue] E --> G[Solvent Extraction
Hexane or Subcritical CO2] G --> H[Miscella
Oil plus Solvent] H --> I[Solvent Recovery
Distillation] I --> J[Crude Oil Pool] E --> J J --> K[Degumming
Phospholipid Removal] K --> L[Neutralization
Free Fatty Acid Removal] L --> M[Bleaching
Pigment and Soap Removal] M --> N[Dewaxing
Winterization] N --> O[Deodorization
Steam Distillation at 240C] O --> P[Refined Bleached Deodorized Oil] P --> Q[Fortification
Vitamin A and D Addition] Q --> R[Packaged Seed Oil Product] F --> S[Solvent Desolventizing] S --> T[Defatted Meal
Animal Feed Protein] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Mechanism of Lipid Oxidation and Antioxidant Defense ```mermaid graph TD A[Unsaturated Fatty Acid
RH] --> B[Initiation
Heat Light Metal] B --> C[Free Radical
R dot] C --> D[Propagation
Oxygen Attack] D --> E[Peroxy Radical
ROO dot] E --> F[Lipid Hydroperoxide
ROOH] F --> G[Secondary Oxidation
Aldehydes Ketones] G --> H[Rancidity
Off flavor and Toxicity] I[Antioxidant Defense
Tocopherol Polyphenol] --> J[Radical Scavenging
AH plus R dot gives RH plus A dot] J --> K[Stable Radical
A dot Resonantly Stabilized] K --> L[Chain Reaction Terminated] M[Metal Chelators
Citric Acid EDTA] --> N[Sequestration of Fe2 plus Cu2 plus] O[Synergists
Ascorbic Acid] --> P[Regeneration of Tocopherol] P --> L ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Biosynthetic Origin of Seed Oil Phytochemicals ```mermaid graph TD A[Acetyl CoA
Primary Metabolic Precursor] --> B[Malonyl CoA
via Acetyl CoA Carboxylase] B --> C[Fatty Acid Synthase Complex
Type II in Plastids] C --> D[C16 0 Palmitic Acid
First Product] D --> E[Elongases and Desaturases
Endoplasmic Reticulum] E --> F1[C18 0 Stearic] E --> F2[C18 1 Oleic
via Stearoyl ACP Desaturase] E --> F3[C18 2 Linoleic
via Oleate Desaturase] E --> F4[C18 3 Linolenic
via Linoleate Desaturase] F1 --> G[Kennedy Pathway
Glycerol 3 Phosphate] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> H[Triacylglycerol Assembly
DGAT Diacylglycerol Acyltransferase] H --> I[Storage in Oil Bodies
Oleosin Coated Lipid Droplets] A --> J[Shikimate Pathway] J --> K[Phenylpropanoid Pathway] K --> L[Tocopherols and Phenolic Acids] A --> M[Mevalonate Pathway] M --> N[Phytosterols and Squalene] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]** **Q:** List any three **fatty acid–based classification categories** of edible seed oils and give one example oil for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Oleic–Linoleic acid oils** — Sunflower oil (contains ~60% linoleic and ~28% oleic acid). 2. **Linolenic acid oils (omega-3 rich)** — Flaxseed (Linseed) oil (contains ~55% α-linolenic acid). 3. **Lauric acid oils (saturated)** — Coconut oil (contains ~50% lauric acid, C12:0). *(Award 1 mark per correct category + example pair.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]** **Q:** What is **Iodine Value (IV)**? State the IV range that classifies an oil as a "drying oil." **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Iodine Value (IV)** is the number of grams of iodine (or equivalent halogen) absorbed by 100 g of oil under standard conditions, reflecting the degree of unsaturation (number of C=C double bonds) in the fatty acid chains. **(2 marks)** - **Drying oil:** IV > 130 (e.g., linseed oil, IV ~190) - **Semi-drying oil:** IV 95–130 (e.g., soybean) - **Non-drying oil:** IV < 95 (e.g., coconut, olive) **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023 Pattern]** **Q:** (a) Describe the **chemical composition of seed oils** with emphasis on the triacylglycerol backbone and the stereospecific distribution of fatty acids. **(7 marks)** **(b) Explain the mechanism of **lipid oxidation in seed oils** and discuss the role of **tocopherols, phytosterols, and lignans** as natural antioxidants. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Triacylglycerol Structure (2 marks):** A triacylglycerol (TAG) consists of a glycerol molecule esterified at the sn-1, sn-2, and sn-3 hydroxyl positions with three fatty acids, forming an ester bond with the release of three water molecules. The general formula is C₃H₅(OOCR₁)(OOCR₂)(OOCR₃). 2. **Stereospecific Distribution (2 marks):** In most seed oils, the **sn-2 (middle) position** is enriched in **unsaturated fatty acids** (e.g., linoleic, oleic) while **sn-1 and sn-3** are enriched in **saturated fatty acids** (palmitic, stearic). This positioning is governed by **glycerol-3-phosphate acyltransferases (GPAT)** and **lysophosphatidic acid acyltransferases (LPAT)**. 3. **Minor Components (2 marks):** The 2–5% non-TAG fraction includes tocopherols (α, β, γ, δ), phytosterols (β-sitosterol, campesterol), phospholipids, squalene, and lignans — the phytochemicals of interest. 4. **Functional Significance (1 mark):** The stereospecific structure affects **digestibility, melting point, oxidative stability**, and **crystallization behavior** of the oil. For example, cocoa butter's sn-2 oleic acid is responsible for its sharp melting at body temperature. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks 1. **Initiation Step (1 mark):** Heat, light, or transition metals (Fe²⁺, Cu⁺) abstract a hydrogen from a bis-allylic methylene of an unsaturated fatty acid (RH), forming a **lipid alkyl radical (R•)**. 2. **Propagation (2 marks):** R• rapidly reacts with atmospheric O₂ to form a **peroxyl radical (ROO•)**, which abstracts H from another fatty acid to yield **lipid hydroperoxide (ROOH)** and a new R• — perpetuating a chain reaction. 3. **Termination (1 mark):** Two radicals combine to form non-radical products, or are quenched by antioxidants. 4. **Tocopherols (1.5 marks):** α-Tocopherol donates a phenolic H• to ROO•, becoming a stable **tocopheroxyl radical** that resonates and is regenerated by ascorbic acid. The order of antioxidant potency: α > β > γ > δ-tocopherol (in vivo) but γ > α in foods. 5. **Phytosterols (0.5 marks):** Primarily act as **membrane stabilizers** in oil bodies and reduce **LDL-cholesterol** in humans, with secondary antioxidant effects. 6. **Lignans (1 mark):** Flaxseed lignans (SDG) and sesame lignans (sesamin, sesamolin) scavenge hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and upregulate endogenous antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase, GPx). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative Pattern]** **Q:** (a) Explain the **industrial extraction and refining of seed oils**, including degumming, neutralization, bleaching, and deodorization. **(7 marks)** **(b) Discuss the **nutritional and nutraceutical significance of seed oil phytochemicals**, with special reference to **rice bran (γ-oryzanol)**, **flaxseed (SDG lignan)**, and **evening primrose (γ-linolenic acid – GLA)**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Mechanical Expeller Pressing (1.5 marks):** Pre-cleaned, dehulled, and conditioned seeds are crushed in a screw press at 60–80°C, yielding ~70% of the oil. The residual **oil cake** (15–18% residual oil) is sent for solvent extraction. 2. **Solvent Extraction (1.5 marks):** Hexane (boiling point 69°C) percolates through the cake, dissolving the oil. The **miscella** (oil + hexane) is then distilled to recover both. Modern subcritical CO₂ extraction is used for premium "cold-pressed" labelled oils. 3. **Degumming (1 mark):** Hydration with 1–3% water at 60–80°C hydrates **phospholipids** (lecithin, cephalin), which are then centrifuged out. This is the source of commercial **soy lecithin**. 4. **Neutralization (1 mark):** Caustic soda (NaOH, 8–12°Bé) reacts with free fatty acids forming **soapstock** that is separated. This reduces AV to < 0.1 mg KOH/g. 5. **Bleaching (1 mark):** **Bleaching earth (Fuller's earth, bentonite, 0.5–2%)** at 90–110°C under vacuum adsorbs pigments (carotenoids, chlorophylls) and soap residues. Critical for light-colored refined oils. 6. **Deodorization (1 mark):** Steam distillation at 220–250°C under 2–6 mbar vacuum removes volatile **off-flavor compounds** (aldehydes, ketones, free fatty acids), yielding a bland, shelf-stable RBD (Refined, Bleached, Deodorized) oil. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks 1. **Rice Bran Oil – γ-Oryzanol (2.5 marks):** γ-Oryzanol is a mixture of **cycloartenyl ferulate, 24-methylene cycloartanyl ferulate, and campesteryl ferulate**. It exhibits **antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and plasma-cholesterol-lowering** effects by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase. Clinically shown to reduce LDL by 10–15% at 300 mg/day dose. 2. **Flaxseed Oil – SDG Lignan (2.5 marks):** Secoisolariciresinol diglucoside (SDG) is the **major plant lignan**. After ingestion, gut microbiota converts SDG → **enterodiol → enterolactone**, which are weak **phytoestrogens** binding estrogen receptor β. Exhibits **antiproliferative activity in breast and colon cancer** cell lines, and reduces **hot flashes in menopausal women** by 50% in RCTs. 3. **Evening Primrose Oil – GLA (2 marks):** Evening primrose (*Oenothera biennis*) oil contains **9% γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n-6)**, bypassing the rate-limiting **Δ6-desaturase** step. GLA → dihomo-γ-linolenic acid (DGLA) → **prostaglandin E1 (PGE₁)**, an anti-inflammatory eicosanoid. Indicated for **atopic dermatitis, PMS, and mastalgia**; reduces itching and erythema in eczema patients. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing the Iodine Value Units:** Students often forget the factor **12.69** in IV calculation and lose 1 mark. Remember: 12.69 = (molecular weight of I₂ / number of electrons transferred × 100) — memorize this. > 2. **Misidentifying sn-2 Position:** A common error is claiming that the sn-2 position holds **saturated** fatty acids — this is the opposite of the truth. Unsaturated FA occupy sn-2 in seed oils. > 3. **Refining vs. Cold-Pressed:** Many students write that cold-pressed oil is "purified" — but cold-pressing is a **non-refined** method that retains phytochemicals (tocopherols, lignans) but has shorter shelf life. > 4. **Confusing γ-Oryzanol with Vitamin E:** Rice bran's γ-Oryzanol is **NOT** a tocopherol — it is a ferulic acid ester of triterpene alcohols. > 5. **Incomplete SDG Metabolism:** Students forget the role of **gut microbiota** in converting SDG to enterolactone — this is essential for its phytoestrogenic activity. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — Read this section twice before the KTU exam.** ### Core Concepts to Memorize - ✅ **Seed oils = TAG (95–98%) + Phytochemical fraction (2–5%)** - ✅ **Major seed oils:** Sunflower, Safflower, Cottonseed, Soybean, Rapeseed/Canola, Sesame, Flaxseed, Mustard, Rice Bran, Evening Primrose, Pomegranate, Perilla, Hemp - ✅ **Stereospecific structure:** Unsaturated FA at **sn-2**; saturated FA at **sn-1, sn-3** - ✅ **Classification by IV:** Drying (IV > 130), Semi-drying (95–130), Non-drying (< 95) - ✅ **Three oxidation stages:** Initiation → Propagation → Termination - ✅ **Tocopherol antioxidant order (in vivo):** α > β > γ > δ - ✅ **Tocopherol antioxidant order (in vitro/food):** γ > δ > β > α - ✅ **Refining steps order:** Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Dewaxing → Deodorization - ✅ **ω-6/ω-3 ratio:** Flaxseed = 0.3 (excellent); Sunflower = 60 (poor) - ✅ **GLA bypasses Δ6-desaturase** in evening primrose and borage oil - ✅ **γ-Oryzanol** = triterpene alcohol ferulate (NOT a tocopherol) - ✅ **SDG → enterodiol → enterolactone** (microbial conversion in colon) - ✅ **Canolol** = 2,6-dimethoxy-4-vinylphenol, formed from sinapic acid during rapeseed deodorization - ✅ **Sesamin + sesamolin** = sesame lignans (sesamin → enterolactone after metabolism) - ✅ **Punicic acid** = conjugated linolenic acid (CLnA) in pomegranate seed oil ### Critical Numerical Values to Remember - 🔹 IV factor for oleic acid: **89.9** - 🔹 IV factor for linoleic acid: **181.0** - 🔹 IV factor for linolenic acid: **273.5** - 🔹 Equivalent weight of KOH: **56.1 g/mol** - 🔹 Saponification value range: 180–200 for most seed oils - 🔹 Coconut oil saponification value: ~**256** (high, due to lauric acid) - 🔹 Rancimat test standard conditions: **110°C, 20 L/h air flow** - 🔹 FSSAI maximum acid value for refined oil: **0.5 mg KOH/g** - 🔹 FSSAI maximum peroxide value for refined oil: **10 meq O₂/kg** ### Quick-Recall Phytochemical Mappings - 🧪 **Sunflower → α-Tocopherol** - 🧪 **Flaxseed → SDG (lignan)** - 🧪 **Sesame → Sesamin + Sesamolin** - 🧪 **Rice Bran → γ-Oryzanol** - 🧪 **Rapeseed → Canolol + Phytosterols** - 🧪 **Pomegranate → Punicic acid (CLA)** - 🧪 **Evening Primrose → GLA (γ-linolenic acid)** - 🧪 **Soybean → Isoflavones + γ-Tocopherol** - 🧪 **Amaranth → Squalene** - 🧪 **Perilla → ALA + Rosmarinic acid** ### Common KTU Exam Traps - ⚠️ Don't confuse **Iodine Value** with **Saponification Value** — they measure different things (unsaturation vs. chain length). - ⚠️ Cold-pressed oils retain **higher phytochemicals** but have **shorter shelf life** than RBD oils. - ⚠️ Refining **removes** most tocopherols and sterols (losses up to 40–60%) — this is why fortification with natural antioxidants is gaining importance. - ⚠️ CLA (conjugated linoleic acid) ≠ Punicic acid (conjugated linolenic acid) — pomegranate seed oil has the latter.

topics.title: Terpenoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Terpenoids # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Terpenoids** (also historically called **isoprenoids**) constitute the largest and most chemically diverse class of plant secondary metabolites, structurally derived from the repetitive assembly of a **5-carbon isoprene unit (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene, C₅H₈)**. They are formally defined as modified terpenes that contain additional functional groups, most commonly oxygen, introduced through oxidation, rearrangement, or cyclization reactions. The empirical general formula that governs terpenoid carbon skeleton construction is the **Isoprene Rule**, formalized by Leopold Ružička (1939): $$\text{General Skeleton} = \left( \text{C}_5\text{H}_8 \right)_n \quad \text{where} \quad n \geq 1$$ > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A terpene contains only carbon and hydrogen (hydrocarbon backbone), whereas a **terpenoid** has been chemically modified to contain oxygen-bearing functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), carbonyl (C=O), or carboxyl (-COOH). In modern usage, the two terms are often used interchangeably in food science literature. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "LEGO Brick" Model of Plant Chemistry Imagine that nature has a single, universal building block — the **isoprene brick (C₅H₈)**. Plants act like master architects that snap these bricks together in different combinations: - **1 brick** → Hemiterpene (C₅) — a tiny hut - **2 bricks** → Monoterpene (C₁₀) — a small cabin - **3 bricks** → Sesquiterpene (C₁₅) — a medium house - **4 bricks** → Diterpene (C₂₀) — a large bungalow - **6 bricks** → Triterpene (C₃₀) — a mansion - **8 bricks** → Tetraterpene (C₄₀) — a palace (e.g., carotenoids!) Once assembled, the plant then **decorates** these structures by adding oxygen, hydroxyls, or carbonyls — converting plain "terpenes" into functional "terpenoids" that give mangoes their aroma, carrots their color, and mint its cooling sensation. --- ## 1.3 Standard Physical Metric — The Isoprene Unit The foundational monomer of every terpenoid is **2-methyl-1,3-butadiene**: | Property | Value | | :--- | :--- | | **Molecular formula** | **C₅H₈** | | **Molar mass** | **68.12 g/mol** | | **Degrees of unsaturation** | **2** | | **Functional isoprene unit (biological)** | **Isopentenyl diphosphate (IPP)** | | **Active isomer (biological)** | **Dimethylallyl diphosphate (DMAPP)** | > [!NOTE] > In living plant cells, plants do **not** use free isoprene gas. They use the **activated phosphorylated form — Isopentenyl Pyrophosphate (IPP)** and its allylic isomer **Dimethylallyl Pyrophosphate (DMAPP)**. This is the biologically active "brick" shipped to the assembly site. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Isoprene Rule — The Architectural Blueprint The **Isoprene Rule** states that every terpenoid carbon skeleton can be conceptually dissected into **isoprene units** joined together in a **head-to-tail** fashion. This rule has two complementary parts: ### (a) The Classical (Wallach) Rule The total number of carbon atoms in any terpenoid is always a **multiple of 5**. ### (b) The Biogenetic (Ružička) Rule The linkage between isoprene units is almost exclusively **1,4 (head-to-tail)**, meaning C-1 of one IPP molecule joins to C-4 of the next. $$\text{IPP (Head)} \xrightarrow{\text{condensation}} \text{Polyisoprenyl-PP}$$ --- ## 2.2 Classification Hierarchy of Terpenoids The classification is **strictly based on the number of isoprene units (n)**: | Class | n (units) | Carbon Atoms | Representative Food Examples | Functional Role in Food | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **Hemiterpenoid** | 1 | C₅ | Isovaleraldehyde (hops), Tiglic acid | Beer aroma | | **Monoterpenoid** | 2 | C₁₀ | Limonene (citrus), Menthol (mint), Linalool (coriander) | Aroma, flavor | | **Sesquiterpenoid** | 3 | C₁₅ | Zingiberene (ginger), Farnesol (berries) | Pungency, aroma | | **Diterpenoid** | 4 | C₂₀ | Cafestol (coffee), Steviol glycosides (stevia), Taxol | Sweeteners, bioactives | | **Sesterterpenoid** | 5 | C₂₅ | Rare in food (mainly fungi/marine) | Minor bioactives | | **Triterpenoid** | 6 | C₃₀ | Squalene (olive oil), Ginsenosides, Oleanolic acid | Cholesterol precursor, bioactives | | **Tetraterpenoid** | 8 | C₄₀ | Carotenoids (β-carotene, lycopene, lutein) | Pigments, provitamin A | | **Polyterpenoid** | >8 | (C₅)ₙ | Natural rubber, Gutta-percha | Industrial polymers | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Memorization Mnemonic (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** > **"Hem-Mono-Sesqui-Di-Sester-Tri-Tetra-Poly"** > **H-M-S-D-S-T-T-P** → C₅-C₁₀-C₁₅-C₂₀-C₂₅-C₃₀-C₄₀-(C₅)ₙ --- ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations and rules are **exam-locked** for the KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize this block — it has appeared in every board paper since 2020. ### (a) Universal Terpenoid Formula $$C_{5n}H_{8n} \quad \text{(for an unmodified terpene hydrocarbon)}$$ ### (b) Carbon Count Validation $$\text{Total Carbons} = 5 \times n$$ ### (c) Molecular Weight Estimation $$M_{\text{terpene}} \approx 68.12 \times n \quad \text{(g/mol, hydrocarbon skeleton only)}$$ ### (d) Hydrogen Deficiency Index (HDI) $$\text{HDI} = \frac{2C + 2 - H}{2}$$ ### (e) Extinction Coefficient — Carotenoids (Lambert-Beer) $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l}$$ | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $A$ | Absorbance at λ_max | dimensionless | | $\varepsilon$ | Molar extinction coefficient | L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | | $c$ | Concentration | mol·L⁻¹ | | $l$ | Optical path length | cm | > For β-carotene, $\varepsilon \approx 1.40 \times 10^5$ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ at **450 nm** in petroleum ether — a value examiners frequently quote in numerical problems. ### (f) Provitamin A Activity (Retinol Equivalents) $$\text{RE} = \frac{\mu g\ \beta\text{-carotene}}{12} + \frac{\mu g\ \text{other carotenoids}}{24}$$ --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Technology | Application Domain | Specific Terpenoid Used | Industrial Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Natural flavoring** | Limonene, menthol, linalool | Beverage, confectionery | | **Natural pigments** | β-carotene, lycopene, annatto | Fat substitutes, colorants | | **Antioxidant preservatives** | Lycopene, astaxanthin | Lipid oxidation inhibition | | **Functional food & nutraceuticals** | Ginsenosides, steviol glycosides | Sweetener, adaptogen | | **Natural antimicrobials** | Thymol, carvacrol | Essential oil packaging films | | **Edible coatings** | Squalene | Barrier against moisture | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proof of the Isoprene Rule and Carbon Count **Problem Statement:** Show that geraniol (a monoterpenoid alcohol in geranium oil with molecular formula C₁₀H₁₈O) is structurally composed of exactly 2 isoprene units and conforms to the classical Isoprene Rule. ### Step 1 — Identify the Molecular Formula Geraniol has the experimentally determined formula: $$\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{18}\text{O}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Number of Isoprene Units (n) Using the universal relation: $$n = \frac{\text{Total Carbon Atoms}}{5}$$ Substituting: $$n = \frac{10}{5} = 2$$ ### Step 3 — Verify Against the Skeletal Formula The isoprene skeleton predicts: $$C_{5n}H_{8n} \Rightarrow C_{10}H_{16}$$ Geraniol has C₁₀H₁₈O — that is, **+2 H** and **+1 O** compared with the pure hydrocarbon skeleton. This perfectly matches the definition: geraniol is a terpenoid (oxygenated), and the two extra hydrogens come from the terminal -CH₂OH (hydroxymethyl) reduction, making it an allylic alcohol. ### Step 4 — Dissect the Carbon Skeleton Visualize geraniol as two isoprene units linked 1,4 (head-to-tail): $$\text{IPP}_1 (C_5) \xrightarrow{\text{1,4-linkage}} \text{IPP}_2 (C_5) \Rightarrow \text{Geranyl-PP (C_{10})}$$ Step 5 — Compute HDI to Confirm the Two Double Bonds $$\text{HDI} = \frac{2C + 2 - H}{2}$$ $$\text{HDI} = \frac{2(10) + 2 - 18}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$ The HDI = **2** corresponds to the **two C=C double bonds** of geraniol, perfectly validating the structure. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Geraniol obeys the Isoprene Rule. It is a monoterpenoid with n = 2. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantitative β-Carotene Content in a Food Extract **Problem Statement:** A carrot oil extract is dissolved in petroleum ether. Using a cuvette of path length **1.0 cm**, the absorbance at 450 nm is measured as **0.840**. The molar extinction coefficient of β-carotene at this wavelength is **1.40 × 10⁵ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**. Calculate the molar concentration of β-carotene in the extract. ### Step 1 — Apply the Lambert-Beer Law $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ ### Step 2 — Rearrange for Concentration $$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Known Values $$c = \frac{0.840}{(1.40 \times 10^5\ \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}) \times (1.0\ \text{cm})}$$ ### Step 4 — Perform the Arithmetic $$c = \frac{0.840}{1.40 \times 10^5}$$ $$c = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol·L}^{-1}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to mg·L⁻¹ for Food Reporting Molar mass of β-carotene (C₄₀H₅₆) = **536.87 g·mol⁻¹** $$c_{\text{mg/L}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol·L}^{-1} \times 536.87\ \text{g·mol}^{-1} \times 1000\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{g}}$$ $$c_{\text{mg/L}} = 3.22\ \text{mg·L}^{-1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The carrot extract contains **3.22 mg/L** β-carotene — a typical value reported in food analytical chemistry literature. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Provitamin A Retinol Equivalent of a Mixed-Carotenoid Food **Problem Statement:** A serving of cooked sweet potato (150 g) contains **4200 µg β-carotene** and **1800 µg α-carotene**. Calculate the total Retinol Equivalent (RE). ### Step 1 — Apply the RE Formula $$\text{RE} = \frac{\mu g\ \beta\text{-carotene}}{12} + \frac{\mu g\ \text{other provitamin A carotenoids}}{24}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$\text{RE} = \frac{4200}{12} + \frac{1800}{24}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Each Term $$\frac{4200}{12} = 350\ \mu g\ \text{RE}$$ $$\frac{1800}{24} = 75\ \mu g\ \text{RE}$$ ### Step 4 — Sum the Contributions $$\text{RE}_{\text{total}} = 350 + 75 = 425\ \mu g\ \text{RE}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to µg RAE (modern FAO/IOM convention) $$\mu g\ \text{RAE} = \mu g\ \text{RE} \approx 425\ \mu g\ \text{RAE per serving}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** This single 150 g serving provides **~127%** of the RDA for Vitamin A (700 µg RAE/day for adult women), confirming sweet potato as a potent provitamin A food. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Structural Comparison of Key Food Terpenoids | Compound | Class (n) | Molecular Formula | Food Source | Functional Group Added | Primary Biological Role | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Limonene** | Monoterpene (n=2) | C₁₀H₁₆ | Citrus peel | None (pure terpene) | Chemopreventive, flavor | | **Menthol** | Monoterpenoid (n=2) | C₁₀H₂₀O | Peppermint | Tertiary -OH | Cooling, analgesic | | **Geraniol** | Monoterpenoid (n=2) | C₁₀H₁₈O | Rose, lemongrass | Primary -OH on allylic C | Antimicrobial, perfumery | | **Zingiberene** | Sesquiterpene (n=3) | C₁₅H₂₄ | Ginger | None (cyclic) | Digestive stimulant | | **Cafestol** | Diterpenoid (n=4) | C₂₀H₂₈O₃ | Unfiltered coffee | 2× -OH, 1× furan | Serum cholesterol effects | | **Squalene** | Triterpene (n=6) | C₃₀H₅₀ | Olive oil, shark liver | None (polyene) | Skin moisturizer, antioxidant | | **β-Carotene** | Tetraterpenoid (n=8) | C₄₀H₅₆ | Carrot, mango | None (pure hydrocarbon) | Provitamin A, antioxidant | | **Lycopene** | Tetraterpenoid (n=8) | C₄₀H₅₆ | Tomato, watermelon | None (acyclic) | Antioxidant (no provitamin A) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Terpenoid Biosynthesis Cascade (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) The following Mermaid schematic illustrates the functional architecture of terpenoid biosynthesis — from the central metabolite pool through the two divergent pathways (Mevalonate and MEP) to the final major food-relevant terpenoid classes. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Central Metabolite Pool
PYRUVATE and ACETYL-CoA"] --> B{"Compartmental Decision
Cytosolic vs Plastidic"} B -->|Cytosol Acetyl-CoA| C["MEVALONATE PATHWAY MVA
3-Acetyl-CoA condensation"] B -->|Plastid Pyruvate G3P| D["NON-MEVALONATE PATHWAY MEP
Methylerythritol Phosphate"] C --> E["MEVALONIC ACID
C6 intermediate"] D --> F["DXP and MEP
C5 sugar derivatives"] E --> G["ISOPENTENYL PYROPHOSPHATE IPP
Universal C5 active isoprene unit"] F --> G G --> H["DIMETHYLALLYL PYROPHOSPHATE DMAPP
C5 allylic isomer"] H --> I["Head-to-tail sequential condensation
1,4 IPP-DMAPP coupling"] I --> J["GERANYL PYROPHOSPHATE GPP
C10 monoterpene precursor"] J --> K["FARNESYL PYROPHOSPHATE FPP
C15 sesquiterpene precursor"] K --> L["GERANYLGERANYL PYROPHOSPHATE GGPP
C20 diterpene precursor"] L --> M["FARNESYL FARNESYL PYROPHOSPHATE FFPP
C30 triterpene precursor"] G --> N["Direct tetraterpene branch
Two GGPP condensation"] J --> O["MONOTERPENOIDS
Limonene Menthol Linalool"] K --> P["SESQUITERPENOIDS
Zingiberene Farnesol"] L --> Q["DITERPENOIDS
Cafestol Steviol Taxol"] M --> R["TRITERPENOIDS
Squalene Ginsenosides"] N --> S["TETRATERPENOIDS
Beta Carotene Lycopene Lutein"] ``` ### Interpretation of the Block Diagram - **Source Node A:** All terpenoids ultimately originate from central carbon metabolism — **pyruvate** (plastids) or **acetyl-CoA** (cytosol). - **Decision Node B:** Plants employ **two spatially separated routes** — the MVA pathway runs in the **cytosol/peroxisome** and primarily feeds sesquiterpene and triterpene synthesis; the MEP pathway operates in the **plastid** and primarily feeds monoterpene, diterpene, and tetraterpene synthesis. - **Convergence Node G:** Both pathways converge on the universal C₅ precursor — **IPP** — the true "isoprene brick" of life. - **Assembly Module I:** Successive 1,4 head-to-tail condensations of IPP with DMAPP, GPP, FPP, and GGPP produce the full cascade. - **Terminal Outputs O–S:** Each functional branch terminates in a chemically distinct family of food-relevant bioactives. --- ## 4.2 Classification Tree — Terpenoids by Carbon Count ```mermaid flowchart TD P["TERPENOIDS
All isoprene-derived plant metabolites"] --> Q{"How many C5 units n"} Q -->|n=1| R1["HEMITERPENOIDS C5
Isovaleraldehyde"] Q -->|n=2| R2["MONOTERPENOIDS C10
Limonene Menthol"] Q -->|n=3| R3["SESQUITERPENOIDS C15
Zingiberene Farnesol"] Q -->|n=4| R4["DITERPENOIDS C20
Cafestol Steviol"] Q -->|n=5| R5["SESTERTERPENOIDS C25"] Q -->|n=6| R6["TRITERPENOIDS C30
Squalene Ginsenosides"] Q -->|n=8| R7["TETRATERPENOIDS C40
Carotenoids"] Q -->|n greater than 8| R8["POLYTERPENOIDS
Natural Rubber"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define terpenoids. State the Isoprene Rule and illustrate it with one example.** **Model Answer:** > **Terpenoids** are a structurally diverse class of plant secondary metabolites biosynthesized from **isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene, C₅H₈)** units. They differ from simple terpenes by the presence of oxygen-bearing functional groups (e.g., -OH, =O, -COOH). > The **Isoprene Rule** (Leopold Ružička, 1939) states that the carbon skeleton of all terpenoids is composed of **isoprene units joined in a head-to-tail (1,4) fashion**, and the total number of carbon atoms is always a **multiple of 5**. > **Example:** Limonene (C₁₀H₁₆) — a cyclic monoterpene found in citrus peel rinds — is composed of 2 isoprene units (n = 2). **(3 marks — allocation: 1 mark definition, 1 mark rule, 1 mark example)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a terpene and a terpenoid with two food-based examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Terpene | Terpenoid | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Chemical nature** | Pure hydrocarbon (C and H only) | Contains oxygen in functional groups | | **General formula** | (C₅H₈)ₙ | (C₅H₈)ₙ + O-bearing groups | | **Example 1 (food)** | **Limonene** — citrus oil | **Menthol** — peppermint | | **Example 2 (food)** | **β-Carotene** — carrot (technically hydrocarbon) | **Geraniol** — rose/lemongrass (has -OH) | | **Polarity** | Non-polar, volatile | Often polar, less volatile | > In summary, a terpenoid is a **biochemically oxidized/functionalized derivative** of a terpene. **(3 marks — 1 mark each point of difference, plus 1 mark for the correct example pair)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation, every Module question in Part B carries an internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both questions below are mapped to Module 3 (Terpenoids).* --- ### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Set A]** **(a)** Classify terpenoids based on the number of isoprene units. Tabulate **at least six classes** with their carbon skeleton size, molecular formula, and **two food examples each**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain in detail the **Mevalonate (MVA) and Methylerythritol Phosphate (MEP) pathways** of terpenoid biosynthesis, highlighting their **cellular localization, key intermediates, and the classes of terpenoids each produces**. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Answer to Question A(a) **Classification of Terpenoids (n = isoprene units):** | Class | n | Carbons | Molecular Formula | Food Example 1 | Food Example 2 | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | Hemiterpenoid | 1 | C₅ | C₅H₈ | Isovaleraldehyde (hops) | Tiglic acid (chamomile) | | Monoterpenoid | 2 | C₁₀ | C₁₀H₁₆ | Limonene (orange peel) | Menthol (peppermint) | | Sesquiterpenoid | 3 | C₁₅ | C₁₅H₂₄ | Zingiberene (ginger) | Farnesol (berries) | | Diterpenoid | 4 | C₂₀ | C₂₀H₃₂ | Cafestol (coffee) | Steviol glycoside (stevia) | | Triterpenoid | 6 | C₃₀ | C₃₀H₄₈ | Squalene (olive oil) | Ginsenosides (ginseng) | | Tetraterpenoid | 8 | C₄₀ | C₄₀H₅₆ | β-Carotene (carrot) | Lycopene (tomato) | **Mark allocation:** - Correct tabulation with 6 classes → 4 marks - Two valid food examples per class → 2 marks - Correct carbon count and molecular formula → 1 mark --- #### Model Answer to Question A(b) **The Mevalonate Pathway (Cytosolic):** 1. **Initiation:** 3 molecules of acetyl-CoA condense to form **3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA)**. 2. **Rate-limiting step:** HMG-CoA is reduced by **HMG-CoA reductase** to **mevalonic acid (MVA)**. 3. **Activation:** MVA undergoes two ATP-dependent phosphorylations and decarboxylation to yield **isopentenyl pyrophosphate (IPP)**. 4. **Output:** Primarily generates **sesquiterpenes (C₁₅)** and **triterpenes (C₃₀)**, plus **ubiquinones** and **sterols**. **The MEP (Non-mevalonate / DXP) Pathway (Plastidic):** 1. **Initiation:** Condensation of **pyruvate + glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P)** to form **1-deoxy-D-xylulose-5-phosphate (DXP)**. 2. **Rearrangement:** DXP is reduced and cyclized to **2-C-methyl-D-erythritol-4-phosphate (MEP)**. 3. **Activation:** MEP undergoes a series of phosphorylations and cyclizations to yield **IPP and DMAPP**. 4. **Output:** Primarily produces **monoterpenes (C₁₀)**, **diterpenes (C₂₀)**, and **tetraterpenes/carotenoids (C₄₀)**. **Comparative Summary Table:** | Feature | MVA Pathway | MEP Pathway | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Localization** | Cytosol / peroxisome | Plastid | | **Starting substrate** | Acetyl-CoA | Pyruvate + G3P | | **Key enzyme** | HMG-CoA reductase | DXP reductoisomerase | | **Major products** | Sesqui-, Triterpenes | Mono-, Di-, Tetraterpenes | | **Statin target?** | Yes (cholesterol control) | No | **Mark allocation:** - MVA pathway with key intermediates → 3 marks - MEP pathway with key intermediates → 3 marks - Localization and product comparison → 1 mark --- ### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Set B]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the chemistry, classification, and food sources of **carotenoids (tetraterpenoids)**. Explain the difference between **provitamin A** and **non-provitamin A** carotenoids with examples. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Describe the **Lambert-Beer Law**. A tomato extract dissolved in hexane shows an absorbance of **0.525** at 503 nm in a 1-cm cuvette. Given ε = **1.72 × 10⁵ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹** for lycopene, calculate (i) the molar concentration and (ii) the concentration in mg·L⁻¹ (Molar mass of lycopene = 536.87 g·mol⁻¹). **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Answer to Question B(a) **Chemistry and Classification of Carotenoids:** Carotenoids are **C₄₀ tetraterpenoids** (n = 8) biosynthesized from the condensation of two **geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate (GGPP, C₂₀)** molecules via the enzyme **phytoene synthase**. **Two structural sub-classes:** 1. **Carotenes** — pure hydrocarbons (no oxygen): - **β-Carotene** (provitamin A) — carrot, sweet potato, mango - **Lycopene** (non-provitamin A, acyclic) — tomato, watermelon, guava - **α-Carotene** (provitamin A) — pumpkin, squash 2. **Xanthophylls** — oxygenated derivatives: - **Lutein** — spinach, kale (non-provitamin A) - **Zeaxanthin** — corn, egg yolk (non-provitamin A) - **β-Cryptoxanthin** — papaya, orange (provitamin A) - **Capsanthin** — red chilli (non-provitamin A) **Provitamin A vs Non-Provitamin A:** | Property | Provitamin A Carotenoids | Non-Provitamin A | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **β-ionone ring requirement** | At least one unsubstituted β-ionone ring | No β-ionone ring | | **Vitamin A conversion** | Yes (cleaved by BCO1 enzyme) | No | | **Examples** | β-carotene, α-carotene, β-cryptoxanthin | Lycopene, lutein, zeaxanthin | | **Conversion efficiency (per RE formula)** | β-carotene ÷ 12, others ÷ 24 | 0 (no conversion) | **Mark allocation:** - Correct C₄₀ origin and chemistry → 2 marks - Carotenes vs xanthophylls → 2 marks - Provitamin A distinction with examples → 3 marks --- #### Model Answer to Question B(b) **The Lambert-Beer Law:** $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ Where: - $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless) - $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹) - $c$ = molar concentration (mol·L⁻¹) - $l$ = path length (cm) **Part (i) — Molar Concentration:** Rearranging: $$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l}$$ Substituting the given values: $$c = \frac{0.525}{(1.72 \times 10^5) \times (1.0)}$$ $$c = 3.05 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol·L}^{-1}$$ **Part (ii) — Concentration in mg·L⁻¹:** $$c_{\text{mg/L}} = c_{\text{mol/L}} \times M \times 1000$$ $$c_{\text{mg/L}} = 3.05 \times 10^{-6} \times 536.87 \times 1000$$ $$c_{\text{mg/L}} = 1.64\ \text{mg·L}^{-1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The tomato extract contains **3.05 × 10⁻⁶ mol·L⁻¹** of lycopene, equivalent to **1.64 mg·L⁻¹**. **Mark allocation:** - Lambert-Beer statement and formula → 2 marks - Correct substitution → 2 marks - Correct final molar concentration → 1 mark - Correct mg·L⁻¹ conversion → 2 marks --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Submit** > > 1. **Carbon count blunders:** Students frequently write "monoterpenoids = C₁₅" or "sesquiterpenes = C₁₀". Always re-derive: n × 5 = carbons. > 2. **Mixing MVA and MEP localizations:** MVA is **cytosolic**, MEP is **plastidic**. Reversing these costs full marks. > 3. **Forgetting units in Lambert-Beer:** If you write the final concentration without units (mol·L⁻¹ or mg·L⁻¹), you lose 1 mark in the numerical part. > 4. **β-Carotene vs lycopene:** β-Carotene has **provitamin A** activity; lycopene does **NOT**. Many students wrongly credit lycopene with Vitamin A conversion. > 5. **Conversion factor trap:** RE ÷ **12** is reserved for β-carotene alone. For all other provitamin carotenoids (α-carotene, β-cryptoxanthin), the divisor is **24**. > 6. **Mermaid / diagram mislabeling:** When asked to draw the classification, label **every branch** with both **n value** and **carbon count**. A diagram with only one label will earn partial credit at best. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this high-density checklist for last-hour revision before the KTU ESE. - ☐ **Terpenoids = (C₅H₈)ₙ** — derived from **isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene)** units. - ☐ **Isoprene Rule (Ružička)** — head-to-tail (1,4) linkage; carbon count always a **multiple of 5**. - ☐ Biological isoprene unit = **Isopentenyl Pyrophosphate (IPP)**; allylic isomer = **DMAPP**. - ☐ **Two biosynthetic pathways:** - **Mevalonate (MVA)** → cytosol → sesquiterpenes (C₁₅), triterpenes (C₃₀). - **MEP / DXP** → plastid → monoterpenes (C₁₀), diterpenes (C₂₀), tetraterpenes (C₄₀). - ☐ Classification mnemonic: **H-M-S-D-S-T-T-P** → **C₅-C₁₀-C₁₅-C₂₀-C₂₅-C₃₀-C₄₀-(C₅)ₙ**. - ☐ **Limonene** (citrus), **Menthol** (mint), **Geraniol** (rose) = monoterpenoids. - ☐ **Zingiberene** (ginger), **Farnesol** (berries) = sesquiterpenoids. - ☐ **Cafestol** (coffee), **Steviol glycosides** (stevia) = diterpenoids. - ☐ **Squalene** (olive oil), **Ginsenosides** (ginseng) = triterpenoids. - ☐ **Carotenoids** = tetraterpenoids (C₄₀). - **Provitamin A:** β-carotene, α-carotene, β-cryptoxanthin. - **Non-provitamin A:** lycopene, lutein, zeaxanthin, capsanthin. - ☐ **Retinol Equivalent (RE)** formula: β-carotene/12 + other provitamin carotenoids/24. - ☐ **Lambert-Beer Law:** $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ → rearrange $c = A / (\varepsilon l)$. - ☐ HDI formula: $\text{HDI} = (2C + 2 - H)/2$ — used to confirm number of double bonds. - ☐ **First committed step of MVA pathway** is catalyzed by **HMG-CoA reductase** — the statin drug target. - ☐ **First committed step of MEP pathway** is catalyzed by **DXP reductoisomerase**. - ☐ Squalene (C₃₀) is the biochemical precursor of **cholesterol** via the steroid biosynthesis pathway. - ☐ **Ginsenosides** are triterpenoid saponins with adaptogenic (anti-stress) properties. - ☐ **Steviol glycosides** are ~300× sweeter than sucrose — natural non-caloric sweetener. ---

topics.title: Glucosinolates, Alkaloids, and Seed Storage Proteins - Capsaicinoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Glucosinolates, Alkaloids, and Seed Storage Proteins - Capsaicinoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Capsaicinoids** are a class of alkaloid-like secondary metabolites belonging to the **vanilloid family** of phytochemicals. Chemically, they are acid amides of vanillylamine (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzylamine) coupled with a C9–C11 branched-chain fatty acid. They are the principal pungent (spicy) principles found exclusively in plants of the genus *Capsicum* (chili peppers), accumulated primarily in the placental tissue (the white spongy tissue to which seeds attach) of the fruit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Capsaicinoids are classified under plant **alkaloids** along with caffeine, theobromine, and solanine, but are often termed *pseudo-alkaloids* because their nitrogen atom is not derived from an amino acid precursor in the classical sense — it comes via phenylalanine via a transamination pathway.

The five major naturally occurring capsaicinoids, in descending order of abundance, are:

1. **Capsaicin** (trans-8-methyl-N-vanillyl-6-nonenamide) — ~69% of total
2. **Dihydrocapsaicin** — ~22%
3. **Nordihydrocapsaicin** — ~7%
4. **Homocapsaicin** — ~1%
5. **Homodihydrocapsaicin** — ~1%

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Chili Fire Alarm"**
> Imagine your tongue as a building equipped with smoke detectors (TRPV1 heat-pain receptors). Capsaicinoids are like mischievous pranksters who release a non-toxic smoke (chemical mimicry of heat) that fools every detector in the building into screaming "FIRE! FIRE!" — even though no actual flame exists. Your brain, trusting the alarm, releases endorphins (natural painkillers) and triggers sweating, salivation, and tears. The "alarm" cannot easily be silenced — only full-fat milk (containing the fat-soluble binding protein **casein**) can "absorb" the prankster and stop the false alarm. Water, being polar, just spreads the prankster around and worsens the sensation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Molecular formula of capsaicin:** C₁₈H₂₇NO₃
- **Molar mass:** **305.41 g/mol**
- **Melting point:** **62–65 °C**
- **Solubility:** Highly lipophilic (fat-soluble); poorly soluble in water at neutral pH
- **Pungency unit (standard metric):** **Scoville Heat Unit (SHU)** — measured organoleptically by the Scoville Organoleptic Test (1912), now often replaced by **HPLC quantification in ppm** (parts per million) of total capsaicinoids, converted via:
  
  $$\text{SHU} = \text{ppm of total capsaicinoids} \times 15$$

- **Reference pungency benchmark:** Pure capsaicin = **16,000,000 SHU**; *Capsicum annuum* (bell pepper) = **0 SHU**; *Capsicum frutescens* (Tabasco) = **30,000–50,000 SHU**; Bhut Jolokia = **>1,000,000 SHU**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SHU is a "human-test" metric:** The original Scoville test was a dilution test — a panel of tasters diluted chili extract in sugar water until 3 out of 5 could no longer detect heat. The dilution factor equals the SHU. Modern HPLC methods give objective ppm values, which the conversion factor ×15 translates into Scoville units for industry reporting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Capsaicinoid

Every capsaicinoid molecule is built from two modular halves joined by an **amide (peptide) bond**:

| Region | Chemical Identity | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Head (Aromatic Ring)** | Vanillyl group (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzyl) | Phenylpropanoid pathway |
| **Linker** | Amide bond (–CO–NH–) | Condensation reaction |
| **Tail (Acyl Chain)** | C9–C11 branched-chain fatty acid | Branched-chain amino acid catabolism |

The differences among the five capsaicinoids arise *only* in the **acyl tail**, while the vanillyl head remains constant. For example:
- **Capsaicin:** has a *trans*-6-enyl branch with an iso-methyl group (derived from leucine/valine pathway).
- **Dihydrocapsaicin:** identical but **saturated** (no double bond) — slightly less pungent.
- **Nordihydrocapsaicin:** one carbon shorter (C10 acyl).

## 2.2 Biosynthesis Pathway — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The pathway elegantly **converges two primary metabolic streams** — the phenylpropanoid pathway and the branched-chain fatty acid pathway — through the enzyme **Capsaicin Synthase (CS)** (also called Putative Aminotransferase, PAT), which performs the final amide condensation.

### Stepwise Flow:

1. **Phenylalanine** (shikimate pathway) → **Cinnamic acid** (PAL enzyme) → *p*-**Coumaric acid** → **Caffeic acid** → **Ferulic acid** → **Vanillin** → **Vanillylamine** (via transamination by PAT).
2. **Valine / Leucine** (catabolism) → **Isobutyryl-CoA / Isovaleryl-CoA** → chain elongation via fatty acid synthase-like enzymes (FatA, FatB, KAS) → **8-Methyl-6-nonenoic acid** (capsaicin precursor acid).
3. **Final condensation:**
   $$\text{Vanillylamine} + \text{8-Methyl-6-nonenoyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{Capsaicin Synthase}} \text{Capsaicin} + \text{CoA-SH}$$

### Why the Placenta? — Tissue Localization Logic

Capsaicinoids are synthesized and stored exclusively in the **epidermal cells of the placental septa**, where they are sequestered in **vacuoles/bladder-like vesicles**. The seeds themselves do not synthesize capsaicinoids — they only carry trace amounts by surface contamination. Removing the placenta removes ~85–95% of pungency.

## 2.3 Mechanism of Pungency — TRPV1 Receptor Binding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Vanilloid Receptor (TRPV1):** Capsaicin is a **selective agonist** of the Transient Receptor Potential Vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) ion channel, also called the "capsaicin receptor," located on nociceptive (pain-sensing) C-fiber neurons and also on the tongue's gustatory neurons.

**Binding mechanism (Why it triggers "burn"):**

1. The **vanillyl head** hydrogen-bonds to specific residues (Arg-491, Glu-570) within the vanilloid-binding pocket of TRPV1.
2. The **lipophilic tail** anchors the molecule into the transmembrane domain of the channel.
3. Channel opening → **influx of Na⁺ and Ca²⁺** ions → neuron depolarization → action potential firing → signal transmitted to brain as pain/heat.
4. Brain releases **substance P** initially, then **endogenous opioids (endorphins)** — producing the paradoxical "pain-pleasure" response.

## 2.4 Stability and Degradation Factors

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Heat (boiling, frying)** | Stable; capsaicinoids resist thermal degradation up to **200 °C** |
| **pH (acidic)** | Stable; preserved in vinegar/pickles |
| **pH (alkaline)** | Hydrolyzed — amide bond cleaved; pungency lost |
| **Light (UV)** | Slow oxidation of the vanillyl ring |
| **Oxidation / Lipoxygenase** | Major cause of pungency loss during post-harvest storage |
| **Water solubility** | Negligible → explains why water does NOT relieve burn |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SHU ↔ ppm conversion | $\text{SHU} = \text{ppm}_{\text{cap}} \times 15$ | 15 = standard Scoville factor |
| 2 | Capsaicin molar mass | $M = 305.41$ | g/mol |
| 3 | Molecular formula | $\text{C}_{18}\text{H}_{27}\text{NO}_3$ | — |
| 4 | Melting point | $T_m = 62\text{–}65$ | °C |
| 5 | Pungency index (relative) | $\text{Capsaicin} \approx 1.0$; $\text{Dihydrocapsaicin} \approx 0.85$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 6 | Heat threshold for TRPV1 | $T \geq 43\,°\text{C}$ activates TRPV1 physiologically | capsaicin activates it at <43 °C |
| 7 | Deactivation temp | Capsaicinoids stable to ~200 °C | above this, oxidative degradation dominates |
| 8 | Average abundance in chili | Capsaicin 69 %, Dihydrocapsaicin 22 %, others 9 % | % of total capsaicinoids |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Food industry:** Standardized capsaicinoid extracts (e.g., **Capsaicin 95% HPLC**, sold under trade names) used as flavoring, in self-heating ready-meals, and in defense sprays (e.g., OC sprays at 2,000,000 SHU).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Topical **capsaicin creams (0.025%–0.1%)** for neuropathic pain (post-herpetic neuralgia, diabetic neuropathy); **Qutenza® (8% patch)** for chronic pain.
- **Agro-engineering:** Breeding programs select cultivars using **HPLC quantification** rather than subjective tasting to standardize pungency for commercial cultivars.
- **Defense/Military:** Oleoresin capsicum (OC) is the active in pepper sprays; its microencapsulation is an active food/pharma engineering challenge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of HPLC ppm to Scoville Heat Units

### Given:
- A sample of chili extract, analyzed by HPLC, shows a **total capsaicinoid concentration** of $C_{\text{cap}} = 800 \text{ ppm}$.

### To Find:
- The corresponding pungency in **Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:

> [!NOTE]
> The Scoville Heat Unit (SHU) is **historically defined as the number of times a chili extract must be diluted in sugar-water for the pungency to become undetectable to the human tongue**. The factor 15 arises because human detection threshold for total capsaicinoids is approximately $1$ ppm in solution (i.e., a 1:1,000,000 dilution). To make 1 ppm undetectable, we must dilute by $\sim 15\times$ the capsaicinoid concentration.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Recall the standard Scoville conversion equation} \\
\text{SHU} &= C_{\text{cap}} \times k \\
\text{where } k &= 15 \text{ (the Scoville conversion factor)} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Substitute the measured concentration} \\
\text{SHU} &= 800 \, \text{ppm} \times 15 \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Compute the numerical result} \\
\text{SHU} &= 12{,}000 \\
\\
\text{Step 4: State the final answer with units} \\
\text{The pungency} &= 12{,}000 \, \text{SHU}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to a moderately hot chili, comparable to a **Cayenne pepper** (30,000–50,000 SHU range) being somewhat hotter; the example value of 800 ppm reflects a less pungent variety such as a mild jalapeño-type pepper.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Biosynthetic Mass Balance — Theoretical Maximum Capsaicin Yield

### Given:
- A batch of fresh chili fruits: mass $m_{\text{fruit}} = 100 \text{ kg}$.
- Literature value for capsaicin content in *placental tissue*: $0.5\%$ w/w on a fresh-weight basis.

### To Find:
- Theoretical maximum mass of pure capsaicin recoverable, $m_{\text{cap}}$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the pungency-bearing tissue} \\
\text{Placental tissue is } \sim 10\% \text{ of total fruit mass.} \\
\therefore \, m_{\text{placenta}} &= 0.10 \times 100 \,\text{kg} = 10 \,\text{kg} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Apply the capsaicin mass fraction in the placenta} \\
m_{\text{cap}} &= m_{\text{placenta}} \times w_{\text{cap}} \\
m_{\text{cap}} &= 10 \,\text{kg} \times 0.005 \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Compute the numerical result} \\
m_{\text{cap}} &= 0.05 \,\text{kg} = 50 \,\text{g} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Convert to molar quantity using molecular weight} \\
n_{\text{cap}} &= \frac{m_{\text{cap}}}{M_{\text{cap}}} = \frac{50 \,\text{g}}{305.41 \,\text{g/mol}} \\
n_{\text{cap}} &\approx 0.1637 \,\text{mol} \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Express final answer} \\
\text{Theoretical yield} &= 50 \,\text{g of pure capsaicin from 100 kg fresh fruit.} \\
\text{Molar yield} &\approx 0.164 \text{ moles.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Capsaicinoid Comparison Table

| Property | Capsaicin | Dihydrocapsaicin | Nordihydrocapsaicin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Molecular formula** | $\text{C}_{18}\text{H}_{27}\text{NO}_3$ | $\text{C}_{18}\text{H}_{29}\text{NO}_3$ | $\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{27}\text{NO}_3$ |
| **Molar mass (g/mol)** | 305.41 | 307.43 | 293.40 |
| **Acyl chain length** | C10 (with unsaturation) | C10 (saturated) | C9 (saturated) |
| **Relative pungency** | 1.00 (reference) | ~0.85 | ~0.90 |
| **% of total capsaicinoids** | ~69 | ~22 | ~7 |
| **TRPV1 binding affinity ($K_i$)** | High | High | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Dihydrocapsaicin have a lower pungency despite chemical similarity?** The *trans*-6 double bond in capsaicin's tail introduces a **rigid kink** that better fits the hydrophobic pocket of TRPV1. Saturation in dihydrocapsaicin allows more rotational freedom, weakening the binding pose. The difference is small (~15%) but measurable by sensory panels.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: TRPV1 Channel Activation Energy Analogy

### Problem:
The activation energy for opening the TRPV1 channel is approximately $E_a \approx 50 \text{ kJ/mol}$ when triggered by heat (≥43 °C). Capsaicin lowers this barrier to approximately $E_a' \approx 20 \text{ kJ/mol}$. **By what factor does capsaicin increase the probability of channel opening at body temperature (37 °C)?**

### Solution using the Arrhenius relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & T = 37\,°\text{C} = 310\,\text{K}; \quad R = 8.314 \,\text{J/(mol·K)} \\
\text{Step 1: Compute the natural log ratio of rate constants} \\
\ln\left(\frac{k'}{k}\right) &= -\frac{1}{RT}\left(E_a' - E_a\right) \\
&= -\frac{(20{,}000 - 50{,}000)}{8.314 \times 310} \\
&= -\frac{-30{,}000}{2577.34} \\
&= +11.64 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Exponentiate to get the fold-increase} \\
\frac{k'}{k} &= e^{11.64} \approx 1.14 \times 10^{5} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: State the answer} \\
\text{Capsaicin increases the channel-opening probability by a factor of} \approx 10^{5} \\
\text{at body temperature — explaining why trace amounts feel intensely "hot."}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capsaicinoid Biosynthesis Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["PHENYLALANINE"] --> node2["Cinnamic Acid"]
    node2 --> node3["p-Coumaric Acid"]
    node3 --> node4["Caffeic Acid"]
    node4 --> node5["Ferulic Acid"]
    node5 --> node6["Vanillin"]
    node6 --> node7["Vanillylamine"]
    node8["VALINE LEUCINE"] --> node9["Branched Chain Acyl-CoA"]
    node9 --> node10["8-Methyl-6-nonenoyl-CoA"]
    node7 --> node11["CAPSAICIN SYNTHASE ENZYME"]
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12["CAPSAICIN STORED IN PLACENTAL VACUOLES"]
    node12 --> node13["TRPV1 RECEPTOR BINDING ON TONGUE"]
    node13 --> node14["PAIN SIGNAL TO BRAIN"]
    node14 --> node15["ENDORPHIN RELEASE AND PUNGENCY PERCEPTION"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart visualizes the convergence of two metabolic streams — the **phenylpropanoid pathway** (left vertical chain) and the **branched-chain amino acid catabolism** (right vertical chain) — into the enzyme **Capsaicin Synthase**, which performs the final amide condensation. The product is stored in placental vacuoles and then exerts biological action via TRPV1 receptor binding.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanism of Pungency Perception

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Capsaicinoid Molecule Ingested"] --> B["Dissolves in Salivary Lipids"]
    B --> C["Reaches Lingual Epithelium"]
    C --> D["Diffuses into TRPV1 Channel on C-Fiber Neuron"]
    D --> E["H-Bond: Vanillyl Head to Arg-491 Glu-570"]
    D --> F["Hydrophobic Insertion: Acyl Tail in TM Domain"]
    E --> G["Channel Opens Ca2 Na2 Influx"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Neuron Depolarization Action Potential"]
    H --> I["Trigeminal Nerve Signal to Thalamus"]
    I --> J["Cortex Interprets as Burn Pain"]
    J --> K["Hypothalamus Triggers Sweating and Salivation"]
```
