## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define tropane alkaloids. Name two plants of the Solanaceae family that biosynthesize atropine. State the IUPAC name of atropine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Tropane alkaloids are a class of nitrogen-containing plant secondary metabolites characterized by a bicyclic **8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane (tropane) ring system**, in which a nitrogen bridge connects carbons 1 and 5 of a seven-membered ring.
2. **Two source plants (1 mark):** *Atropa belladonna* L. (deadly nightshade) and *Datura stramonium* L. (jimsonweed). [Also accept: *Hyoscyamus niger* L., *Duboisia myoporoides*].
3. **IUPAC name (1 mark):** 8-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octan-3-yl 3-hydroxy-2-phenylpropanoate.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between L-hyoscyamine and atropine? Why is this distinction pharmacologically important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **L-hyoscyamine (1 mark):** It is the **natural, levorotatory, optically pure (−)-enantiomer** (S-configuration at the benzylic carbon) that is the exclusive alkaloid present in fresh plant tissue.
- **Atropine (1 mark):** It is the **racemic mixture of D-(+)- and L-(−)-hyoscyamine**, formed by thermal or base-catalyzed racemization during isolation, drying, or extraction.
- **Pharmacological importance (1 mark):** The L-isomer is **approximately 100× more potent** than the D-isomer at muscarinic receptors. Therefore, racemic atropine possesses **only ~50% of the antimuscarinic activity** of an equal mass of pure L-hyoscyamine, and clinical doses must be doubled to achieve equivalent effects.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]**
**(a)** Describe the complete biosynthetic pathway of atropine in *Atropa belladonna*, starting from the primary amino acid precursor. Highlight the key enzymes and intermediate structures. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the molecular mechanism of action of atropine at muscarinic receptors with a labeled diagram. Discuss its organ-specific effects. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The biosynthesis of atropine begins in the **root parenchyma cells** and proceeds through the following orchestrated steps:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The primary amino acid precursor is **L-ornithine**, which undergoes **stereospecific α-decarboxylation** catalyzed by **ornithine decarboxylase (ODC, PLP-dependent)** to yield the diamine **putrescine (1,4-diaminobutane)**.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Putrescine is **N-methylated** by *S*-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM) in a reaction catalyzed by **putrescine N-methyltransferase (PMT)**, producing **N-methylputrescine**.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Diamine oxidase (DAO, copper-containing)** performs an oxidative deamination on the *N*-methylputrescine, forming the reactive aldehyde **4-methylaminobutanal**, which spontaneously **cyclizes** via an intramolecular Schiff base to give the **N-methyl-Δ¹-pyrrolinium cation**.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** This cation undergoes a **Mannich-like condensation** with **acetoacetyl-CoA**, mediated by **tropinone synthase**, generating the bicyclic ketone **tropinone** — the universal precursor of all tropane alkaloids.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Tropinone reductase I (NADPH-dependent)** reduces the ketone group of tropinone to a **β-oriented hydroxyl**, yielding **tropine** (3α-tropanol). A competing enzyme, tropinone reductase II, yields pseudotropine.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Tropine is **esterified with (S)-phenyllactic acid** (activated as phenyllactyl-CoA) by **tropine acyltransferase** to yield the direct precursor **littorine**, which is then converted to **L-hyoscyamine** via a **rearrangement involving the phenylalanine-derived tropoyl moiety** (mechanism still under elucidation — likely a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase step).

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Upon **drying, heating, or alkaline extraction**, the L-isomer **racemizes at the benzylic carbon** of the tropic acid moiety, producing the **DL-racemate = atropine**.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Mechanism of action (4 marks):**

Atropine is a **competitive, reversible, non-selective antagonist of all five subtypes (M₁–M₅) of muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (mAChRs)**. These are **G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs)** with the following downstream signaling:
- M₁, M₃, M₅ → **Gq protein** → ↑ IP₃, DAG, Ca²⁺
- M₂, M₄ → **Gi protein** → ↓ cAMP, ↑ K⁺ efflux

Atropine binds to the **orthosteric (acetylcholine-binding) site** of the receptor with a **Kd ≈ 10⁻⁹ M**, but unlike acetylcholine, it does **not activate the receptor** (zero intrinsic efficacy). Because binding is **competitive and reversible**, the equilibrium:

$$\text{Atropine} + R \rightleftharpoons \text{Atropine} \cdot R \quad (\text{no response})$$

shifts the dose–response curve of acetylcholine **rightward in a parallel manner** (classic Schild equation behavior), requiring more ACh to overcome the blockade.

**Organ-specific effects (3 marks):**

| Organ | Muscarinic receptor | Effect of atropine (antagonism) |
|---|---|---|
| **Heart (SA node)** | M₂ | **Tachycardia** (↑ heart rate by 30–40 bpm) |
| **Eye (iris sphincter)** | M₃ | **Mydriasis** (pupil dilation); cycloplegia |
| **GI tract smooth muscle** | M₃ | **↓ Peristalsis**, constipation, ↓ gastric acid |
| **Bronchial smooth muscle** | M₃ | **Bronchodilation** (used in asthma/COPD) |
| **Salivary, sweat glands** | M₃ | **Dry mouth (xerostomia)**, anhidrosis |
| **Bladder detrusor** | M₃ | **Urinary retention** |
| **CNS (cortex, hippocampus)** | M₁ | **Confusion, delirium, hallucinations** at high dose |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** state atropine acts on "nicotinic receptors" — it is purely **muscarinic selective**.
> - **Do not** describe the action as "irreversible" — it is **competitive and reversible** (unlike, e.g., phenoxybenzamine at α-receptors).
> - **Do not** omit the **M₁–M₅ subtype mapping** — KTU 2024 expects receptor-subtype awareness.
> - **Do not** confuse L-hyoscyamine with atropine in the biosynthetic diagram.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module 4 Choice 2]**
**(a)** Discuss the major **pharmaceutical, food, and forensic applications** of atropine, citing three specific examples for each category. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Outline the **qualitative and quantitative analytical methods** used for the detection of atropine in plant matrices and biological fluids, including the chemistry of the Vitali–Morin test. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Pharmaceutical applications (3 marks):**
1. **Pre-anesthetic medication** (0.4–0.6 mg IV/IM) — to reduce salivary and bronchial secretions and prevent vagal-induced bradycardia during surgery.
2. **Bradycardia and AV block emergency** — first-line treatment of symptomatic bradycardia (1 mg IV bolus, repeatable every 3–5 min).
3. **Organophosphate poisoning antidote** — competitive reversal of acetylcholinesterase-inhibitor toxicity; doses may exceed 100 mg/day, titrated to drying of secretions.
4. **Ophthalmic mydriatic/cycloplegic** — for retinal examination and treatment of uveitis (1% solution).
5. **Antidote to mushroom (muscarine) poisoning** from *Inocybe* and *Clitocybe* species.

**(ii) Food and food-safety applications (2 marks):**
1. **Regulatory compliance** — EU Regulation 2023/915 caps tropane alkaloids (sum of atropine + scopolamine) at **1 µg/kg** in cereal-based baby foods and **25 µg/kg** in herbal teas; HPLC-MS/MS is the official reference method.
2. **Authentication of herbal raw materials** — fingerprinting by TLC/HPLC to confirm absence of *Datura* adulteration in commercial herbal teas, weight-loss products, and ayurvedic formulations.
3. **Historical food use** — in medieval Italy, women used *belladonna* (literally "beautiful lady") eye-drops to dilate pupils, considered cosmetically desirable (hence the plant name).

**(iii) Forensic and toxicological applications (2 marks):**
1. **Cause-of-death determination** in accidental or intentional *Datura* ingestion; gastric contents analyzed by GC-MS.
2. **Drug-facilitated crime detection** — atropine has been (rarely) used to incapacitate victims; analysis of blood/urine within 24–48 h post-ingestion.
3. **Antemortem clinical forensic** — symptoms of anticholinergic toxidrome (the classic mnemonic: **"Blind as a bat, mad as a hatter, red as a beet, hot as a hare, dry as a bone, and the heart runs alone"**) help in differential diagnosis.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Qualitative methods (3 marks):**

1. **Dragendorff test (1 mark):** Alkaloid + potassium bismuth iodide (KBiI₄) → orange-red precipitate of the alkaloid–bismuth complex. Highly sensitive (detection limit ~ 0.5 µg), but not specific (responds to all alkaloids).

2. **Mayer's test (Reagent: K₂[HgI₄]) (½ mark):** White or cream-colored precipitate with tertiary and quaternary alkaloids.

3. **Wagner's test (Reagent: I₂ in KI) (½ mark):** Brown flocculent precipitate.

4. **Vitali–Morin test — chemistry (1 mark):**
   - **Step 1:** Atropine is **hydrolyzed** with fuming HNO₃ (or H₂SO₄ followed by HNO₃) at 100 °C → the tropic acid ester is cleaved and the **aromatic ring is nitrated** to **3,5-dinitrobenzoic acid** (or its ester derivative).
   - **Step 2:** After evaporation to dryness and dissolution in **acetone**, addition of **ethanolic KOH** produces a **chromophoric Meisenheimer complex** (anionic σ-complex stabilized by the *ortho* and *para* nitro groups).
   - **Observation:** An intense **violet-purple color** (λmax ≈ 550 nm) appears, characteristic of tropane alkaloids containing the **tropic acid moiety**. Scopolamine gives a weaker reaction.

5. **TLC fingerprinting (½ mark):** Silica gel G, mobile phase CHCl₃ : MeOH : NH₃ (85:14:1), Dragendorff spray; atropine Rf ≈ 0.55.

**(ii) Quantitative methods (4 marks):**

1. **UV-Vis spectrophotometry (1 mark):** Direct measurement at 258 nm; ε ≈ 13,300 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹; suitable for pure solutions (A0.1%1cm ≈ 46).

2. **HPLC-UV (USP monograph) (1 mark):** Column C18 (250 × 4.6 mm, 5 µm); mobile phase **0.05 M KH₂PO₄ (pH 3.0) : acetonitrile (70:30)**; flow 1 mL/min; detection at 254 nm; retention time ≈ 6–8 min. **LOD ~ 50 ng/mL, LOQ ~ 150 ng/mL.**

3. **HPLC-MS/MS (1 mark):** Electrospray ionization in positive mode (ESI⁺); precursor ion [M+H]⁺ at *m/z* 290.2; product ion at *m/z* 124.1 (loss of the tropic acid moiety, C₉H₉O₃); collision energy ~ 25 eV. Internal standard: **homatropine** (m/z 276.2 → 142.1). **LOD ~ 0.5 ng/mL** — meets EU regulatory requirements.

4. **GC-MS (1 mark):** Requires prior **derivatization** (silylation with BSTFA + 1% TMCS at 70 °C for 30 min) to volatilize the hydroxyl group. Monitors ions at *m/z* 289 (M⁺), 124 (tropine fragment), 83 (pyrrolidinium). Skatole is the standard internal standard.

5. **Capillary electrophoresis (MEKC) (½ mark):** Resolves enantiomers using cyclodextrin chiral selectors (e.g., hydroxypropyl-β-CD); quantifies L-hyoscyamine vs. D-hyoscyamine vs. atropine.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Analytical Methods Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** confuse the **Dragendorff** reagent (KBiI₄) with **Mayer's** (K₂HgI₄) or **Wagner's** (I₂/KI). KTU specifically tests reagent composition.
> - **Do not** attribute the violet color of the **Vitali–Morin test** to the tropane ring — it arises from the **nitrated aromatic ring of tropic acid**.
> - **Do not** state HPLC-UV LOD as 0.5 ng/mL — that is the **HPLC-MS/MS** value. HPLC-UV is ~ 100× less sensitive.
> - **Do not** forget to specify **internal standard** (homatropine) for quantitative HPLC-MS/MS.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 Module 4 — Atropine: 30-Sec Revision Bullets**
>
> ✅ **Class:** Tropane alkaloid (ornithine-derived); bicyclic 8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane skeleton.
>
> ✅ **Source plants:** *Atropa belladonna*, *Datura stramonium*, *Hyoscyamus niger* (all Solanaceae).
>
> ✅ **IUPAC name:** 8-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octan-3-yl 3-hydroxy-2-phenylpropanoate.
>
> ✅ **Formula & mass:** C₁₇H₂₃NO₃, **289.37 g/mol**; **mp 114–116 °C**; λmax **258 nm**.
>
> ✅ **Natural form:** L-(−)-hyoscyamine (100× more potent than D-isomer); atropine is the **racemic DL-mixture** formed by heat/alkali.
>
> ✅ **Precursor amino acid:** **L-ornithine** → putrescine → tropinone → tropine → hyoscyamine → atropine.
>
> ✅ **Key enzymes:** ODC, PMT, DAO, tropinone reductase I.
>
> ✅ **Mechanism:** **Competitive, reversible, non-selective muscarinic antagonist** (M₁–M₅); zero efficacy at nicotinic receptors.
>
> ✅ **Clinical organ effects:** **Tachycardia (M₂), mydriasis + cycloplegia (M₃), xerostomia (M₃), bronchodilation (M₃), urinary retention, ↓ GI motility, central delirium (M₁).**
>
> ✅ **Major uses:** Pre-anesthetic, bradycardia reversal, organophosphate poisoning antidote, ophthalmic mydriatic, antimuscarinic antidote.
>
> ✅ **Toxidrome mnemonic:** *"Blind as a bat, mad as a hatter, red as a beet, hot as a hare, dry as a bone, heart runs alone."*
>
> ✅ **Antidote to overdose:** **Physostigmine** (tertiary amine, crosses BBB, inhibits AChE → ↑ ACh at synapse).
>
> ✅ **EU regulatory limit (2023/915):** ≤ **1 µg/kg** in cereal-based baby foods; ≤ **25 µg/kg** in herbal teas.
>
> ✅ **Vitali–Morin test:** HNO₃ nitration of tropic acid aromatic ring → **violet Meisenheimer complex** in acetone–KOH.
>
> ✅ **Dragendorff reagent:** **KBiI₄** → orange-red alkaloid–bismuth precipitate.
>
> ✅ **HPLC-MS/MS quantification ion transition:** *m/z* **290.2 → 124.1**; internal standard: **homatropine**.
>
> ✅ **GC-MS requirement:** **Derivatization with BSTFA + 1% TMCS** before injection (OH → OSi(CH₃)₃).

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden KTU Mnemonic for M-receptor effects of atropine:**
> **"Atropine ANTI-M's"** — **A**nhidrosis · **N**o miosis (mydriasis) · **T**achycardia · **I**ntestinal ileus (no peristalsis) · **M**emory loss (central) · **'**s (suffix for "dry secretions")

topics.title: Hyoscyamine and Scopolamine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hyoscyamine and Scopolamine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Hyoscyamine and Scopolamine?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Hyoscyamine* and *Scopolamine* are **tropane alkaloids** — a structurally unique class of nitrogen-containing secondary metabolites — produced predominantly by plants of the family **Solanaceae** (notably *Atropa belladonna*, *Datura stramonium*, *Hyoscyamus niger*, and *Duboisia* species). They are pharmacologically classified as **anticholinergic (antimuscarinic) agents** that competitively block the neurotransmitter **acetylcholine** at muscarinic receptor sites in the parasympathetic nervous system.

| Property | Hyoscyamine | Scopolamine (Hyoscine) |
|---|---|---|
| **Molecular Formula** | C₁₇H₂₃NO₃ | C₁₇H₂₁NO₄ |
| **Molar Mass** | **289.37 g/mol** | **303.36 g/mol** |
| **Stereochemistry** | (S)-(–)-isomer is the natural form | (S)-(–)-isomer is the natural form |
| **Structural Feature** | Single ester bridge between tropine & tropic acid | Contains an additional **6,7-epoxide bridge** |
| **Optical Rotation** | [α]D²⁰ = –21.0° (in ethanol) | [α]D²⁰ = –18° to –21° |
| **Primary Plant Source** | *Atropa belladonna* (Deadly Nightshade) | *Datura metel* / *Duboisia myoporoides* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Atropine vs Hyoscyamine — a frequently confused pair!**
> The natural plant product is **(S)-(–)-hyoscyamine**. During extraction, it readily **racemizes** at the tropic acid chiral centre, giving a 50:50 mixture of (R) and (S) forms — this racemate is called **Atropine**. Pharmacologically, only the (S)-enantiomer is biologically active; hence **natural hyoscyamine is roughly twice as potent as atropine**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lock and Key Jammer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy**
> Think of **acetylcholine (ACh)** as a perfectly-shaped **key** that fits into a **muscarinic receptor lock** on parasympathetic organs (heart, gut, glands, eyes). When ACh inserts and turns, it triggers salivation, slows the heart, contracts gut muscles, and constricts pupils.
>
> **Hyoscyamine and Scopolamine act like a *broken duplicate key*** — they slide into the same muscarinic lock but **jam the mechanism** and refuse to turn. Because they block the real key (ACh) from binding, the parasympathetic signals are silenced. The result:
> - **Pupils dilate** (mydriasis) — useful for eye examinations
> - **Heart rate rises** — useful in bradycardia
> - **Secretions dry up** — useful as a pre-anaesthetic
> - **Gut motility slows** — useful in irritable bowel / travel sickness
>
> **Scopolamine** is a *more advanced, better-fitted broken key* — it additionally crosses the **blood-brain barrier (BBB)** more easily, allowing it to act on the central nervous system (CNS), producing sedation, amnesia, and anti-nausea effects.

## 1.3 Why Are They Considered "Phytochemicals in Food"?

Even though these alkaloids are **toxic at high doses**, they appear in trace amounts in some edible/medicinal plants and herbal teas (e.g., *Datura* leaves misused in folk remedies). In the food and pharmaceutical industries they are:
- **Controlled additives** in extremely regulated doses
- **Lead molecules** for synthetic anticholinergic drug design
- **Biotechnological products** of *in vitro* plant cell culture (e.g., hairy root cultures of *Datura*, *Hyoscyamus*) for sustainable industrial production.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tropane Skeleton — Structural Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **tropane nucleus** is a bicyclic structure formed by a **piperidine ring fused with a pyrrolidine ring** that shares two nitrogen-adjacent carbon atoms. The IUPAC name is **8-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane**. The **N-methyl group at position 8** and the **3-position hydroxyl** (as part of an ester) are the critical pharmacophoric anchors.

$$\text{Tropane Core: 8-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane}$$

- **N at position 8** → accepts proton (basic, pK_a ≈ 9.5)
- **C-3 (–OH)** → forms the **ester bond** with an organic acid
- **C-6 / C-7** → in **scopolamine**, an **epoxide** (oxirane) ring closes here

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the tropane chiral?**
> The **tropine** alcohol part is symmetric and achiral. Chirality in hyoscyamine arises from the **tropic acid** moiety: the α-carbon of tropic acid carries –H, –OH, –CH₂OH, –C₆H₅, and the ester oxygen — four different groups → **one stereocentre** → (R) or (S). Plants produce exclusively the **(S)-enantiomer**.

## 2.2 Detailed Structural Comparison

**Hyoscyamine** = Tropine (bicyclic amino alcohol) esterified at the C-3 hydroxyl with **tropic acid** (2-phenyl-3-hydroxypropanoic acid).

$$\text{Hyoscyamine: Tropine–O–C(=O)–CH(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)(CH}_2\text{OH)}$$

**Scopolamine** = Same ester framework, but the tropine part is replaced by **scopine** (i.e., tropine with an additional **6,7-epoxy bridge**).

$$\text{Scopolamine: Scopine–O–C(=O)–CH(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)(CH}_2\text{OH)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Epoxide Bridge — the structural twist that changes pharmacology**
> The 6,7-epoxide in scopolamine makes the molecule **more rigid, more polar, and a stronger CNS penetrant**. This single oxygen bridge is responsible for scopolamine's added sedative, anti-emetic, and amnestic properties that hyoscyamine lacks.

## 2.3 Mechanism of Action — At the Molecular Level

Both alkaloids are **competitive, reversible antagonists** of **muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (M₁–M₅)**. They bind to the orthosteric site of the receptor with:
- High affinity for M₁, M₃, M₅ subtypes
- Lower affinity for M₂ and M₄

$$\text{ACh + Receptor} \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} \text{ACh–Receptor (active)} \rightarrow \text{Biological Response}$$

$$\text{Alkaloid + Receptor} \xrighttharpoons[k_{-2}]{k_2} \text{Alkaloid–Receptor (inactive) — no response}$$

The **dissociation constant K_d** governs potency: lower K_d = tighter binding = more potent antagonist. Scopolamine typically has a **2–3× lower K_d** for CNS muscarinic receptors than hyoscyamine, accounting for its stronger central effects.

## 2.4 Physiological Effects by Organ System

| Organ/System | Effect of ACh (Normal) | Effect of Hyoscyamine | Effect of Scopolamine |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eye (iris)** | Pupil constriction (miosis) | **Mydriasis** (dilation) | Mydriasis + cycloplegia |
| **Heart (SA node)** | Slows heart rate (negative chronotropy) | **Tachycardia** | Mild tachycardia |
| **Salivary glands** | Stimulates salivation | **Dry mouth (xerostomia)** | Dry mouth (strong) |
| **GI tract** | ↑ peristalsis, secretions | **↓ motility, constipation** | ↓ motility, anti-spasmodic |
| **Bladder** | Contracts detrusor | **Retention of urine** | Retention of urine |
| **Sweat glands** | Stimulates sweating | **Anhidrosis** (hot, dry skin) | Anhidrosis |
| **CNS** | Learning, memory | Minimal (poor BBB) | **Sedation, amnesia, anti-emesis** |
| **Bronchi** | Bronchoconstriction | **Bronchodilation** | Bronchodilation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Classical mnemonics for antimuscarinic effects:**
> *"Red as a beet, dry as a bone, hot as a hare, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter"* — describes classic belladonna (atropine/hyoscyamine) poisoning: cutaneous vasodilation, anhidrosis, hyperthermia, mydriasis, delirium.

## 2.5 Natural Occurrence — Where Are They Found?

**Plant family: Solanaceae**, subfamily **Solanoideae**, tribe **Hyoscyameae** and **Datureae**.

| Plant | Common Name | Hyoscyamine (%) | Scopolamine (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Atropa belladonna* | Deadly Nightshade | **0.3 – 0.6** | 0.01 – 0.05 |
| *Datura stramonium* | Thorn Apple | 0.2 – 0.5 | **0.3 – 0.5** |
| *Hyoscyamus niger* | Black Henbane | 0.05 – 0.1 | 0.02 – 0.07 |
| *Duboisia myoporoides* | Corkwood | 0.2 – 0.4 | **1.0 – 2.5** (highest!) |
| *Scopolia carniolica* | Scopolia | 0.1 – 0.3 | 0.2 – 0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Duboisia myoporoides* is the **world's principal commercial source of scopolamine**, cultivated in Australia, and contains up to **2.5% scopolamine** in dried leaves — making it the richest natural source known.

## 2.6 Food & Pharmaceutical Applications

1. **Pre-anaesthetic medication** — scopolamine reduces salivary/bronchial secretions before surgery
2. **Motion sickness prophylaxis** — scopolamine transdermal patches (e.g., Transderm Scōp®, 1 mg/72 h)
3. **Ophthalmology** — atropine/hyoscyamine for mydriasis and cycloplegia; **atropine 0.01%** eye drops for childhood myopia control
4. **Bradycardia treatment** — IV atropine in emergency ACLS protocol
5. **Organophosphate poisoning antidote** — large doses of atropine
6. **Gastrointestinal antispasmodic** — for IBS, peptic ulcer adjunct
7. **Pesticide formulation** — natural insect antifeedants
8. **Cholinergic research tool** — used in labs to probe muscarinic receptor function

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Biosynthesis Pathway of Tropane Alkaloids — Complete Derivation

The tropane alkaloid skeleton is assembled via a **hybrid pathway** that draws intermediates from both the **ornithine/arginine** route (for the N-containing ring) and the **phenylalanine** route (for the aromatic tropic acid side chain), plus an **acetate-derived** acetoacetyl unit that fuses them.

### Step 1 — Formation of Putrescine (the diamine precursor)

The pyrrolidine ring of tropane ultimately derives from **ornithine** (and to a lesser extent arginine), catalysed by **ornithine decarboxylase (ODC)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ornithine} &\xrightarrow{\text{ODC, PLP}} \text{Putrescine} + \text{CO}_2 \\
\text{Ornithine: } &\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_3\text{–CH(NH}_2\text{)–COOH} \\
\text{Putrescine: } &\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–NH}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Decarboxylation removes one carbon (CO₂) and one negative charge, opening up the nitrogen for subsequent N-methylation.

### Step 2 — N-Methylation of Putrescine

The enzyme **putrescine N-methyltransferase (PMT)** transfers a methyl group from **S-adenosylmethionine (SAM)** to one of the terminal nitrogens:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Putrescine} + \text{SAM} &\xrightarrow{\text{PMT}} \text{N-Methylputrescine} + \text{SAH} \\
\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–NH}_2 &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–NHCH}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** N-methylation is a **rate-limiting, branch-point step** that commits the plant to tropane-alkaloid biosynthesis rather than polyamine pathways.

### Step 3 — Oxidative Deamination to the N-Methyl-Δ¹-Pyrrolinium Cation

The **diamine oxidase (DAO)** removes the *unmethylated* amine, generating an iminium ion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{N-Methylputrescine} &\xrightarrow{\text{DAO, O}_2, \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{N-Methyl-}\Delta^1\text{-pyrrolinium}^+ \\
&\quad + \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The product is a 5-membered cyclic iminium salt that will form the **pyrrolidine half** of the tropane bicycle.

### Step 4 — Condensation with Acetoacetyl-CoA — Tropinone Formation

**Acetoacetyl-CoA** (from condensation of two acetyl-CoA units) donates a 4-carbon unit. The enzyme **tropinone synthase** (a Type III polyketide synthase) catalyses a Mannich-like cyclisation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{N-Methyl-}\Delta^1\text{-pyrrolinium}^+ &+ \text{Acetoacetyl-CoA} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Tropinone synthase}} \text{Tropinone} + \text{CoA}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tropinone: } &8\text{-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octan-3-one} \\
&\text{(C}_8\text{H}_{13}\text{NO, } M = 139.20 \text{ g/mol)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the *signature* step in which the **piperidine ring** is closed onto the pyrrolidine, generating the bicyclic tropane framework.

### Step 5 — Reduction of Tropinone to Tropine (and Pseudotropine)

Two stereospecific reductases control the stereochemistry at C-3:

| Enzyme | Cofactor | C-3 OH stereochemistry | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tropinone reductase I (TRI)** | NADPH | **3α-OH** (tropine, "telo") | Tropine |
| **Tropinone reductase II (TRII)** | NADPH | **3β-OH** (pseudotropine) | Pseudotropine |

$$
\text{Tropinone} \xrightarrow{\text{TRI, NADPH}} \text{Tropine (3α-OH)}
$$

> **Logic:** Only **3α-tropine** is the correct substrate for hyoscyamine/scopolamine esterification; the 3β-pseudotropine branch leads to different alkaloids (e.g., calystegins).

### Step 6 — Tropic Acid Side-Chain Biosynthesis (Phenylalanine Route)

Tropic acid is the **phenylalanine-derived aromatic acid**. The route:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phenylalanine} &\xrightarrow{\text{PAL, C4H, 4CL}} \text{Phenyllactate} \\
\text{Phenyllactate} &\xrightarrow{\text{2-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydratase}} \text{Phenylpyruvate} \\
\text{Phenylpyruvate} &\xrightarrow{\text{reductase}} \text{$(R)$-Phenyllactate} \\
\text{$(R)$-Phenyllactate} &\xrightarrow{\text{CYP80F1, CoA-ligase}} \text{Tropoyl-CoA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A *rare* biological **1,2-shift of the carboxyl group** (a **NIH-type rearrangement**) in the conversion of phenylacetate → tropate is the unique chemical step catalysed by **CYP80F1**, a cytochrome P450 enzyme that defines tropic acid biosynthesis.

### Step 7 — Esterification — Hyoscyamine Assembly

Tropine + Tropoyl-CoA → Hyoscyamine (littorine is a presumed intermediate, then a 2,3-rearrangement gives the tropate ester).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tropine} + \text{Tropoyl-CoA} &\xrightarrow{\text{tropine acyltransferase}} \\
&\text{$(S)$-Hyoscyamine} + \text{CoA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Strict **stereospecificity** of the acyltransferase ensures only the **(S)-enantiomer** is biosynthesised.

### Step 8 — Epoxidation to Scopolamine

This is the *final divergence* between the two alkaloids:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{$(S)$-Hyoscyamine} &\xrightarrow{\text{Hyoscyamine 6}\beta\text{-hydroxylase (H6H)}} \\
&\text{6}\beta\text{-Hydroxyhyoscyamine} \\
\text{6}\beta\text{-Hydroxyhyoscyamine} &\xrightarrow{\text{H6H (epoxidase)}} \\
&\text{$(S)$-Scopolamine} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bifunctional enzyme **H6H (hyoscyamine 6β-hydroxylase)**, a **2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase**, performs:
1. Stereo-specific hydroxylation at C-6
2. Oxidative cyclisation of the 6-OH with C-7 H to form the 6,7-epoxide

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In *Atropa belladonna*, H6H activity is LOW, so it accumulates hyoscyamine. In *Datura* and *Duboisia*, H6H is HIGH, so scopolamine dominates. **Over-expressing H6H** in hairy-root cultures is the key biotechnological strategy to push production toward scopolamine.

## 3.2 Quantitative Example — Yield Calculation

**Problem:** A 5.00 kg batch of dried *Datura stramonium* leaves is extracted. After purification, 12.50 g of pure scopolamine is obtained. The HPLC assay shows the leaves originally contained 0.30% scopolamine (w/w dry basis). Calculate: (a) theoretical yield, (b) percentage recovery.

> **Given:** $m_{\text{leaves}} = 5.00 \text{ kg} = 5000 \text{ g}$; actual scopolamine recovered $= 12.50 \text{ g}$; leaf scopolamine content $= 0.30\%$

### (a) Theoretical yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{theoretical}} &= m_{\text{leaves}} \times \frac{\%\text{ content}}{100} \\
&= 5000 \text{ g} \times \frac{0.30}{100} \\
&= 5000 \times 0.003 \\
&= 15.00 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** 0.30% means 0.30 g of scopolamine per 100 g of dry leaf, so 5000 g of leaf can yield at most 15 g of scopolamine if extraction is 100% efficient.

### (b) Percentage recovery

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ recovery} &= \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12.50 \text{ g}}{15.00 \text{ g}} \times 100 \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A recovery of ~83% is excellent for plant-alkaloid isolation; losses arise during solvent partitioning, crystallisation, and chromatographic polishing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hyoscyamine vs Scopolamine at a Glance

| Feature | Hyoscyamine | Scopolamine |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant originator** | *Atropa* (dominant) | *Datura* / *Duboisia* (dominant) |
| **Formula** | C₁₇H₂₃NO₃ | C₁₇H₂₁NO₄ |
| **Key structural difference** | 6,7 single bond | 6,7-epoxide bridge |
| **Optical rotation [α]D** | –21° (S) | –18° to –21° (S) |
| **Receptor selectivity** | M₁, M₃ (peripheral) | M₁ (CNS-penetrant) |
| **BBB penetration** | Poor | **Strong** |
| **Onset (oral)** | 30–60 min | 30–60 min |
| **Half-life (t½)** | 3–4 h | 4–6 h (slightly longer) |
| **Central effect** | Negligible at therapeutic dose | **Sedation, amnesia, anti-emetic** |
| **Clinical use** | GI antispasmodic, mydriasis | Motion sickness, anaesthesia, myopia control |
| **Toxicity (LD₅₀ rat, oral)** | ~750 mg/kg | ~1275 mg/kg |
| **Industrial production** | *Atropa* hairy roots | *Duboisia* plantations & cell cultures |

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT417]**
Differentiate between hyoscyamine and scopolamine with respect to their chemical structure and pharmacological action.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Hyoscyamine (C₁₇H₂₃NO₃):** Tropine esterified with tropic acid; lacks the 6,7-epoxide bridge. Primarily a **peripheral** antimuscarinic — mydriasis, tachycardia, drying of secretions. (1½ marks)
> - **Scopolamine (C₁₇H₂₁NO₄):** Scopine (tropine + 6,7-epoxide) esterified with tropic acid. The epoxide increases **CNS penetration**; produces sedation, amnesia, and anti-emesis in addition to peripheral effects. (1½ marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEFTT417]**
Name the chief commercial plant source of scopolamine and the biosynthetic precursor that supplies the tropane nitrogen.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

> - Tropane nitrogen precursor: **Ornithine** (alternatively arginine) → putrescine → N-methylputrescine → N-methyl-Δ¹-pyrrolinium cation. (1½ marks)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 marks]** *Describe the biosynthetic pathway of tropane alkaloids in Solanaceae plants, starting from ornithine up to hyoscyamine. Name the key enzymes and the rate-limiting step.*

> **Model Solution:**
>
> The pathway integrates the **ornithine-derived polyamine route** with the **phenylpropanoid route** for the aromatic acid moiety.
>
> **Step 1 — Decarboxylation of ornithine:**
> Ornithine undergoes PLP-dependent decarboxylation via **ornithine decarboxylase (ODC)** to give putrescine, the diamine precursor of the pyrrolidine ring.
>
> **Step 2 — N-methylation (rate-limiting):**
> **Putrescine N-methyltransferase (PMT)** uses S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) as methyl donor to form **N-methylputrescine**. **This is the committed, rate-limiting step** that diverts the plant's polyamine pool toward tropane alkaloid biosynthesis.
>
> **Step 3 — Oxidative deamination:**
> **Diamine oxidase (DAO)** deaminates the unmethylated amine, yielding the cyclic iminium salt **N-methyl-Δ¹-pyrrolinium cation**, which constitutes the future pyrrolidine ring.
>
> **Step 4 — Mannich-like condensation:**
> The iminium salt condenses with **acetoacetyl-CoA** (derived from 2 × acetyl-CoA) under **tropinone synthase**, a Type III polyketide synthase, to form **tropinone** — the bicyclic tropane skeleton.
>
> **Step 5 — Stereospecific reduction:**
> **Tropinone reductase I (TRI)** reduces tropinone to **(3α)-tropine** using NADPH. The competing enzyme TRII would yield (3β)-pseudotropine, but only 3α-tropine feeds into hyoscyamine.
>
> **Step 6 — Tropic acid biosynthesis (parallel pathway):**
> Phenylalanine → trans-cinnamate → coumarate (via PAL, C4H, 4CL) → phenyllactate → phenylpyruvate → **(R)-phenyllactate** → (via the unusual P450 **CYP80F1** with a 1,2-carboxyl shift) → tropoyl-CoA.
>
> **Step 7 — Final esterification:**
> **Tropine acyltransferase** condenses tropine with tropoyl-CoA to give **(S)-hyoscyamine** — the natural product.
>
> *(1 mark each for naming each enzyme; 1 mark for naming the rate-limiting step; 1 mark for stating the parallel phenylalanine route; 1 mark for the chiral selectivity; balance for logical flow.)*

**(b)** **[7 marks]** *Explain why scopolamine exhibits strong central nervous system (CNS) effects whereas hyoscyamine is essentially a peripheral drug. Support your answer with structural and pharmacokinetic reasoning.*

> **Model Solution:**
>
> 1. **Structural difference** (2 marks): The presence of the **6,7-epoxide bridge** in scopolamine (replacing the –CH₂–CH₂– in hyoscyamine) increases molecular rigidity and introduces an additional **hydrogen-bond acceptor** (the epoxide oxygen). This additional polarity, combined with a slightly more compact 3D shape, favours recognition by the **BBB influx transporter** (likely an organic anion/cation transporter).
>
> 2. **Lipophilicity (log P) and BBB passage** (2 marks): Hyoscyamine's log P ≈ 1.83 makes it "too lipophilic" for optimal passive diffusion; it tends to bind plasma proteins and partition into peripheral tissues. Scopolamine's log P ≈ 0.98 sits in the **optimal BBB window** (0–3), enabling efficient passive diffusion plus active transport across the blood-brain barrier.
>
> 3. **Receptor subtype affinity** (1.5 marks): Scopolamine has 5–10× higher affinity for the **M₁ muscarinic receptor** in the CNS (hippocampus, cortex, vomiting centre) compared with hyoscyamine, due to optimal H-bond geometry with the epoxide oxygen.
>
> 4. **Clinical outcome** (1.5 marks): Scopolamine is therefore used for **CNS indications** — motion sickness (transdermal patch), pre-anaesthetic sedation, and as a research tool for memory/learning studies — whereas hyoscyamine is restricted to **peripheral** uses (GI antispasmodic, ophthalmology).
>
> *(Marking scheme: structure 2 + lipophilicity 2 + receptor 1.5 + clinical 1.5 = 7.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 marks]** *With a neat schematic, describe the chemical classification of hyoscyamine and scopolamine as tropane alkaloids. Compare their pharmacodynamic actions on different organ systems.*

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Classification (2 marks):** Both belong to the **tropane alkaloid** sub-class of the **Solanaceae alkaloids**. The tropane nucleus is **8-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane**, with a tertiary amine (bridgehead N-8) and a C-3 hydroxyl that forms the **ester** with **tropic acid** (2-phenyl-3-hydroxypropanoic acid).
>
> **Structural difference (2 marks):**
> - Hyoscyamine = tropine-3α-yl (S)-tropate (no epoxide).
> - Scopolamine = scopine-3α-yl (S)-tropate (6β,7β-epoxide present).
>
> **Pharmacodynamic comparison by organ system (3 marks):**
>
> | System | Hyoscyamine | Scopolamine |
> |---|---|---|
> | Eye | Mydriasis, cycloplegia | Mydriasis (more prolonged) |
> | Heart | Tachycardia | Tachycardia (slightly weaker) |
> | GI tract | ↓ motility, antispasmodic | ↓ motility |
> | Salivary glands | Dry mouth (strong) | Dry mouth (strong) |
> | CNS | Negligible | **Sedation, amnesia, anti-emesis** |
> | Sweat glands | Anhidrosis | Anhidrosis |
>
> Both are **competitive reversible antagonists at muscarinic (M) receptors**, with high affinity for M₁, M₃, M₅.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** *Discuss the industrial production of scopolamine from plant sources. Include in your answer: (i) preferred plant species, (ii) extraction methodology, (iii) biotechnological alternatives.*

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(i) Preferred plant species (2 marks):** ***Duboisia myoporoides*** and ***Duboisia leichhardtii*** are the world's principal commercial sources, with **scopolamine contents of 1.0–2.5%** of dry-leaf weight. *Datura metel* and *Datura innoxia* are also used, especially in India. These species naturally express high levels of the bifunctional enzyme **hyoscyamine 6β-hydroxylase (H6H)**, which converts hyoscyamine → scopolamine.
>
> **(ii) Extraction methodology (2.5 marks):**
> 1. **Alkalisation:** Dried leaves are macerated with dilute NaOH or Ca(OH)₂ (pH 9–10) to liberate the free bases from their salts.
> 2. **Solvent extraction:** The alkaline slurry is extracted with a non-polar organic solvent (toluene, dichloromethane, or ethyl acetate) which dissolves the free-base alkaloids.
> 3. **Acid back-extraction:** The organic layer is re-extracted with dilute H₂SO₄ (pH 2–3), protonating the alkaloids into the aqueous phase as salts.
> 4. **Re-alkalisation and precipitation:** Aqueous phase is re-basified to pH 9–10; scopolamine precipitates as crude crystals.
> 5. **Purification:** Recrystallisation, then column chromatography or preparative HPLC to API grade (>99.5%).
>
> **(iii) Biotechnological alternatives (2.5 marks):**
> 1. **Hairy-root cultures** induced by *Agrobacterium rhizogenes* in *Hyoscyamus*, *Datura*, or *Atropa* — these are genetically stable, fast-growing, and free of microbial contamination. They can be scaled up in **bubble-column bioreactors**.
> 2. **Transgenic over-expression of H6H** under the CaMV 35S promoter in *Atropa belladonna* redirects the pathway toward scopolamine, raising the scopolamine : hyoscyamine ratio from ~1:50 to ~3:1.
> 3. **Elicitor treatment** (methyl jasmonate, salicylic acid) in cell-suspension cultures boosts secondary-metabolite production 3–10×.
> 4. **Synthetic biology** approaches in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* or *Nicotiana benthamiana* expressing the entire tropane pathway are emerging; key genes: ODC, PMT, DAO, tropinone synthase, TRI, CYP80F1, H6H.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the (S) stereochemistry** when discussing hyoscyamine. The plant produces *only* (S)-(–)-hyoscyamine. Writing "L-hyoscyamine" or omitting chirality costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing atropine with hyoscyamine.** They are NOT synonyms. **Atropine = racemate of hyoscyamine; natural hyoscyamine is the (S)-isomer.** Examiners love this trap.
> 3. **Skipping the epoxide bridge** when drawing scopolamine's structure. Always show the 6,7-epoxide — it is the most-asked distinguishing feature.
> 4. **Writing "ornithine → tropane" in one step** in the biosynthetic pathway. The pathway has **at least 7 enzymatic steps**; examiners expect at least PMT, DAO, and TRI to be named.
> 5. **Citing *Atropa belladonna* as the main scopolamine source.** It is the main *hyoscyamine* source. The main *scopolamine* source is ***Duboisia myoporoides***.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in numerical yield calculations (e.g., writing 83 instead of 83.33%). Always include % or g.
> 7. **Writing `|` inside markdown tables** (e.g., for absolute value), which breaks the KTU answer-template parser. Use `\vert` in LaTeX mode instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - [x] **Tropane alkaloids** are bicyclic N-containing secondary metabolites of the **Solanaceae** family.
> - [x] The **tropane nucleus** is **8-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane**.
> - [x] **Hyoscyamine** = tropine + (S)-tropic acid; formula C₁₇H₂₃NO₃, M = 289.37 g/mol.
> - [x] **Scopolamine** = scopine (tropine + 6,7-epoxide) + (S)-tropic acid; C₁₇H₂₁NO₄, M = 303.36 g/mol.
> - [x] **Atropine = DL-racemate** of hyoscyamine; produced by racemisation of the natural (S)-isomer.
> - [x] Natural hyoscyamine is **~2× more potent** than atropine because only (S) is biologically active.
> - [x] **Biosynthesis** starts with **ornithine → putrescine → N-methylputrescine (rate-limiting, PMT)** → N-methyl-Δ¹-pyrrolinium⁺ → + acetoacetyl-CoA → **tropinone** (tropinone synthase) → **tropine** (TRI) → + **tropoyl-CoA** (from phenylalanine via CYP80F1) → **hyoscyamine** (tropine acyltransferase).
> - [x] **Hyoscyamine → scopolamine** is catalysed by the bifunctional **H6H (hyoscyamine 6β-hydroxylase)** = hydroxylation at C-6 + epoxidation.
> - [x] **Mechanism**: competitive, reversible antagonism of **muscarinic ACh receptors (M₁–M₅)**.
> - [x] **Peripheral effects**: mydriasis, tachycardia, dry mouth, ↓ GI motility, urinary retention, anhidrosis, bronchodilation.
> - [x] **CNS effects** (mainly scopolamine): sedation, amnesia, anti-emesis — due to BBB penetration aided by the 6,7-epoxide.
> - [x] **Main plant sources**: *Atropa belladonna* (hyoscyamine), *Datura stramonium* & ***Duboisia myoporoides*** (scopolamine).
> - [x] **Industrial production**: alkali extraction → organic solvent partition → acid back-extraction → crystallisation; biotechnological alternative = *Agrobacterium rhizogenes* hairy-root cultures with H6H over-expression.
> - [x] **Applications**: motion-sickness patches, pre-anaesthetic, ophthalmology, ACLS bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning antidote, myopia control in children.
> - [x] **Toxicity**: "Red as a beet, dry as a bone, hot as a hare, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter" — classic belladonna poisoning.
> - [x] **Dose safety**: therapeutic scopolamine patch 1 mg/72 h; LD₅₀ atropine oral (rat) ≈ 400 mg/kg; treat overdose with **physostigmine** (crosses BBB, reverses CNS anticholinergic effects).
> - [x] **Analytical formula**: $\%\text{ recovery} = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure and Use of Homatropine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure and Use of Homatropine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Homatropine** is a **semi-synthetic tropane alkaloid** prepared by the **esterification of tropine** (the amino-alcohol moiety obtained by the alkaline hydrolysis of the natural alkaloid *atropine*) **with mandelic acid** (α-hydroxyphenylacetic acid). It belongs to the **anticholinergic / antimuscarinic** class of plant-derived bioactive molecules and is pharmacologically classified as a **mydriatic** and **cycloplegic** agent.

- **IUPAC Name:** (8-methyl-8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octan-3-yl) 2-hydroxy-2-phenylacetate
- **Molecular Formula:** $\mathbf{C_{16}H_{21}NO_3}$
- **Molar Mass:** $\mathbf{275.35 \ g \cdot mol^{-1}}$
- **Optical Activity:** Exists as the pharmacologically active **racemate**; the **L-enantiomer** is the active form.
- **Parent Alkaloid:** Derived structurally from **Atropine** (which is the ester of tropine with *tropic acid*).
- **Biological Source of Tropine:** *Atropa belladonna* (Deadly nightshade), *Datura stramonium*, *Hyoscyamus niger*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture for First-Time Learners**

Imagine a natural plant molecule, **atropine**, which works powerfully on the human eye but whose effects last **far too long** (sometimes up to 7–10 days) — inconvenient for diagnostic eye tests.

Chemists, therefore, performed a "**molecular surgery**": they snipped off the **tropic acid** "arm" of atropine and grafted a smaller, simpler **mandelic acid** "arm" in its place. The result — **Homatropine** — is a **hybrid molecule**: the natural bicyclic "head" of atropine joined to a synthetic "tail."

- The **"head"** (tropine) → comes from *nature* (plant phytochemistry).
- The **"tail"** (mandelic acid) → comes from a *synthetic organic acid*.
- The result behaves like atropine but **wears off in 1–2 days**, making it ideal for routine ophthalmic examination.

This concept of taking a **plant-derived scaffold** and chemically modifying it to fine-tune activity is a cornerstone of **phytochemistry-based drug design**.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Metrics

| Feature | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Duration of Mydriatic Action** | **6 – 24 hours** (vs. 5–7 days for atropine) |
| **Cycloplegic Recovery** | **1 – 2 days** (vs. 4–6 days for atropine) |
| **Clinical Use** | Diagnostic ocular examination |
| **Route of Administration** | Topical ophthalmic drops (0.5% – 2%) |
| **Phytochemical Class** | **Tropane Alkaloid (semi-synthetic)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — The Two Halves of Homatropine

Homatropine is conceptually composed of two functional halves joined by an **ester linkage**:

### A. The Tropine Half (Plant-Derived Amino-Alcohol)

- Tropine is a **tropane ring system**: an **8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane** skeleton.
- It contains a **tertiary nitrogen (N-CH₃)** bridge.
- It bears a **secondary hydroxyl group at C-3** — this –OH is the site of esterification.

### B. The Mandelic Acid Half (Synthetic Acid)

- Mandelic acid = **α-hydroxyphenylacetic acid**.
- It contains a **phenyl ring**, a **carboxylic acid group (–COOH)**, and a **hydroxyl group on the α-carbon**.

### C. The Linkage

- The –COOH of mandelic acid reacts with the –OH of tropine → **ester bond (–COO–)** is formed, releasing **H₂O**.

## 2.2 Reaction Schematic (Logical Steps)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Logic of Formation**

1. **Isolation of tropine** by alkaline hydrolysis of the natural alkaloid atropine (from *Belladonna*).
2. **Activation of mandelic acid** (synthetic, α-hydroxyphenylacetic acid) — usually as the acyl chloride or anhydride.
3. **Condensation (Esterification)** between the C-3 hydroxyl of tropine and the carboxyl of mandelic acid.
4. **Purification** of the resulting semi-synthetic alkaloid — homatropine — usually isolated as the **hydrobromide salt** for clinical stability.

## 2.3 Why Replace Tropic Acid with Mandelic Acid?

| Property | Atropine (Tropic Acid Tail) | Homatropine (Mandelic Acid Tail) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lipophilicity** | Higher | Slightly Lower |
| **Receptor Affinity (Muscarinic)** | Very High | High but shorter duration |
| **Hydrolysis Rate in Eye** | Slow | Faster |
| **Duration of Mydriasis** | 5–7 days | 6–24 hours |
| **Clinical Advantage** | Therapeutic uveitis | Diagnostic refraction |

The **phenyl group of mandelic acid** lacks the **β-hydroxy ethyl side-chain** of tropic acid, reducing binding persistence at the muscarinic receptor — hence the **shorter action**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol / Formula | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Molecular Formula** | $C_{16}H_{21}NO_3$ | Semi-synthetic tropane |
| **Molar Mass** | $M$ | $\mathbf{275.35 \ g \cdot mol^{-1}}$ |
| **Degree of Unsaturation (DBE)** | $\text{DBE} = C - \dfrac{H}{2} + \dfrac{N}{2} + 1$ | $\mathbf{7}$ (1 ring + 1 N-bridge ring + 1 phenyl + 1 C=O) |
| **Mydriatic Onset** | $t_{onset}$ | **30 – 60 minutes** |
| **Mydriatic Duration** | $t_{1/2, \ clinical}$ | **6 – 24 hours** |
| **Receptor Target** | Muscarinic AChR ($M_3$) | Antagonist |
| **Functional Group** | Ester + Tertiary amine | Anticholinergic signature |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering & Pharmaceutical Significance**

- **Ophthalmology** — homatropine hydrobromide eye drops are routinely used to dilate pupils before retinal examination and refraction testing.
- **Food & Phytochemical Analysis** — homatropine is a textbook example of how **plant scaffolds** (tropine) are **semi-synthetically tailored** for human therapeutic use, a key concept in **functional food & nutraceutical chemistry**.
- **Phytochemical Quality Control** — homatropine's structure is studied to understand the **structure–activity relationship (SAR)** of tropane alkaloids found in nightshade family plants (solanaceae) which sometimes contaminate food crops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Molecular Formula from Structural Components

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical construction of the formula $C_{16}H_{21}NO_3$**

### Step 1 — Tropine Fragment

Tropine has the molecular formula $\mathbf{C_8H_{15}NO}$. This is a bicyclic tertiary amine with a free –OH at C-3.

$$
\text{Tropine} \Rightarrow C_8H_{15}NO
$$

### Step 2 — Mandelic Acid Fragment

Mandelic acid has the molecular formula $\mathbf{C_8H_8O_3}$ (phenyl + α-OH + COOH).

$$
\text{Mandelic Acid} \Rightarrow C_8H_8O_3
$$

### Step 3 — Esterification (Loss of $H_2O$)

When a carboxylic acid (–COOH) condenses with an alcohol (–OH), one molecule of water is eliminated:

$$
\underbrace{C_8H_{15}NO}_{\text{Tropine}} \;\; + \;\; \underbrace{C_8H_8O_3}_{\text{Mandelic Acid}} \;\; \longrightarrow \;\; C_{16}H_{23}NO_4 \;\; + \;\; H_2O
$$

### Step 4 — Final Subtraction

Subtract the atoms of one $H_2O$ molecule from the intermediate adduct:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C: \;\; & 8 + 8 = 16 \\
H: \;\; & 15 + 8 - 2 = 21 \\
N: \;\; & 1 \\
O: \;\; & 1 + 3 - 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\therefore \ \text{Molecular Formula of Homatropine} \ = \ C_{16}H_{21}NO_3}
$$

---

## 3.2 Verification Using Degree of Unsaturation (DBE)

For a compound $C_cH_hN_nO_o$, ignoring oxygen:

$$
\text{DBE} = c - \frac{h}{2} + \frac{n}{2} + 1
$$

Substituting $c=16, h=21, n=1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DBE} &= 16 - \frac{21}{2} + \frac{1}{2} + 1 \\
&= 16 - 10.5 + 0.5 + 1 \\
&= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of DBE = 7:**
> - 1 unsaturation from the **ester carbonyl (C=O)**
> - 1 unsaturation from the **phenyl ring** (4 DBE: 3 C=C + 1 ring)
> - **Wait** — 1 phenyl ring alone accounts for 4 DBE. The tropane bicyclic framework accounts for **2 rings (2 DBE)**. The C=O accounts for **1 DBE**.
> - **Total = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7** ✓ (matches the formula calculation)

The DBE calculation is therefore a **mathematical fingerprint** confirming the structure contains one aromatic ring, one carbonyl, and a bicyclic amino-alcohol framework.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Structural Feature → Property Relationship

| Structural Feature | Position | Functional Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tropane bicyclic N** | N-8 (bridge) | Constrains tertiary amine geometry → optimum muscarinic binding |
| **N-methyl group** | N-8 | Protonatable → water-soluble HBr salt for ophthalmic drops |
| **Ester at C-3** | Tropine C-3 ↔ Mandelic C-1 | Hydrolysable → short in-vivo half-life |
| **Phenyl ring (mandelic)** | α-carbon of acid half | Hydrophobic anchor at receptor; absent in tropic acid's aliphatic chain |
| **α-Hydroxyl of mandelic** | On the acid α-carbon | Hydrogen-bond donor → stabilises receptor interaction |
| **Absence of β-hydroxy ethyl** | Mandelic acid (vs. tropic) | Reduces extra H-bonding → faster dissociation → **short duration** |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Mydriatic Mechanism (How It Works)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism in the Human Eye**

1. **Topical instillation** of homatropine hydrobromide drop onto the cornea.
2. Drug **penetrates the cornea** and diffuses into the **aqueous humour**.
3. It reaches the **iris sphincter muscle** (pupil constrictor) and the **ciliary muscle** (lens focusing).
4. **Competitive antagonism** at **muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (M3)** → blocks the parasympathetic signal.
5. The **sphincter pupillae relaxes** → pupil dilates (**mydriasis**).
6. The **ciliary muscle relaxes** → lens flattens (**cycloplegia**) → loss of accommodation.
7. Recovery: ester **hydrolysed by tissue esterases** → effect fades within 24 h.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Molecular Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Plant Source: Atropa belladonna"] --> B["Natural Alkaloid: Atropine C17H23NO3"]
    B --> C["Alkaline Hydrolysis Step"]
    C --> D["Tropine Fragment C8H15NO"]
    C --> E["Tropic Acid Fragment Discarded"]
    D --> F["Esterification with Mandelic Acid C8H8O3"]
    F --> G["Homatropine Semi Synthetic Alkaloid C16H21NO3"]
    G --> H["Salt Formation with HBr"]
    H --> I["Homatropine Hydrobromide Ophthalmic Drops"]
    I --> J["Clinical Use: Mydriasis and Cycloplegia"]
```

---

## 4.2 Structure-Function Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Head["Tropine Head Plant Derived"]
        H1["Tropane Bicyclic Ring"]
        H2["N Methyl Tertiary Amine"]
        H3["C3 Hydroxyl Ester Site"]
    end
    subgraph Link["Ester Linkage"]
        L1["COO Bridge from Esterification"]
    end
    subgraph Tail["Mandelic Acid Tail Synthetic"]
        T1["Phenyl Ring Aromatic"]
        T2["Alpha Hydroxyl H Bond Donor"]
        T3["Carboxyl Carbon Now Esterified"]
    end
    H1 --> L1
    H2 --> L1
    H3 --> L1
    L1 --> T1
    L1 --> T2
    L1 --> T3
    T1 --> Effect["M3 Muscarinic Antagonism"]
    T2 --> Effect
    Effect --> Result["Mydriasis plus Cycloplegia Short Duration 6 to 24 h"]
```

---

## 4.3 Receptors and Cellular Action Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DrugInstill["Topical Eye Drop Instillation"] --> CornealPen["Corneal Penetration"]
    CornealPen --> Aqueous["Diffusion into Aqueous Humour"]
    Aqueous --> M3Receptor["M3 Muscarinic Receptor on Iris Sphincter"]
    M3Receptor --> BlockAch["Blocks Acetylcholine Binding"]
    BlockAch --> SphincterRelax["Sphincter Pupillae Muscle Relaxation"]
    SphincterRelax --> Mydriasis["Pupil Dilation Mydriasis"]
    BlockAch --> CiliaryRelax["Ciliary Muscle Relaxation"]
    CiliaryRelax --> Cycloplegia["Loss of Accommodation Cycloplegia"]
    Mydriasis --> Recovery["Hydrolysis by Esterases 6 to 24 h"]
    Cycloplegia --> Recovery
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Atropine vs Homatropine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Atropine: Tropine + Tropic Acid"] --> A1["Action: 5 to 7 days"]
    A --> A2["Use: Therapeutic Uveitis"]
    H["Homatropine: Tropine + Mandelic Acid"] --> H1["Action: 6 to 24 hours"]
    H --> H2["Use: Diagnostic Refraction"]
    A -. "Semi Synthetic Modification" .-> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often write the structure of **atropine** by mistake when homatropine is asked. **Always verify** the acid part: it is **mandelic acid (phenyl + α-OH)**, NOT tropic acid (phenyl + α-OH + β-CH₂).
> - Confusing the **mydriatic duration** with atropine will cost **2 marks** in descriptive answers.
> - Do not forget to mention the **semi-synthetic nature** of the molecule — a question favourite in **Part A (2 marks)**.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Homatropine. Mention its natural phytochemical source and the synthetic modification involved.**

**Model Answer:**

Homatropine is a **semi-synthetic tropane alkaloid** with molecular formula $C_{16}H_{21}NO_3$, prepared by **esterifying tropine** (obtained by alkaline hydrolysis of the natural alkaloid **atropine** from *Atropa belladonna*) **with mandelic acid** (α-hydroxyphenylacetic acid).

- **Natural source of tropine:** *Atropa belladonna* (Solanaceae).
- **Synthetic step:** Condensation of tropine with **mandelic acid** to form an **ester bond**.
- It is used as a **mydriatic and cycloplegic** in ophthalmology.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare the duration of action of homatropine and atropine on the human eye.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Atropine | Homatropine |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mydriatic Duration** | 5 – 7 days | 6 – 24 hours |
| **Cycloplegia Recovery** | 4 – 6 days | 1 – 2 days |
| **Clinical Use** | Therapeutic | Diagnostic |

The shorter action of homatropine is due to the **mandelic acid tail** lacking the β-hydroxy ethyl side-chain of tropic acid, allowing **faster hydrolysis** by tissue esterases.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Draw the complete chemical structure of **Homatropine** and label the **tropine moiety**, the **mandelic acid moiety**, and the **ester linkage**.

**(b)** Explain the **stepwise preparation** of homatropine from *Atropa belladonna*, including the role of **alkaline hydrolysis** and **esterification**. Justify why homatropine is preferred over atropine for routine ophthalmic diagnosis.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure Description (7 marks):**

- Draw the **8-azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane** skeleton (tropane) with **N-methyl** at position 8 and a **hydroxyl group** at C-3.
- Connect the C-3 oxygen via an **ester bond (–C(=O)–O–)** to the **α-carbon** of mandelic acid.
- Show the **phenyl ring** attached to the α-carbon, with the **α-OH** group.
- Label the **three regions** clearly as required.

$$
\text{Tropine} \; \left[ \text{bicyclic amine with N-CH}_3 \text{ and C-3 OH} \right] \; \longrightarrow \text{–O–CO–CH(OH)–C}_6\text{H}_5 \; \leftarrow \text{Mandelic Acid Tail}
$$

**(b) Stepwise Preparation (7 marks):**

1. **Extraction** of crude alkaloids from dried *Atropa belladonna* leaves using alcoholic or aqueous acidic solvents.
2. **Isolation of atropine** by solvent partitioning and crystallisation.
3. **Alkaline hydrolysis** of atropine using **Ba(OH)₂ or NaOH** → yields **tropine** (C₈H₁₅NO) and **tropic acid** (discarded).
4. **Esterification**: tropine + **mandelic acid** (or mandelyl chloride) → homatropine + H₂O.
5. **Salt formation** with HBr to give **homatropine hydrobromide** (pharmaceutical form).

**Justification (preferred over atropine):**

- Shorter duration of action (6–24 h) → patient comfort.
- Faster cycloplegic recovery → rapid restoration of vision.
- Safer for diagnostic refraction; atropine reserved for therapeutic uveitis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Outline the **mechanism of mydriatic action** of homatropine at the **muscarinic receptor level**. Identify the specific receptor subtype involved.

**(b)** Describe the **pharmaceutical formulation** of homatropine as an eye drop. Mention concentration ranges and **storage precautions**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of Action (7 marks):**

- **Receptor target:** **M3 muscarinic acetylcholine receptor** on the **iris sphincter muscle** and **ciliary muscle**.
- **Mechanism:** **Competitive, reversible antagonism** at the orthosteric site of M3.
- Acetylcholine (ACh) normally binds M3 → activates Gq → IP₃/Ca²⁺ pathway → muscle contraction.
- Homatropine **blocks ACh binding** → no Gq activation → **sphincter/ciliary muscle relaxation** → **mydriasis + cycloplegia**.
- The **tertiary amine (protonated at physiological pH)** forms a salt bridge with **Asp residue** in the receptor's binding pocket.
- The **ester linkage** is hydrolysed by tissue **esterases**, terminating the action.

**(b) Pharmaceutical Formulation (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Salt used** | Homatropine hydrobromide |
| **Concentration** | **0.5% – 2% w/v** in sterile aqueous solution |
| **Route** | Topical ophthalmic (1–2 drops) |
| **Adjuncts** | Boric acid buffer, benzalkonium chloride (preservative) |
| **pH** | ~4.5 – 6.0 (stability range) |
| **Storage** | Below **25 °C**, protected from light; single-use vials preferred |
| **Onset / Duration** | Onset 30–60 min; recovery in 6–24 h |

- For deeper cycloplegia (in children), **2% atropine** may be preferred; homatropine 2% is an alternative for milder cases.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam**

- **Homatropine = Tropine + Mandelic Acid** (joined by an **ester bond**).
- **Molecular formula:** $C_{16}H_{21}NO_3$ ; **Molar mass:** $275.35 \ g \cdot mol^{-1}$.
- **Class:** **Semi-synthetic tropane alkaloid**; subclass: **anticholinergic / antimuscarinic**.
- **Source of tropine:** *Atropa belladonna* (alkaloid hydrolysis of natural atropine).
- **DBE = 7** → 1 phenyl + 1 C=O + 2 rings (tropane bicyclic).
- **Mydriatic duration:** **6 – 24 hours** (vs. 5–7 days for atropine).
- **Mechanism:** Competitive antagonist at **M3 muscarinic receptors** of iris sphincter and ciliary muscle.
- **Clinical use:** Routine **diagnostic ophthalmic examination** (refraction, fundoscopy).
- **Pharmaceutical form:** **Homatropine hydrobromide 0.5–2%** eye drops.
- **Why shorter action?** Mandelic acid lacks the β-hydroxy ethyl side-chain of tropic acid → faster esterase hydrolysis.
- **SAR principle:** Modifying the **acid half** of a tropane alkaloid tunes the **duration** of muscarinic blockade.
- **Safety point:** Always distinguish homatropine's **mandelic acid** from atropine's **tropic acid** in structures (the most common exam error).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vincristine and Vinblastine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT417 - PHYTOCHEMICALS IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vincristine and Vinblastine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vinca Alkaloids (Vincristine and Vinblastine):** A class of **bisindole (dimeric) indole alkaloids** isolated from the aerial parts of the ornamental medicinal plant *Catharanthus roseus* (L.) G. Don (formerly *Vinca rosea*), belonging to the family **Apocynaceae**. Chemically, they are composed of two monomeric indole alkaloid units — **catharanthine** (indole moiety) and **vindoline** (dihydroindole moiety) — joined by a C–C bond, exhibiting potent **antineoplastic (anticancer)** activity by binding to β-tubulin and arresting mitosis at the **metaphase** stage.

| Compound | IUPAC Nomenclature | Molecular Formula | Molecular Weight |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Vincristine (VCR)** | Methyl (1R,9R,10S,11R,12R,19R)-11-acetyloxy-12-ethyl-4-[(13S,15R)-17-ethyl-17-hydroxy-13-methoxycarbonyl-1,11-diazatetracyclo[13.3.1.04,12.05,10]nonadeca-4(12),5,7,9-tetraen-13-yl]-8-formyl-5-hydroxy-10-methoxy-5,16-dimethyl-2,6-dioxo-15-(2-oxoethyl)-9,11,18-triazatricyclo[11.5.2.01,11]icosa-3,13,16-trien-19-oate | **C₄₆H₅₆N₄O₁₀** | **824.96 g/mol** |
| **Vinblastine (VBL)** | Methyl (1R,9R,10S,11R,12R,19R)-11-acetyloxy-12-ethyl-4-[(13S,15R)-17-ethyl-17-hydroxy-13-methoxycarbonyl-1,11-diazatetracyclo[13.3.1.04,12.05,10]nonadeca-4(12),5,7,9-tetraen-13-yl]-8-formyl-10-hydroxy-5,16-dimethyl-2,6-dioxo-15-(2-oxoethyl)-9,11,18-triazatricyclo[11.5.2.01,11]icosa-3,13,16-trien-19-oate | **C₄₆H₅₈N₄O₉** | **810.97 g/mol** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The difference between Vincristine and Vinblastine is a single functional group — Vincristine carries a **formyl (–CHO) group** on the indole nitrogen of the catharanthine moiety, whereas Vinblastine carries a **methyl (–CH₃) group** in that position. This single-atom difference accounts for drastically different clinical pharmacology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **construction site** where a massive building (a new cell) is being built. Tiny steel rods (called **microtubules**) act like scaffolding that pulls the building materials into two new, identical buildings when the cell divides.

Now imagine a **traffic police officer** who slips into the construction site and **ties up all the steel rods** so they can no longer be assembled into scaffolding. The cell cannot split, and it eventually dies.

- **Vinblastine and Vincristine are those "traffic police officers."**
- They are plant defense molecules (made by *Catharanthus roseus* to deter herbivores) that humans cleverly harvest because they are exceptionally good at sabotaging **rapidly dividing cells** — which is exactly what **cancer cells** are.
- Vinblastine is the "softer" officer (used in slow-growing cancers), while Vincristine is the "stricter" officer (used in fast-growing leukemias and lymphomas).

### 1.3 Source Plant and Historical Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Botanical Source:** *Catharanthus roseus* (Madagascar Periwinkle), also called *Vinca rosea*, Sadabahar, or Cape Periwinkle.
> **Family:** Apocynaceae (Dogbane family)
> **Plant Part Used:** Leaves (aerial parts)
> **Native to:** Madagascar; now cultivated worldwide in tropical and subtropical regions.

- **Discovery Timeline:**
  - 1950s: Originally studied in Jamaica and the Philippines as an oral hypoglycemic agent for diabetes.
  - 1958: Noble, Beer, and Cutts (University of Western Ontario, Canada) discovered its **antileukemic activity**.
  - 1961: Vinblastine approved by **US-FDA** for clinical use.
  - 1963: Vincristine approved by **US-FDA** for clinical use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the plant produces these molecules:** They are secondary metabolites that act as a **chemical defense** against herbivores and pathogens. They are **not** involved in primary plant growth (unlike carbohydrates, proteins, lipids).

### 1.4 Classification of Vinca Alkaloids

| Type | Examples | Origin |
|------|----------|--------|
| **Naturally Occurring** | Vinblastine, Vincristine (trace), Vindesine | Direct isolation from *C. roseus* |
| **Semi-Synthetic Derivatives** | Vinorelbine, Vindesine, Anhydrovinblastine | Chemical modification of natural precursors |
| **Synthetic Analogues** | Vinflunine, Vincristine (commercial) | Chemical synthesis / partial synthesis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often write that Vincristine is "naturally abundant." In reality, **Vincristine occurs only in trace amounts** (≈ 0.0003% dry weight) in the plant. It is produced commercially by **semi-synthesis** from the more abundant vinblastine or from catharanthine + vindoline coupling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Biosynthesis of Vinca Alkaloids in Catharanthus roseus

The biosynthetic pathway is one of the most complex in plant secondary metabolism, involving multiple cell types and enzymatic steps.

#### Step-by-Step Biosynthetic Logic:

1. **Primary Metabolism Source:** The precursor **tryptophan** (an aromatic amino acid) undergoes decarboxylation.
2. **Strictosidine Formation:**
   - Tryptophan → Tryptamine (by tryptophan decarboxylase, TDC)
   - Tryptamine + Secologanin (from iridoid pathway) → **Strictosidine** (by strictosidine synthase, STR — the central branch-point enzyme)
3. **Vindoline Pathway:**
   - Strictosidine → Stemmadenine → Preakuammicine → Vindoline (multi-step)
4. **Catharanthine Pathway:**
   - Strictosidine → Cathenamine → Catharanthine (via reduction and rearrangement)
5. **Coupling Step:**
   - Catharanthine + Vindoline → Anhydrovinblastine (AVLB) — by class III peroxidase, **an** vinblastine biosynthesis steps.
6. **Final Oxidation:**
   - AVLB → Vinblastine (VBL) — by anhydrovinblastine hydroxylase
   - Vinblastine → Vincristine (VCR) — by a specific **N-methyltransferase / oxidase** system that converts the –N–CH₃ to –N–CHO

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Strictosidine is the **central intermediate** in the biosynthesis of all monoterpenoid indole alkaloids (MIAs). The question "Which is the central precursor in Vinca alkaloid biosynthesis?" has the standard KTU answer: **Strictosidine**.

#### 2.1.1 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Biosynthesis Summary

| Step | Substrate → Product | Key Enzyme | Cellular Location |
|------|---------------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Tryptophan → Tryptamine | TDC (Tryptophan Decarboxylase) | Cytoplasm |
| 2 | Geraniol → 10-Hydroxygeraniol → Secologanin | Multiple | Plastid / Cytoplasm |
| 3 | Tryptamine + Secologanin → Strictosidine | STR (Strictosidine Synthase) | Vacuole |
| 4 | Strictosidine → Stemmadenine | Strictosidine β-glucosidase, etc. | Cytoplasm |
| 5 | Stemmadenine → Vindoline / Catharanthine | Multiple OMTs, acetyltransferases | Cytoplasm, Idioblast cells |
| 6 | Catharanthine + Vindoline → Anhydrovinblastine | Peroxidase (CRPRX) | Vacuole |
| 7 | Anhydrovinblastine → Vinblastine | AVLB Hydroxylase | Cytoplasm |
| 8 | Vinblastine → Vincristine | N-Demethylation / N-Formylation Oxidase | Cytoplasm |

### 2.2 Chemistry of Vincristine and Vinblastine

#### 2.2.1 Structural Features

Both alkaloids are **heterodimeric bisindole alkaloids** built from:

- **Catharanthine unit (Indole half):** Contributes a 15-membered macrocyclic ring system with an indole chromophore. UV absorption at **λ_max ≈ 220, 254, 287, 295 nm**.
- **Vindoline unit (Dihydroindole half):** Contributes the second 15-membered ring with two methoxy groups, a hydroxyl group, an acetyl ester, and a methyl ester.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ring System:** Each half contains a **velbanamine-type** skeleton. The two halves are joined by a **C16'–C21' carbon–carbon bond**.

#### 2.2.2 Key Structural Differences Between VCR and VBL

| Feature | Vincristine (VCR) | Vinblastine (VBL) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Substituent on indole N of catharanthine | **–CHO (Formyl)** | **–CH₃ (Methyl)** |
| Molecular formula | C₄₆H₅₆N₄O₁₀ | C₄₆H₅₈N₄O₉ |
| Molecular weight | 824.96 g/mol | 810.97 g/mol |
| Number of oxygen atoms | 10 | 9 |
| Methylation state | N-formylated | N-methylated |
| Yield from plant | < 0.0003% dry wt | ~ 0.005% dry wt |

### 2.3 Mechanism of Action (Anticancer Activity)

#### 2.3.1 Cellular Target — Tubulin and Microtubules

> [!NOTE]
> **Microtubules** are dynamic polymers of α-tubulin and β-tubulin heterodimers. They are essential for:
> 1. **Mitotic spindle formation** during cell division (M-phase)
> 2. **Intracellular transport** of vesicles and organelles
> 3. **Cell shape and motility**
> 4. **Signal transduction**

#### 2.3.2 Binding Mechanism

- Both VCR and VBL bind specifically to the **β-tubulin subunit** of the α/β-tubulin heterodimer.
- The binding site is located at the **vinca binding domain** (also called the "Maytansine site" overlap region), distinct from the **taxane (paclitaxel) site**.
- **Stoichiometry:** 1 molecule of VCR/VBL per tubulin heterodimer.
- **Effect:** They **prevent tubulin polymerization** (they are *microtubule destabilizers* — the opposite of paclitaxel) and induce the formation of **tubulin heterodimer–drug aggregates** (paracrystals).

#### 2.3.3 Consequence — Mitotic Arrest and Apoptosis

When microtubules cannot form:
1. The **mitotic spindle fails to assemble** during prophase.
2. Chromosomes cannot align at the metaphase plate.
3. The cell cycle is **arrested at the G2/M transition** (or metaphase).
4. Sustained mitotic arrest triggers **apoptosis (programmed cell death)** via the intrinsic (mitochondrial) pathway.
5. This is **most toxic to rapidly dividing cells** — cancer cells, but also normal bone marrow, gut epithelium, and hair follicles (explaining side effects).

### 2.4 Clinical Pharmacology and Uses

| Property | Vincristine (VCR) | Vinblastine (VBL) |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Primary Use** | Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), Hodgkin's & non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, neuroblastoma, Wilms' tumor, rhabdomyosarcoma | Hodgkin's lymphoma, testicular cancer, breast cancer, Kaposi's sarcoma, choriocarcinoma |
| **Route** | IV only (oral inactive) | IV only |
| **Neurotoxicity** | **High** (peripheral neuropathy, dose-limiting) | **Low** |
| **Myelosuppression** | **Low** | **High** (dose-limiting) |
| **Half-life (t½)** | ~ 85 hours (long) | ~ 25 hours (shorter) |
| **Notable Regimens** | CHOP (C-H-O-P), MOPP (M-O-P-P), R-CHOP | ABVD (A-B-V-D), PVB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acronyms to remember:**
> - **CHOP** = Cyclophosphamide, **H**ydroxydaunorubicin, **O**ncovin (VCR), Prednisone
> - **MOPP** = **M**echlorethamine, **O**ncovin, **P**rocarbazine, **P**rednisone
> - **ABVD** = **A**driamycin, **B**leomycin, **V**in**b**lastine, **D**acarbazine
> - **PVB** = Cis-**P**latin, **V**in**b**lastine, **B**leomycin

### 2.5 Pharmacokinetics and Drug Interactions

- **Distribution:** Highly bound to plasma proteins (α1-acid glycoprotein, albumin).
- **Metabolism:** Hepatic metabolism via **CYP3A4** and **CYP3A5** of the cytochrome P450 system.
- **Excretion:** Predominantly biliary/fecal (> 80%); only ~ 5–15% renal.
- **Drug Interactions:**
  - **CYP3A4 inhibitors** (ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir, erythromycin) **↑ toxicity** of VCR/VBL.
  - **CYP3A4 inducers** (rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, St. John's Wort) **↓ efficacy** of VCR/VBL.
  - **P-glycoprotein (P-gp) substrates** — VCR and VBL are P-gp substrates, so they are subject to multidrug resistance (MDR) by tumor cells overexpressing P-gp.

### 2.6 Toxicity Profile and Adverse Effects

| Toxicity | Vincristine | Vinblastine |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Neuropathy (peripheral) | **++++** (Dose-limiting) | + |
| Myelosuppression (neutropenia) | + | **++++** (Dose-limiting) |
| Alopecia | ++ | ++ |
| Nausea / Vomiting | + | ++ |
| SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH) | + | — |
| Vesicant (tissue damage on extravasation) | Yes | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vincristine and Vinblastine are potent vesicants.** If extravasation occurs (leakage out of the vein), they cause severe tissue necrosis. A **warmed compress, hyaluronidase, or DMSO** may be used as antidote.

### 2.7 Engineering and Industrial Significance

In food and pharmaceutical engineering:

1. **Plant Cell Culture Technology (PCT):** Because the natural yield is so low, *C. roseus* is one of the **model systems** for **plant cell fermentation** to produce alkaloids industrially. Bioreactor designs (stirred-tank, airlift, wave-type) have been developed for large-scale culture.
2. **Elicitation:** Fungal elicitors (e.g., *Botrytis*, *Rhodotorula*) and methyl jasmonate are used to **upregulate alkaloid biosynthesis genes** (TDC, STR, DAT) in culture.
3. **Hairy Root Culture:** *Agrobacterium rhizogenes*-transformed hairy roots are used for stable, high-yield production of VBL/VCR.
4. **Metabolic Engineering:** Overexpression of rate-limiting enzymes (e.g., **STR1**, **TDC**, **DAT** — deacetylvindoline acetyltransferase) and suppression of competing branches.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Bioprocess Engineering:** Designing fed-batch bioreactors for *C. roseus* cell cultures.
- **Downstream Processing:** Resin-based chromatography (cation exchange, reversed-phase) for alkaloid purification.
- **Analytical Chemistry:** HPLC-UV, HPLC-MS, LC-MS/MS methods for quantification in plant extracts and pharmaceutical formulations.
- **Food Safety:** Although the alkaloids themselves are toxic (drugs), the leaves have been used **inappropriately** as a herbal remedy for diabetes, leading to **poisoning cases** — making them relevant to food toxicology.
- **Pharmacognosy & Quality Control:** Standardization of herbal drugs demands quantification of VBL and VCR markers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Molar Ratio and Stoichiometry in Tubulin Binding

The drug–tubulin interaction can be quantified. Below is the **step-by-step analytical derivation** of the apparent binding constant and the minimum concentration required for mitotic arrest.

#### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> **Given:** Tubulin heterodimer concentration in a typical cell ≈ 20 μM. The dissociation constant (Kd) for Vinblastine-tubulin binding is approximately **K_d ≈ 0.3 μM** (varies by literature; commonly cited range: 0.1 – 1.0 μM).
> **Find:** The minimum drug concentration needed to occupy **≥ 50%** of tubulin sites (the standard threshold for biological effect).

#### 3.1.2 Derivation

Let:
- $[\text{D}]$ = Free drug concentration
- $[\text{T}]$ = Free tubulin concentration
- $[\text{DT}]$ = Drug–tubulin complex concentration
- $[\text{T}]_{\text{total}}$ = Total tubulin = 20 μM
- $[\text{D}]_{\text{total}}$ = Total drug
- $K_d = \dfrac{[\text{D}][\text{T}]}{[\text{DT}]}$

**Step 1:** Mass balance on tubulin:

$$[\text{T}]_{\text{total}} = [\text{T}] + [\text{DT}]$$

**Step 2:** Mass balance on drug:

$$[\text{D}]_{\text{total}} = [\text{D}] + [\text{DT}]$$

**Step 3:** Fraction of tubulin bound (θ) is:

$$\theta = \dfrac{[\text{DT}]}{[\text{T}]_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging the Kd expression:

$$K_d = \dfrac{[\text{D}][\text{T}]}{[\text{DT}]} = \dfrac{[\text{D}]\big( [\text{T}]_{\text{total}} - [\text{DT}] \big)}{[\text{DT}]}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $[\text{DT}]$ as a function of free drug:

$$[\text{DT}] = \dfrac{[\text{T}]_{\text{total}} \cdot [\text{D}]}{K_d + [\text{D}]}$$

**Step 6:** When $[\text{D}] = K_d$, we have $\theta = 0.5$. Substituting:

$$[\text{DT}] = \dfrac{20 \cdot 0.3}{0.3 + 0.3} = \dfrac{6.0}{0.6} = 10 \, \mu\text{M}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, $[\text{D}]_{\text{total}} \approx K_d + [\text{DT}] = 0.3 + 10 \approx 10.3 \, \mu\text{M}$ to achieve 50% occupancy.

**Step 8:** Therapeutic plasma concentrations of VBL are typically 0.01 – 1 μM, but intracellular concentrations in cancer cells can reach **5 – 50 μM** due to active uptake and drug trapping, sufficient to cause mitotic arrest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The Kd-based analysis is used in **pharmacokinetic modeling (PK/PD)** to design dosing schedules that maintain effective intracellular drug concentrations.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix of the Two Compounds

| Parameter | Vincristine (VCR) | Vinblastine (VBL) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
